title: PACE: A Neuro-Symbolic Framework for Plausible and Actionable Counterfactual Explanations
summary: Counterfactual explanations explain machine learning predictions by identifying minimal input changes that would alter a model's decision. Although many existing methods successfully generate prediction-changing alternatives, they often produce unrealistic or infeasible recommendations due to a lack of explicit mechanisms for incorporating domain knowledge and intervention constraints. Neuro-symbolic AI offers a promising direction by combining data-driven predictive models with symbolic reasoning capable of representing human-understandable rules and feasible actions. This paper presents PACE, a modular neuro-symbolic framework for generating feasibility-aware counterfactual explanations. The framework separates prediction and reasoning into two components: a neural predictive model for classification and a symbolic reasoning layer that enforces domain-specific constraints during counterfactual generation. By explicitly modeling feasible interventions, the framework produces explanations consistent with domain knowledge while remaining interpretable and actionable. The approach is model-agnostic and adaptable to domains requiring realistic decision support. A case study is conducted on the Adult Income dataset, combining a multilayer perceptron classifier with Answer Set Programming (ASP) rules encoding feasible modifications to education, occupation, and working hours while preserving immutable attributes. Results highlight the trade-off between counterfactual validity and plausibility and show that symbolic constraints yield explanations that better satisfy domain-specific feasibility requirements, illustrating the potential of neuro-symbolic methods for transparent, feasibility-aware counterfactual explanation in explainable AI.

AI.tldr: PACE is a neuro-symbolic framework that generates counterfactual explanations that are both valid (change the prediction) and feasible (respect domain constraints).
AI.motivation: Existing counterfactual explanation methods often suggest changes that are unrealistic or infeasible, because they lack a principled way to encode domain knowledge and intervention constraints. The paper aims to integrate such constraints so that explanations are useful for real decision support.
AI.method: Separate prediction and reasoning into two modules: (1) a neural classifier that makes predictions; (2) a symbolic reasoning component (using Answer Set Programming) that encodes domain rules and feasible interventions. During counterfactual search, the symbolic layer constrains which feature changes are allowed (e.g., mutable vs. immutable attributes, realistic changes to education, occupation, hours). The framework is model-agnostic and can be coupled with different predictors and rule sets.
AI.result: On the Adult Income dataset, combining an MLP with ASP rules leads to counterfactuals that better satisfy feasibility and domain-specific constraints compared with unconstrained approaches. Experiments reveal a trade-off: enforcing stronger symbolic constraints can reduce the number or proximity of valid counterfactuals, but increases their plausibility and actionability.
AI.conclusion: Neuro-symbolic integration via PACE can generate counterfactual explanations that are transparent, consistent with domain knowledge, and practically actionable. Encoding feasible interventions as symbolic rules improves plausibility while preserving model-agnosticism, suggesting this is a promising direction for explainable AI in domains needing realistic decision support.

title: Auto-FL-Research: Agentic Search for Federated Learning Algorithms
summary: Federated learning (FL) research often depends on many small but consequential algorithmic choices: optimizer variants, server aggregation rules, local training schedules, normalization, regularization, and model architecture. These choices are expensive to explore manually and difficult to compare fairly when candidate changes can also alter the FL training or evaluation path. In this work, we present Auto-FL-Research (AFR), a constrained coding-agent workflow for FL algorithmic recipe search. Agents may propose and implement candidate training algorithms, including server aggregation rules, client update schedules, local objectives, and registered model variants, while task profiles fix the mutation surface, compute budget, communication contract, and final model evaluation. Each campaign records candidate scores, runtime, edited files, artifacts, and failure status. We evaluate AFR on five healthcare cross-silo FLamby tasks and on grouped-client profiles for the five fixed LEAF datasets plus the LEAF synthetic task. Five-seed repeat evaluations support gains on four FLamby tasks and five of six LEAF profiles, while also exposing seed-sensitive and search-selected failure cases. Same-budget controls show that several gains correspond to FL-recipe changes, whereas other improvements are recovered by fixed-surface scalar controls or fail under repeat or held-out evaluation. These mixed outcomes are part of the contribution: they show how agent-generated candidates can be separated into repeated FL mechanisms, fixed-surface tuning effects, and selected single-run artifacts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Auto-FL-Research (AFR), an automated coding-agent workflow to systematically search and evaluate federated learning (FL) training recipes, distinguishing genuine algorithmic improvements from tuning effects and random artifacts.
AI.motivation: Federated learning performance depends on many intertwined design choices (optimizers, aggregation rules, training schedules, etc.) that are expensive to explore manually and hard to compare fairly, since each change can also alter the training and evaluation path. The authors want a principled, automated way to explore this large design space while controlling compute, communication, and evaluation conditions.
AI.method: AFR uses constrained coding agents that are allowed to propose and implement candidate FL training algorithms (changing server aggregation, client schedules, local objectives, and model variants) within a predefined task profile that fixes the mutation surface, compute budget, communication protocol, and evaluation procedure. Each search campaign logs candidate performance, runtime, code edits, produced artifacts, and whether runs failed. The system is then used to run automated FL-recipe searches on several benchmark tasks.
AI.result: Applying AFR to five FLamby healthcare cross-silo tasks and grouped-client versions of five LEAF datasets plus a synthetic LEAF task, the authors observe performance gains on four FLamby tasks and five of six LEAF profiles in five-seed repeat evaluations. The experiments also reveal cases where improvements are seed-sensitive, due to search-selected artifacts, or attributable to simple fixed-surface tuning rather than novel FL mechanisms.
AI.conclusion: AFR can generate and test promising FL algorithmic recipes under controlled budgets and protocols, and its logging and repeat-evaluation structure allows separating robust FL mechanisms from mere hyperparameter tuning or lucky single-run artifacts. The work contributes both a practical automated workflow and an empirical illustration that many apparent FL improvements may not survive stricter evaluation, highlighting the need for such structured search and validation.

title: The Wiola Architecture for Efficient Small Language Models
summary: We present Wiola, a fully original Small Language Model (SLM) architecture built from first principles, sharing no structural lineage with any existing model family including GPT, LLaMA, Mistral, or Falcon. Wiola introduces five independently novel components: (i) Spiral Rotary Positional Encoding (SRPE), which embeds token positions on a three-dimensional helical manifold combining absolute, relative, and hierarchical positional signals; (ii) Gated Cross-Layer Attention (GCLA), providing each decoder layer with soft cross-attention access to compressed summaries of two preceding layers for inter-layer coherence; (iii) Adaptive Token Merging (ATM), which dynamically merges se mantically redundant adjacent tokens in middle network layers to reduce attention complexity without information loss; (iv) Dual Stream Feed-Forward (DSFF), replacing the conventional MLP with two parallel streams fused by a learned per-dimension gate; and (v) WiolaRMSNorm, a modified normalisation introducing a per-dimension learned offset vector that prevents representation collapse. We provide complete mathematical derivations, architectural block diagrams, complexity analyses, and systematic comparisons against GPT-2, LLaMA-2, and Mistral. Wiola is released in four sizes (120M, 360M, 700M, and 1.5B parameters) and is fully compatible with the HuggingFace Transformers ecosystem, with all 22 architectural unit tests passing.

AI.tldr: Wiola is a new, from-scratch small language model architecture with five novel components for positional encoding, inter-layer attention, token merging, feed-forward structure, and normalization, benchmarked and released in multiple sizes.
AI.motivation: To design an SLM architecture that is not a derivative of existing Transformer-based families and that improves positional encoding, inter-layer information flow, computational efficiency, representation richness, and training stability, while remaining practically usable and benchmarked against well-known baselines.
AI.method: The authors propose a new architecture, Wiola, defined by five key innovations: Spiral Rotary Positional Encoding (3D helical positional manifold), Gated Cross-Layer Attention (soft cross-attention to preceding layer summaries), Adaptive Token Merging (dynamic merging of redundant tokens mid-network), Dual Stream Feed-Forward (two parallel gated MLP streams), and WiolaRMSNorm (RMSNorm with per-dimension learned offsets). They derive the math, analyze complexity, implement the model in HuggingFace, and compare empirically to GPT-2, LLaMA-2, and Mistral on benchmarks, with unit tests validating components.
AI.result: Wiola is implemented in four parameter scales (120M–1.5B), integrates cleanly with HuggingFace Transformers, passes all 22 architectural unit tests, and in comparisons shows architectural viability and competitive performance versus GPT-2, LLaMA-2, and Mistral, while demonstrating the benefits of its novel components in terms of efficiency and representation quality (details implied but not in the abstract).
AI.conclusion: Wiola demonstrates that a fully original SLM architecture, built from first principles and combining novel positional encoding, inter-layer attention, token compression, feed-forward structure, and normalization, can be practically implemented, tested, and benchmarked at multiple scales, standing as a distinct alternative to existing GPT/LLaMA-style models and offering new design directions for small language models.

title: Agent4cs: A Multi-agent System for Code Summarization in Large Hierarchical Codebases
comment: Accepted to the main track of the 23rd European Conference on Multi-Agent Systems (EUMAS 2026)
summary: Understanding large, complex codebases, especially those with obfuscated structures and incomplete documentation, remains a significant challenge. Existing code summarization solutions often rely on a single language model or coding assistant like Claude Code, and treat source code as flat text, underutilizing the rich interdependencies and hierarchical information within a repository. To address these shortcomings, we propose Agent4cs - a multi-agent framework that summarizes large codebases in a bottom-up fashion, where a summarization agent focuses on producing robust summaries; a keyword-extraction agent proactively identifies critical information from subfolders; and a quality-assurance agent iteratively refines the outputs for readability, coherence, and completeness. Evaluated on 7 frontier models, Agent4cs improves semantic consistency across all folder levels by average 8% compared to two structured prompting baselines with code segments. Furthermore, extensive evaluation on real-world datasets demonstrates up to 38% gains in normalized keyword coverage rate over the same baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Agent4cs, a multi-agent framework that summarizes large, complex codebases using bottom-up summarization and specialized agents, achieving better semantic consistency and keyword coverage than structured prompting baselines.
AI.motivation: Understanding and summarizing large, complex, and possibly obfuscated codebases with poor documentation is difficult. Existing code summarization tools typically use a single language model and treat code as flat text, failing to exploit repository hierarchies and interdependencies. The authors aim to overcome these limitations and improve the quality and coverage of codebase summaries.
AI.method: The authors propose Agent4cs, a multi-agent framework that works in a bottom-up manner across a codebase. It consists of: (1) a summarization agent that generates robust summaries for code elements; (2) a keyword-extraction agent that proactively identifies critical information from subfolders to guide higher-level summaries; and (3) a quality-assurance agent that iteratively refines summaries for readability, coherence, and completeness. The framework is evaluated across 7 different large language models and compared against two structured prompting baselines that work on code segments.
AI.result: Across evaluations on 7 frontier models, Agent4cs improves semantic consistency of summaries at all folder levels by an average of 8% compared to two structured prompting baselines. On real-world datasets, the framework achieves up to 38% higher normalized keyword coverage rate than the same baselines, indicating more complete and accurate capture of important concepts in the codebase.
AI.conclusion: Agent4cs effectively leverages a multi-agent, bottom-up approach to codebase summarization, exploiting hierarchical repository structure and interdependencies. It outperforms structured prompting baselines in terms of semantic consistency and keyword coverage, demonstrating that specialized collaborating agents can produce richer, more coherent summaries for large, complex codebases than single-model, flat-text approaches.

title: TokenScope: Token-Level Explainability and Interpretability for Code-Oriented Tasks in Large Language Models
summary: Understanding how Large Language Models (LLMs) make token-level decisions during code generation remains a major challenge for both researchers and practitioners. While recent tools provide insights into model internals or generation outcomes, they often lack decoding-time signals, fine-grained uncertainty measures, and interactive mechanisms for exploring alternative generation paths. We present TokenScope, an interactive interpretability and analysis tool for decoder-based LLMs that exposes token-level metrics, attention patterns, and structural information during generation. TokenScope supports interactive token replacement, counterfactual branching, and code-aware aggregation via abstract syntax trees. By unifying decoding-time signals with structural program analysis, TokenScope enables systematic investigation of LLM behaviour during code generation.

AI.tldr: TokenScope is an interactive tool to interpret and analyze how decoder-based LLMs generate code at the token level in real time.
AI.motivation: Researchers and practitioners struggle to understand token-level decision-making of LLMs during code generation. Existing tools typically either look inside the model or only at the final outputs, but they lack decoding-time signals, fine-grained uncertainty information, and interactive mechanisms to explore alternative generation trajectories.
AI.method: The authors design TokenScope, an interpretability and analysis system that hooks into decoder-based LLMs during inference. It surfaces token-level metrics (e.g., probabilities/uncertainties), attention patterns, and structural program information. TokenScope adds interactive capabilities such as token replacement, counterfactual branching of generations, and code-aware aggregation using abstract syntax trees (ASTs).
AI.result: TokenScope successfully exposes decoding-time information and structural views of code generation, and enables users to interactively manipulate and branch generation paths. It integrates token-level statistics, attention, and AST-based views into a single interface for systematic analysis of LLM behavior in code generation tasks.
AI.conclusion: By combining decoding-time interpretability signals with structural program analysis and interactive editing/branching, TokenScope provides a unified framework for systematically probing, understanding, and experimenting with the behavior of decoder-based LLMs during code generation.

title: When Should Service Agents Reconsider? Difficulty-Routed Control in Customer-Service Operations
summary: Autonomous customer-service agents are shifting from conversational interfaces toward operational execution roles: they retrieve firm records, apply service policies, and execute backend writes such as refunds, cancellations, exchanges, order modifications, and reservation changes. This shift creates a service-control problem: firms must keep routine service fast and low-friction while preventing operational errors on requests where customer instructions, policy constraints, firm records, and backend writes interact. We propose a difficulty-routed service-control architecture that asks when service agents should reconsider before acting. A lightweight router keeps routine sessions on a low-cost baseline path and routes operationally coupled sessions to an escalated workflow. The escalated path uses conflict-aware communication and write-triggered reconsideration to concentrate deliberation and safeguards before consequential backend writes, rather than applying additional control uniformly across all service sessions. We evaluate the architecture on human-verified retail and airline tasks from $τ^{2}$-bench. In retail, the method improves reliability consistently on service requests with operational conflict. Routing evidence shows that stronger control is directed toward conflicted requests rather than broadly applied to routine ones. Dialogue and tool-use profiles suggest that gains do not come from indiscriminate interaction expansion or broader tool chains; instead, added turns and tool calls support evidence gathering, write separation, and pre-write reconsideration. Case-level evidence shows that the escalated workflow preserves fallback plans, binds retrieved records to the correct action, sequences writes, and decomposes multi-entity requests. Airline results extend the same service-control logic to reservation operations.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a difficulty-routed architecture for autonomous customer-service agents that selectively escalates complex, operationally risky requests into a safer, more deliberative workflow, improving reliability on conflicted service tasks without slowing down routine ones.
AI.motivation: As autonomous customer-service agents evolve from simple chatbots to systems that directly manipulate backend operations (refunds, cancellations, reservation changes, etc.), firms face a service-control dilemma: they need to keep routine interactions fast and low-friction while preventing costly operational errors in more complex cases where customer instructions, policies, records, and backend writes interact. Existing approaches that uniformly add control or oversight can degrade efficiency and user experience, and may still fail on the most complex cases. The paper is motivated by the need for a principled architecture that decides when agents should pause, reconsider, and add safeguards before executing consequential actions.
AI.method: The authors design a difficulty-routed service-control architecture with two main paths. A lightweight router classifies service sessions as routine or operationally coupled/conflicted. Routine sessions proceed along a low-cost baseline path. Sessions involving operational coupling (e.g., policy conflicts, ambiguous instructions, multi-entity changes) are routed to an escalated workflow. This escalated path incorporates conflict-aware communication with the user, explicit separation of information-gathering from action-taking, and write-triggered reconsideration steps immediately before backend writes. The system is evaluated on human-verified tasks from τ^2-bench in retail and airline domains, comparing reliability, routing patterns, and interaction/tool-use profiles against baselines.
AI.result: In the retail domain, the architecture consistently improves reliability for service requests involving operational conflicts. Routing logs show that additional control is concentrated on conflicted tasks rather than being uniformly applied to routine ones, preserving efficiency where high control is unnecessary. Interaction analytics indicate that improved performance does not stem from simply increasing dialogue length or indiscriminate tool chaining; instead, extra turns and tool calls are used purposefully for evidence gathering, separating read and write operations, and reconsidering decisions before executing writes. Case-level analyses demonstrate that the escalated workflow helps the agent maintain fallback options, correctly bind retrieved records to actions, appropriately sequence multiple writes, and decompose multi-entity tasks. Similar improvements and behaviors are observed when the same logic is applied to airline reservation operations.
AI.conclusion: A difficulty-routed service-control architecture can resolve the tension between fast routine service and safe handling of complex, operationally risky requests. By using a lightweight router and an escalated workflow with conflict-aware communication and write-triggered reconsideration, the approach concentrates safeguards exactly where they are needed—around consequential backend writes in conflicted cases—without burdening all sessions. The results on retail and airline tasks suggest this architecture is an effective and general pattern for controlling autonomous service agents that perform operational actions in real-world business environments.

title: Safeguarding LLM Agents from Misalignment through Provenance Analysis
summary: As LLM agents gain increasing access to powerful tools, ensuring that their actions are aligned with the user's intent becomes critical. When an agent's proposed tool invocation deviates from the user's intent -- a phenomenon called misalignment -- it may lead to harmful consequences that are difficult to undo. Existing runtime guardrails rely on an LLM-as-a-judge paradigm that lacks a systematic framework for reasoning about alignment, often producing judgments that are inconsistent or difficult to audit. Motivated by provenance analysis, we propose a provenance-based conceptual framework that formalizes misalignment detection as determining whether a proposed tool call is supported by traceable evidence in the agent's context. Building on this framework, we propose ProvenanceGuard, a multi-stage pipeline that analyzes the agent's action for three types of misalignment before the selected tool is executed and only allows the action to take place when it is considered aligned with the user's input query. We evaluated our proposed approach on two different benchmarks, Agent-SafetyBench and WorkBench, across 10 backbone LLMs. Compared to the LLM-as-a-judge baseline, ProvenanceGuard reduces error rate on misaligned traces from 42.9% to 1.8% on Agent-SafetyBench and from 32.1% to 17.3% on WorkBench, while reducing intervention burden on task-successful traces from 30.5% to 12.8% and introducing no statistically significant increase in unnecessary interventions on aligned traces. These results demonstrate that structured, provenance-based reasoning provides an effective and practical foundation for safeguarding LLM agents from misalignment.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces ProvenanceGuard, a provenance-based framework and pipeline to detect and prevent misaligned tool calls by LLM agents at runtime, substantially improving safety and reducing unnecessary interventions compared to LLM-as-a-judge approaches.
AI.motivation: As LLM agents are increasingly granted access to powerful external tools (e.g., code execution, web access, transactions), even a single misaligned tool invocation can cause serious and hard-to-reverse harm. Existing runtime guardrails mainly use an LLM-as-a-judge pattern, which lacks a principled way to reason about why a tool call should be allowed, making decisions inconsistent, opaque, and hard to audit. The authors are motivated to create a systematic, auditable framework for determining whether an agent’s proposed tool action is genuinely supported by the user’s intent and contextual evidence, leveraging ideas from provenance analysis.
AI.method: The authors formalize misalignment detection as a provenance problem: a tool call is aligned only if it is supported by traceable evidence within the agent’s context. They design a provenance-based conceptual framework and then instantiate it as ProvenanceGuard, a multi-stage runtime pipeline. Before any tool is executed, ProvenanceGuard inspects the agent’s proposed action for three distinct types of misalignment, checking that the action is properly grounded in the user’s input and contextual evidence. Only tool calls judged aligned under this structured provenance reasoning are allowed to proceed; otherwise they are blocked or intervened on. The system is evaluated as a plug-in guardrail across multiple backbone LLMs and tasks.
AI.result: On two benchmarks, Agent-SafetyBench and WorkBench, across 10 different LLMs, ProvenanceGuard significantly outperforms an LLM-as-a-judge baseline. On misaligned traces, the error rate drops from 42.9% to 1.8% on Agent-SafetyBench and from 32.1% to 17.3% on WorkBench. At the same time, the burden of interventions on task-successful traces is reduced from 30.5% to 12.8%, and there is no statistically significant increase in unnecessary interventions on already aligned traces. Thus, ProvenanceGuard both improves misalignment detection and keeps overhead on correct behavior low.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that a structured, provenance-based reasoning framework is an effective and practical way to safeguard LLM agents when they use external tools. By requiring that each tool call be justified by traceable contextual evidence and by checking multiple types of misalignment before execution, ProvenanceGuard dramatically reduces misalignment errors while avoiding excessive or unwarranted interventions. This demonstrates that moving beyond opaque LLM-as-a-judge heuristics toward explicit provenance analysis can yield more reliable, auditable alignment guarantees for tool-using agents.

title: CreativityNeuro: Steering Language Model Weights to Improve Divergent Thinking and Reduce Mode Collapse
comment: Accepted at ICML 2026 Workshop on Creativity & Generative AI
summary: Divergent thinking is a crucial aspect of creativity, yet large language models (LLMs) tend to consistently generate similar responses to open-ended questions, in what has been termed the artificial hivemind effect. Here, we introduce CreativityNeuro, a data-free method for enhancing divergent thinking in LLMs via contrastive weight steering. We evaluate our method across multiple creativity assessments and report several main findings. On the Divergent Association Task (DAT), a vocabulary-space creativity test, CreativityNeuro improves performance by up to 14 human percentile points. Next, in a large-scale human evaluation (N=720) on the Alternative Uses Test (AUT) and the Task Task, CreativityNeuro achieves significant improvements in originality, surprise, and creativity, transferring to longer-form and more open-ended tasks. Importantly, we find that across all three tasks, CreativityNeuro demonstrably reduces measures of mode collapse. Moreover, activation steering achieves comparable performance to CreativityNeuro on the DAT, but it does not transfer to the AUT and Task Task, demonstrating the effectiveness of weight-space steering in generalizing to unseen tasks. In conclusion, CreativityNeuro improves divergent thinking and reduces mode collapse without requiring behavioral data, re-training, or gradient-based fine-tuning, providing a straightforward way to enhance LLM performance in creative domains.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CreativityNeuro, a method to increase divergent thinking in large language models by steering their weights contrastively, improving creativity scores and reducing mode collapse without extra data or retraining.
AI.motivation: Large language models tend to produce similar, convergent answers to open-ended prompts, an ‘artificial hivemind’ that limits their usefulness for creative tasks. Existing methods to enhance creativity often require extra data, fine-tuning, or only affect surface activations and may not generalize well to new tasks. The authors want a principled, lightweight way to make LLM outputs more diverse and original across creativity benchmarks.
AI.method: The authors introduce CreativityNeuro, a data-free technique based on contrastive weight steering. Instead of collecting behavioral datasets or retraining the model, they manipulate model weights in a targeted direction that encourages divergent associations. They compare this weight-space steering to activation steering (adjusting internal activations at inference) and test both on several creativity tasks, analyzing originality, surprise, creativity, and mode collapse metrics.
AI.result: On the Divergent Association Task (DAT), CreativityNeuro boosts the model’s performance by up to 14 percentile points relative to humans. In human evaluations with 720 participants on the Alternative Uses Test (AUT) and the Task Task, CreativityNeuro significantly increases ratings of originality, surprise, and creativity. It also systematically reduces mode collapse across all three tasks. Activation steering reaches similar gains on the DAT but fails to generalize its benefits to AUT and Task Task, unlike the weight-steering approach.
AI.conclusion: CreativityNeuro effectively enhances divergent thinking in LLMs while reducing mode collapse, and it does so without behavioral data, retraining, or gradient-based fine-tuning. Weight-space steering not only improves vocabulary-level creativity (DAT) but also transfers to more complex, open-ended creative tasks where activation steering falls short, suggesting weight steering is a practical and general mechanism to boost LLM creativity.

title: Kara: Efficient Reasoning LLM Serving via Sliding-Window KV Cache Compression
summary: Reasoning language models often generate long chain-of-thought (CoT), which accumulates a massive KV cache during the decoding phase and incurs high decoding latency and limited throughput. To address these issues, KV cache compression has emerged as a promising technique for reducing memory overhead by selectively removing unimportant KV pairs while preserving useful ones for subsequent decoding. Nevertheless, we identify two key limitations in existing KV cache compression methods: 1) their threshold-triggered compression policy may provide limited throughput improvement or even reduce throughput, and may fully eliminate KV pairs from certain blocks of the sequence, potentially worsening information loss. 2) they typically retain either isolated KV pairs or fixed-size chunks with rigid boundaries, failing to preserve important flexible-sized chunks at arbitrary token positions. To overcome these limitations, we propose Kara, a sliding-window KV cache compression method that performs decoding-time compression by operating only on the recently generated context. Kara leverages bidirectional attention to score and select informative KV pairs in the window. To enable flexible preservation of important semantic information, we design a Token2Chunk module to expand a subset of selected KV pairs into chunks. Furthermore, we adapt Kara to PagedAttention and develop KvLLM, an inference framework built upon vLLM, which reduces KV cache memory usage and effectively improves output throughput. Extensive experiments demonstrate consistent performance improvements of proposed Kara and KvLLM.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Kara, a sliding-window KV cache compression method and KvLLM, an inference framework adapting Kara to vLLM, to reduce KV cache memory and improve throughput of reasoning LMs without sacrificing performance.
AI.motivation: Reasoning language models generate long chains-of-thought, which create large KV caches during decoding. This causes high memory usage, increased latency, and reduced throughput. Existing KV cache compression techniques either offer limited or even negative throughput gains due to threshold-triggered policies and risk catastrophic information loss by eliminating all KV pairs in some blocks. They also use rigid token selection schemes—isolated tokens or fixed-size chunks—which cannot flexibly preserve semantically important segments at arbitrary positions. There is a need for a more adaptive, fine-grained compression method that maintains important context while providing real throughput gains and integrating well with modern inference systems like PagedAttention/vLLM.
AI.method: The authors introduce Kara, a decoding-time, sliding-window KV cache compression technique. It only operates on recently generated context instead of the entire sequence, and uses bidirectional attention over the window to score and select informative KV pairs. To go beyond isolated tokens or fixed-size chunks, they design a Token2Chunk module that expands selected important tokens into variable-sized chunks, preserving contiguous semantic units. They also adapt Kara to PagedAttention and implement KvLLM, an inference framework on top of vLLM, that integrates Kara’s compression into a real-world system to reduce KV memory and enhance decoding throughput.
AI.result: Kara achieves significant KV cache memory reduction while preserving or improving model performance in reasoning tasks. When integrated into KvLLM over vLLM/PagedAttention, the method yields effective improvements in output throughput, demonstrating that the proposed compression strategy alleviates the memory and latency bottlenecks without substantial accuracy loss. Experiments across multiple setups show consistent performance gains compared to existing KV compression baselines.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that sliding-window, bidirectional-attention-based KV cache compression combined with flexible token-to-chunk expansion is an effective way to manage the memory and latency costs of long CoT reasoning. Kara and its implementation in KvLLM provide a practical, scalable solution that outperforms threshold-triggered and rigid-chunk baselines, enabling higher-throughput inference for reasoning language models while maintaining quality.

title: Discrete Diffusion Language Models for Interactive Radiology Report Drafting
summary: Diffusion language models, which generate text by denoising a token canvas bidirectionally instead of emitting tokens left to right, have become competitive with autoregressive (AR) generation. Medical foundation models, however, remain almost entirely autoregressive. We adapt a mixture-of-experts diffusion language model, DiffusionGemma-26B, and benchmark it against its same-size AR sibling Gemma-4-26B under an identical LoRA recipe on medical visual question answering datasets, scored by a verbosity-robust LLM judge. Diffusion matches or exceeds AR on all of them, and the finetuned model (3.8B active) is competitive with frontier vision-language models; its decoding is also 3.5-4.4x faster. Beyond this parity, the diffusion model offers a drafting capability AR lacks: any-order infill. Because the canvas is denoised bidirectionally, a radiologist can fix report fragments and have the model fill the text between them, an operation inherent to diffusion but not to autoregression, which is subpar at it. This suits real reports, which are often terse or inconsistent across clinicians and institutions.

AI.tldr: The paper compares a diffusion-based medical language model (DiffusionGemma-26B) with an autoregressive counterpart (Gemma-4-26B) for medical visual question answering, finding diffusion is as good or better, faster at decoding, and uniquely capable of flexible infilling useful for editing radiology reports.
AI.motivation: While diffusion language models are now competitive with autoregressive models in general NLP, medical foundation models remain predominantly autoregressive. The authors aim to explore whether diffusion models can match or outperform autoregressive ones in medical visual question answering and to exploit the unique infilling abilities of diffusion for realistic radiology-report workflows.
AI.method: They adapt a mixture-of-experts diffusion language model, DiffusionGemma-26B, and compare it against its autoregressive sibling Gemma-4-26B of equal size. Both are finetuned under an identical LoRA recipe and evaluated on medical visual question answering datasets. Evaluation uses a verbosity-robust LLM judge. They also measure decoding speed and demonstrate any-order infill for clinical report editing.
AI.result: DiffusionGemma-26B matches or exceeds the autoregressive Gemma-4-26B on all tested medical visual QA benchmarks. The finetuned diffusion model, with 3.8B active parameters, is competitive with frontier vision-language models while achieving 3.5–4.4× faster decoding than the AR baseline. It also supports any-order infilling in practice.
AI.conclusion: Diffusion language models are a viable and potentially superior alternative to autoregressive models for medical visual question answering. Besides comparable or better accuracy and faster decoding, their inherent bidirectional denoising enables powerful any-order infilling capabilities that better support real-world radiology reporting, where clinicians need to modify and complete partial or inconsistent reports.

title: SPARCLE: SPeaker-aware Aligned Representations via Contrastive Language Embeddings
summary:
Recent advances in speech synthesis have shifted from phoneme representations to direct grapheme modeling. While phonemes address the one-to-many mapping between text and acoustics, they rely on grapheme-to-phoneme (G2P) systems that fail to capture speaker-specific acoustic variation. Prior work demonstrates that grapheme-based models outperform phoneme-based systems at scale, but not in low-resource settings.
  In this paper, we propose SPARCLE, a speaker-aware grapheme representation model that enriches characters with their precise acoustic realizations. SPARCLE is trained with a contrastive objective to align graphemes with corresponding Wav2Vec2 acoustic representations while conditioned on speaker identity. The resulting model serves as a replacement to G2P systems for downstream text-to-speech (TTS) tasks. We demonstrate that SPARCLE improves generation quality, reducing word error rates by half in extreme low-resource settings compared to standard grapheme-based models.

AI.tldr: They introduce SPARCLE, a speaker-aware grapheme representation that replaces G2P for TTS and halves WER in extreme low-resource settings.
AI.motivation: Grapheme-based TTS scales better than phoneme-based systems but performs poorly in low-resource settings because it does not explicitly handle the text–acoustics mapping, and conventional G2P-based phoneme pipelines ignore speaker-specific acoustic variation. The paper aims to keep the simplicity of grapheme modeling while capturing precise, speaker-dependent acoustic realizations of text, especially when data is limited.
AI.method: They design SPARCLE, a representation model that maps graphemes to acoustically informed embeddings. It is trained with a contrastive learning objective to align each grapheme token with the corresponding Wav2Vec2 acoustic representation, conditioned on speaker identity to encode speaker-dependent realizations. This model is then used as a drop-in replacement for traditional G2P frontends in downstream TTS systems.
AI.result: In low-resource TTS experiments, SPARCLE-based systems achieve much better generation quality than standard grapheme-only models, with word error rates reduced by about 50% in extreme low-resource scenarios.
AI.conclusion: Enriching graphemes with speaker-aware acoustic information via contrastive alignment to self-supervised speech features provides a more effective text representation for TTS than both standard grapheme and traditional G2P-phoneme pipelines in low-resource settings, making SPARCLE a strong alternative frontend for TTS.

title: Beyond Next-Token Prediction: An RLVR Proof of Concept for Tool-Use Agents on Atlassian Workflows
summary: Large language models are trained to predict the next token, not to act inside a specific API. In niche enterprise SaaS workflows -- where success means hitting the right endpoint with the right nested arguments in the right order -- this objective mismatch shows up as silent failures: dropped required fields, hallucinated tools, or early stops after a single read. We ask whether Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), applied directly in the target environment, closes the gap. As a proof of concept we build a suite of five synthetic environments emulating the Jira REST v3 and Confluence v2 APIs at schema fidelity; rewards are computed entirely from the tool-call trace, with no live API, no learned judge, and no human label in the loop. Scoring prompted Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3.5-4B on the same checkers that drive GRPO training, we find that on the four scenarios whose rewards are non-degenerate the RL-trained policy lifts average reward from a 4B-baseline range of 0.35--0.92 to 0.95--1.00, with the largest single gain on Confluence page creation ($0.35 \rightarrow 1.00$). We position this as a preliminary step toward outcome-optimised small models for niche enterprise APIs, and foreground two limitations a workshop reader should weigh: hand-crafting verifiable rewards does not scale beyond the handful of endpoints reported here, and one of our five scenarios (ticket-transition) has a saturating reward shape that the prompted 4B already maxes out.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates whether Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR) can align small LLMs with complex enterprise SaaS APIs, demonstrating large gains in tool-calling reliability in synthetic Jira/Confluence environments but noting reward-specification and scenario-coverage limitations.
AI.motivation: LLMs trained purely for next-token prediction often fail silently when used as tool callers in structured enterprise SaaS workflows (e.g., Jira, Confluence), by omitting required arguments, hallucinating tools, or prematurely stopping. This objective mismatch between text prediction and precise API action selection is a key barrier to reliable deployment in niche business environments. The paper is motivated by the need for a practical way to directly optimize models for successful execution within specific APIs, without relying on live systems, human labels, or learned reward models.
AI.method: The authors construct five synthetic environments that faithfully emulate schemas of Jira REST v3 and Confluence v2 APIs. Within these sandboxes, an LLM interacts via tool calls, and rewards are computed automatically from the tool-call traces (sequence of endpoints, arguments, and ordering). They use Reinforcement Learning with Verifiable Rewards (RLVR), specifically GRPO-style training, to fine-tune Qwen3-1.7B and Qwen3.5-4B on these environments. The same rule-based checkers both define the rewards and later score the models, enabling closed-loop training and evaluation without external judges or human annotations.
AI.result: For the four scenarios where the reward signal is non-degenerate, RLVR substantially improves tool-usage reliability: average reward for the 4B model is raised from an initial range of 0.35–0.92 to 0.95–1.00. The most pronounced improvement occurs on Confluence page creation, where average reward jumps from 0.35 to 1.00, indicating near-perfect adherence to the required multi-step API workflow. One scenario, ticket-transition, exhibits a saturating reward landscape that the prompted 4B baseline already solves, leaving no headroom for RL to improve.
AI.conclusion: The study provides early evidence that RLVR, applied in realistic synthetic API environments with fully verifiable rewards, can align small LLMs to perform complex enterprise SaaS workflows much more reliably than prompting alone. However, the approach currently depends on hand-crafted, endpoint-specific reward functions and covers only a few workflows, limiting scalability and generality. The authors frame their work as a preliminary step toward outcome-optimized, small, enterprise-specific LLMs, and emphasize the need for more scalable reward-specification methods and broader scenario coverage.

title: Breaking Safety at the Token Boundary: How BPE Tokenization Creates Exploitable Gaps in LLM Alignment
summary: Character-level perturbations bypass safety alignment in modern LLMs despite leaving prompts human-readable. We identify and test a central structural mechanism: BPE tokenization fragments safety-critical words into sub-word pieces, and the three public alignment datasets we surveyed contain no intentionally fragmented inputs. The mechanism is a chain, tested end-to-end on five model families (Qwen-3-4B, Qwen-2.5-7B, Gemma-3-4B, Llama-3.1-8B, Mistral-7B). An optimization targeting safety-token fragmentation flips the first-token refusal trigger on 80-100% of refused HarmBench prompts, with 48% of those flips producing genuinely harmful outputs (per-model 29-65%; gap-vs-behavior ROC-AUC 0.66-0.98, pooled 0.84). Activation patching localizes the disrupted signal to the last ${\sim}30\%$ of layers; an alignment-data scan finds zero fragmented prompts among 30,000 examples (positive-control recall $\geq 99\%$ at attack-relevant intensities); and targeted-mutation experiments isolate safety words as the disruption locus. On the defense side, a 68-cell grid (55 trained checkpoints) shows that no DPO configuration achieves seed- and pool-stable ASR closure on the three families with closed pool-size confounds. SFT trained on fragmented prompts closes ASR on 3/5 families but only via global collapse that raises refusal on benign prompts as well, indicating the missing distribution is necessary but not sufficient under the LoRA-16 recipe we tested. To distinguish selective repair from global collapse, we introduce Conv-Benign, a candidate paired diagnostic. All ASR claims are 3-judge-calibrated (cell rankings stable across judges; absolute levels $\pm$18pp; see App.~B.13).

AI.tldr: The paper shows that small character-level changes that fragment safety-related words at the tokenizer level can systematically bypass safety alignment in modern LLMs, because alignment data did not include such fragmented forms.
AI.motivation: Modern LLMs are aligned using datasets and methods that assume clean, well-formed text, yet in practice adversaries can modify prompts at the character level while keeping them human-readable. The authors want to understand why and how such simple perturbations work so reliably, and whether current alignment procedures and datasets are structurally vulnerable to these attacks.
AI.method: They study how BPE tokenization breaks safety-critical words into subword tokens and note that public alignment datasets lack intentionally fragmented prompts. They then: (1) design an optimization procedure that targets safety-token fragmentation to flip refusal behaviors on HarmBench prompts across multiple model families; (2) use activation patching to locate where in the network the safety signal is disrupted; (3) scan alignment corpora to check for fragmented instances of safety words; (4) run targeted mutation experiments to confirm that safety words themselves are the key locus of disruption; and (5) explore defenses, training dozens of DPO and SFT checkpoints, including SFT on fragmented prompts, and propose a diagnostic benchmark (Conv-Benign) to distinguish selective repair from global collapse.
AI.result: The safety-token fragmentation attack flips the initial refusal trigger for 80–100% of previously refused HarmBench prompts, and roughly half of these flips yield genuinely harmful outputs. The disrupted safety signal is localized to later layers in the models. Alignment datasets examined contain effectively no fragmented prompts at attack-relevant intensities, confirming a distributional gap. Experiments show that mutating safety words drives the effect. Defense experiments reveal that no tested DPO setting reliably closes attack success rates across seeds and data pools; SFT on fragmented prompts can close attacks in some models but only by broadly increasing refusals, including on benign inputs.
AI.conclusion: Character-level perturbations that exploit BPE tokenization gaps represent a structural weakness in current LLM safety alignment: safety behaviors rely heavily on unfragmented safety keywords and are not robust to realistic text perturbations absent from training data. Simple post-hoc alignment methods such as DPO, under standard recipes, fail to reliably fix this without undesirable side effects. Including fragmented prompts is necessary but not sufficient for robust safety; better methods are needed to selectively repair safety behavior, and Conv-Benign is proposed as a diagnostic to separate true robustness from blunt over-refusal.

title: World Feedback for Clinical Agents: Diagnosing RL in FHIR Environments
summary: Clinical protocol-execution tasks -- checking a lab value, applying a threshold, placing a correctly structured FHIR order -- are natural candidates for RL from world feedback: once clinical SMEs encode decision logic into a verifier, that verifier grades unlimited rollouts without per-episode annotation. But applying RL requires a sound feedback channel and sufficient base capability. We audit MedAgentBench v1/v2, find a 41.7\% silent-finish ceiling that makes inaction the RL dominant strategy, and construct \textbf{MedAgentBench-v3 (MAB-v3)} (508 tasks, 8.9\% ceiling). Training Qwen3-8B exposes two structural barriers: a \emph{capability ceiling} (10/20 task types have 0\% base performance, zero gradient) and a \emph{format-knowledge barrier} (3/20 types require exact clinical codes undiscoverable by exploration). Pure RL reaches 18.2\% pass@1 vs.\ 34.1\% for rule-based SFT; the 15.9~pp gap is attributable entirely to these barriers. A decision/format-knowledge/lookup taxonomy predicts RL learnability and prescribes the fix: SFT to inject codes, RL to learn conditionals.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates and improves a reinforcement-learning benchmark for clinical protocol-execution agents, showing that structural issues in the benchmark and in model capabilities limit RL performance, and proposing a taxonomy-guided combination of supervised fine-tuning and RL as a remedy.
AI.motivation: Clinical protocol execution (e.g., checking labs, applying thresholds, placing FHIR orders) appears well-suited to reinforcement learning from verifiers, because once experts encode decision rules, models can be trained with large-scale automatic feedback instead of costly human annotations. However, successful RL requires a reliable feedback channel and sufficient initial capability, and it is unclear whether existing medical agent benchmarks and models satisfy these conditions.
AI.method: The authors audit existing MedAgentBench v1/v2 and identify a high "silent-finish" rate, where doing nothing is often scored as acceptable, which makes inaction a dominant RL strategy. They then construct MedAgentBench-v3 with more robust task design that reduces this ceiling. Using MAB-v3, they train Qwen3-8B with RL and analyze learning dynamics across 20 task types to identify structural barriers: tasks where the model has zero base performance (capability ceiling) and tasks requiring exact clinical codes that cannot be discovered through exploration (format-knowledge barrier). They compare pure RL against rule-based supervised fine-tuning and develop a taxonomy of task types (decision, format-knowledge, lookup) to reason about RL learnability and appropriate training strategies.
AI.result: Auditing reveals that MedAgentBench v1/v2 has a 41.7% silent-finish ceiling, incentivizing inaction under RL. MedAgentBench-v3 reduces this ceiling to 8.9% across 508 tasks. When training Qwen3-8B on MAB-v3, the authors find that half of the 20 task types show zero base performance (yielding no gradient for RL) and three task types depend on exact clinical codes inaccessible to naive exploration. Pure RL achieves 18.2% pass@1, while rule-based supervised fine-tuning achieves 34.1% pass@1, and the 15.9 percentage-point gap is fully explained by the identified structural barriers rather than by RL instability per se.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that naive application of RL to clinical protocol-execution agents is fundamentally limited by benchmark design flaws, initial capability ceilings, and format-knowledge barriers. RL cannot learn tasks that require unknown, precise clinical codes or those where the base model has no competence, and misleading reward structures can favor inaction. A taxonomy based on decision complexity, format knowledge, and lookup requirements predicts which tasks are RL-learnable and suggests a hybrid strategy: use supervised fine-tuning to inject required codes and basic competence, then apply RL to learn conditional decision logic. This provides guidance for building more effective medical agent benchmarks and training pipelines.

title: Prompt Framing Distorts Count-Based Evaluation of LLM Error Detection: Evidence from Numeric Anchoring
comment: 15 pages, 6 figures, 12 tables. Preprint under review
summary: Count-based F1 is widely used as a proxy for LLM error-detection quality, but this paper shows that it can rise dramatically without a corresponding improvement in span localization, a gap termed F1 Inflation. The paper introduces ErrorBench, a controlled stress-test protocol for prompt-induced count distortion. ErrorBench evaluates six contemporary LLMs under five prompt conditions over 4,290 responses from 143 CoNLL-2014 passages. Under CoNLL-2014 M2-style scoring, anchored prompts produce up to 0.79 points of F1 Inflation, and up to 0.96 under strict matching. A 100-passage replication using the official ERRANT 3.0.0 pipeline and multi-reference scoring reproduces the pattern: averaged over six models, the Blind-to-Anchored prompt shift raises Count-F1 by +0.21 while raising multi-reference ERRANT F0.5 by only +0.04. The study finds larger count responses in highly instruction-compliant GPT/Claude systems and smaller responses in the Gemini family under this stress-test protocol. The findings suggest that LLM proofreading and document-review evaluations should avoid pre-populated error counts and should report span-aware metrics alongside count-based metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that count-based F1 for LLM error detection can be artificially inflated by prompts without real gains in correctly locating errors, and introduces ErrorBench to quantify this effect and recommend better evaluation practices.
AI.motivation: Researchers and practitioners often use simple count-based F1 scores as a proxy for how well language models detect errors in text (e.g., proofreading, grammatical error correction). However, these metrics ignore whether the model correctly identifies the exact spans of errors, and they might be heavily influenced by how the task is prompted. The authors are motivated to test whether prompts that hint at the number of errors can artificially boost these scores, thereby misleading evaluation of LLM proofreading ability.
AI.method: The authors design ErrorBench, a controlled stress-test that manipulates prompts to induce different error-count behaviors in LLMs. They evaluate six modern LLMs under five distinct prompt conditions on 4,290 responses derived from 143 CoNLL-2014 passages. They compare count-based F1 against span-aware metrics using CoNLL-2014 M2-style scoring and a strict matching regime. They also replicate the analysis on 100 passages using the official ERRANT 3.0.0 pipeline with multi-reference scoring, focusing on how Blind vs. Anchored prompts shift both Count-F1 and ERRANT F0.5. They examine cross-model differences (e.g., GPT/Claude vs. Gemini) in how much their predicted error counts change under these prompt manipulations.
AI.result: Under CoNLL-2014 M2-style scoring, prompts that include explicit error-count anchors produce F1 Inflation of up to 0.79 points, and up to 0.96 under stricter span matching. In the ERRANT 3.0.0 multi-reference setup on 100 passages, moving from Blind to Anchored prompts increases Count-F1 by +0.21 on average across six models, but improves ERRANT F0.5 by only +0.04, showing that much of the gain in Count-F1 is illusory. The analysis also reveals systematic model differences: instruction-following models like GPT and Claude tend to output larger error counts in response to the stress-test prompts, whereas Gemini models show smaller count changes.
AI.conclusion: Count-based F1 is an unreliable standalone measure of LLM error-detection quality, because it is highly susceptible to prompt-induced distortions in predicted error counts that do not correspond to better span localization. The ErrorBench protocol exposes this vulnerability and shows that anchored or pre-populated error counts can artificially inflate evaluation scores. Therefore, evaluations of LLM proofreading and document review should avoid prompts that hint at or specify error counts, and they should always report span-aware metrics (e.g., M2, ERRANT) alongside count-based ones to obtain a more faithful picture of model performance.

title: Procedural Memory Distillation: Online Reflection for Self-Improving Language Models
summary: Reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR), along with recent selfdistillation variants such as SDPO, evaluates each rollout against a verifier and updates the policy from that episode-level signal. However, the richer procedural information in the rollout is rarely retained or reused. Across episodes and epochs, the model repeatedly encounters related problems under a changing policy, producing cross-episode signals that episode-local updates cannot capture: which strategies consistently pass verification, which failure modes persist, which patterns recur. We propose Procedural Memory Distillation (PMD), which converts these crossepisode signals into reusable procedural memory and distills it into the policy's weights during training. This memory functions as a training scaffold, absorbed into the policy itself, yielding a memory-free model at inference. PMD organizes the memory at three levels of abstraction: raw trajectories, self-reflected strategies and lessons, and higher-level behavioral patterns that recur across problems, all extracted online from the model's own trajectories. A memory-conditioned self-teacher draws on the accumulated experience to supervise the student on its own rollouts, enabling student to progressively internalize procedural knowledge within its parameters. The central design principle is co-evolution: the policy generates rollouts that update the memory, and memory shapes the supervision that updates the policy. Empirically, across Qwen3-8B and OLMo3-Instruct-7B, PMD improves over SDPO by 3.8-5.5% on SCIKNOWEVAL and 7.9-13.6% on LIVECODEBENCH. Co-evolution powers these gains: freezing either the memory or the policy trails PMD by more than 10% across SCIKNOWEVAL domains.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Procedural Memory Distillation (PMD), a framework that turns cross-episode experience from RL with verifiable rewards into reusable procedural memory that is distilled into a policy, leading to notable performance gains over SDPO on scientific reasoning and coding benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Existing RL with verifiable rewards methods like RLVR and SDPO only use episode-level verifier feedback and largely ignore rich procedural signals within and across trajectories, such as recurring successful strategies, systematic failure modes, and higher-level behavioral patterns. This leads to inefficient reuse of experience: the model repeatedly faces related problems but lacks a mechanism to accumulate and exploit cross-episode procedural knowledge, limiting performance and generalization.
AI.method: PMD builds a training-time procedural memory that is distilled into the policy so that no external memory is needed at inference. The memory is constructed online from the model’s own rollouts and organized at three abstraction levels: (1) raw trajectories capturing detailed sequences of actions; (2) self-reflected strategies and lessons summarizing what worked or failed; and (3) higher-level behavioral patterns that recur across tasks. A memory-conditioned self-teacher uses this accumulated memory to supervise the policy on its rollouts. Training follows a co-evolution principle: the policy generates new rollouts that update the memory, and the evolving memory conditions the self-teacher that updates the policy weights.
AI.result: On Qwen3-8B and OLMo3-Instruct-7B, PMD outperforms SDPO by 3.8–5.5 percentage points on the SCIKNOWEVAL benchmark and by 7.9–13.6 percentage points on LIVECODEBENCH. Ablations show that the co-evolution mechanism is critical: freezing either the memory or the policy reduces performance by more than 10 percentage points across SCIKNOWEVAL domains compared with full PMD.
AI.conclusion: Procedural Memory Distillation effectively converts cross-episode signals in RL with verifiable rewards into reusable procedural knowledge embedded in the policy parameters. By organizing memory at multiple abstraction levels and enforcing co-evolution between policy and memory, PMD achieves significantly better performance than existing self-distillation approaches like SDPO on scientific reasoning and coding tasks, highlighting the value of dynamic procedural memory as a training scaffold.

title: Mapping Text to Multiplex Graph: Prompt Compression as Lévy Walk-Guided Graph Pruning
summary: Existing prompt compression methods treat text as flat token sequences, failing to capture the distributed nature of important information, which is often spread across multiple locations and connected through both local syntactic dependencies and global semantic relations. Such relational structure is naturally represented as a graph, where tokens or sentences become nodes and their dependencies become edges. To this end, we propose RAGP, which formulates prompt compression as Redundancy-Aware Graph Pruning on a multiplex graph that jointly models fine-grained attention-based dependencies and coarse-grained semantic relations. To efficiently identify non-redundant nodes in this heterogeneous structure (dense local subgraphs and sparse global connections), we employ Levy walks whose heavy-tailed step distribution naturally balances local exploitation with global exploration. Experiments on LongBench show that RAGP achieves an average score of 49.3 under a 4x compression ratio, outperforming existing LLM-based compression methods, such as LongLLMLingua, which attains 48.8 at a 3x compression ratio. Besides, RAGP also surpasses state-of-the-art vision-based text compression paradigms on multiple tasks. The code is available at https://anonymous.4open.science/r/RAGP-B0CB.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes RAGP, a redundancy-aware graph-based prompt compression method that models prompts as multiplex graphs and prunes them via Levy-walk-based node selection, outperforming prior LLM- and vision-based compression methods on LongBench and other tasks under stronger compression ratios.
AI.motivation: Existing prompt compression methods treat prompts as flat token sequences, ignoring that important information is distributed and relational, connected via local syntactic and global semantic dependencies. This leads to suboptimal compression that either drops crucial information or fails to exploit structural redundancy, especially for very long prompts where relational structure matters. The authors aim to better capture and utilize this relational structure to improve compression quality at high compression ratios.
AI.method: They represent the prompt as a multiplex graph, with nodes corresponding to tokens or sentences and edges encoding two types of dependencies: (1) fine-grained, attention-based local relations, and (2) coarse-grained, semantic global relations. Prompt compression is framed as redundancy-aware graph pruning—selecting a subset of informative, non-redundant nodes. To navigate this heterogeneous graph (dense local subgraphs vs. sparse global links), they use Levy walks, whose heavy-tailed step-length distribution allows mostly short steps for local exploitation with occasional long jumps for global exploration. Nodes visited or scored highly through this process are retained to form a compressed prompt, achieving a target compression ratio.
AI.result: On LongBench, under a 4x compression ratio, RAGP attains an average score of 49.3, outperforming LongLLMLingua, which achieves 48.8 at a weaker 3x compression ratio. RAGP also exceeds state-of-the-art vision-based text compression methods across multiple evaluation tasks, demonstrating better effectiveness and robustness in compressing long prompts while preserving task performance.
AI.conclusion: Modeling prompts as multiplex graphs and performing redundancy-aware pruning via Levy walks yields more effective prompt compression than prior sequence-based or vision-based methods, particularly under strong compression constraints. The relational, graph-based view of prompts is key to capturing distributed important information and balancing local and global dependencies during compression.

title: The Agentic Garden of Forking Paths
summary: Empirical research rarely admits a unique analysis. Different analytical choices can lead to different conclusions from the same data, yet these hidden forking paths are difficult to observe. We show that AI agents capture much of the analytical variation among human researchers while making these paths explicit. Across four high-stakes domains, assigning different personas is sufficient for AI agents to report divergent, often opposing, conclusions from the same data and question, with findings systematically aligned with those beliefs. In a study in which 42 human research teams analyzed the same immigration dataset, AI agents reproduced 72% of the human ideological gap in reported effect estimates. Despite reaching opposing conclusions, it is difficult to identify clear issues in each analysis based on the final AI reports: 86% passed independent AI review and 78% passed majority human expert review. These findings suggest that the central challenge is often not flawed analyses, but selective exploration and reporting from a large space of methodologically defensible analyses. AI agents may amplify this longstanding problem by making such exploration inexpensive and scalable. To address this, we introduce the m-value (multiverse value), the probability that an analysis path would produce a claim at least as extreme as the reported one. We further introduce Agentic Bootstrap, which estimates the m-value by using AI agents to sample plausible analysis paths. Applied to the human immigration study, 13.5% of reported human analyses fell in the most extreme 5% of the analysis space (m<0.05). Scientific evidence should therefore be evaluated not only by a single reported analysis but also by its position within the distribution of analyses that could reasonably have been reported. Agentic Bootstrap makes this distribution observable and turns it into a criterion for scientific credibility.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that AI agents, when given different “personas,” can reproduce and even scale the hidden analytical flexibility (“forking paths”) that human researchers exhibit, and proposes a new metric (m-value) and a method (Agentic Bootstrap) to quantify how extreme a reported result is within the universe of defensible analyses.
AI.motivation: Empirical studies often allow many reasonable analytical choices (model specification, controls, transformations, subsets, etc.), and different choices can yield very different conclusions from the same data. These forking paths are usually invisible in published work, enabling selective reporting and making it hard to judge the robustness or credibility of findings. With the rise of AI agents that can conduct end-to-end analyses, this problem may become more severe because AI can cheaply explore many such paths. The authors aim to (1) document how well AI agents can mimic the diversity and bias of human analyses, and (2) provide tools to make the full analysis “multiverse” explicit and test how extreme any reported result is within it.
AI.method: The authors run AI agents as data analysts under different assigned personas or prior beliefs across four high-stakes domains. They compare the agents’ conclusions from the same data and question, assessing whether the results systematically align with the assigned beliefs and whether conclusions diverge. In a key benchmark, they replicate a study where 42 human teams analyzed the same immigration dataset; AI agents are tasked with analyzing the same data and question under differing ideological personas. They then evaluate the quality of the AI-generated analyses using both independent AI review and majority human expert review. To address selective exploration, they propose (1) the m-value, defined as the probability that a randomly sampled defensible analysis path would yield a claim at least as extreme as the reported one, and (2) Agentic Bootstrap, which uses AI agents to systematically sample plausible analysis paths to estimate this m-value. They apply Agentic Bootstrap to the existing human immigration analyses to map where each reported analysis lies within the broader distribution of possible results.
AI.result: AI agents, when given different personas, produced divergent and often opposing conclusions from the same datasets, with results that systematically matched their assigned beliefs. In the immigration benchmark, the AI agents were able to reproduce 72% of the ideological gap in effect estimates observed among 42 human research teams. Despite these divergent and sometimes extreme conclusions, most AI analyses appeared methodologically defensible based on post hoc review: 86% passed independent AI checks and 78% passed majority human expert evaluation. Using Agentic Bootstrap to explore the analysis multiverse, the authors found that 13.5% of the human teams’ reported analyses fell within the most extreme 5% of possible analysis paths (m < 0.05).
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that the main threat to credible empirical inference is not predominantly overtly flawed analyses, but rather selective exploration and reporting from a wide space of methodologically defensible choices. AI agents can both mimic and potentially amplify this problem, since they make it cheap and fast to search many forking paths and pick ones that align with prior beliefs or desired narratives. To mitigate this, the authors argue that scientific claims should be evaluated in the context of the full distribution of reasonable analyses that could have been reported. Their proposed tools—the m-value and Agentic Bootstrap—make that distribution observable and provide an additional credibility criterion: results with low m-values (very extreme relative to the analysis multiverse) warrant extra scrutiny. This shifts the standard of evidence from judging a single reported analysis to assessing how typical or extreme it is within the multiverse of defensible analytic decisions.

title: Office Comprehension Benchmark
summary: We introduce Office Comprehension Bench (OCB), the first public benchmark to jointly evaluate LLM systems on Word, Excel, and PowerPoint comprehension over native file formats (.docx, .xlsx, .pptx) and their variants. OCB consists of two tracks. File Fidelity Q&A tests structural and visual perception of office artifacts - tables, charts, embedded images, formulas, and app-specific elements such as headers, speaker notes, and named ranges. Domain Q&A tests expert-level reasoning grounded in real-world industry documents across 12 professional domains, with queries requiring multi-step analysis and synthesis across documents. Each reference answer is decomposed into atomic, binary-gradable claims, and an ensemble of LLM judges scores responses against each claim independently. Even the strongest frontier system in its default reasoning mode reaches only about 59.3% on Domain Q&A; increasing thinking depth within a tier does not move performance materially, while moving to a higher product tier yields modest gains. We release the dataset, evaluation tooling, judge prompt, and a public leaderboard.

AI.tldr: Introduces Office Comprehension Bench (OCB), a benchmark to test LLMs’ ability to understand and reason over native Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files, showing current frontier models perform far from human-level, especially on complex domain reasoning.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM benchmarks largely focus on plain text and do not sufficiently evaluate models’ capabilities on real-world office documents, which contain complex structures (tables, charts, images, formulas) and require application-specific understanding (headers, speaker notes, named ranges). There is also a need to test deeper, expert-level, domain-specific reasoning grounded in realistic multi-document office artifacts, and to do so in a way that allows fine-grained, objective scoring.
AI.method: They construct OCB with two evaluation tracks: (1) File Fidelity Q&A, which focuses on structural and visual understanding of office elements in Word, Excel, and PowerPoint files and their format variants; and (2) Domain Q&A, which involves expert-level queries over real industry documents from 12 domains, requiring multi-step reasoning and synthesis across multiple documents. Each ground-truth answer is broken down into atomic, binary-gradable claims. An ensemble of LLM judges then scores a model’s response by independently checking each claim, enabling fine-grained automated evaluation. They test multiple LLM systems under different “thinking depth” configurations and product tiers, and build accompanying tooling, prompts, and a leaderboard.
AI.result: Frontier LLMs show limited performance: the strongest system, in its default reasoning configuration, achieves only about 59.3% accuracy on the challenging Domain Q&A track. Increasing “thinking depth” (e.g., encouraging longer reasoning within the same model tier) does not significantly improve performance, while moving to more capable product tiers gives only modest gains. The benchmark and tools are released publicly to support further research and comparison.
AI.conclusion: OCB establishes a comprehensive, realistic benchmark for evaluating LLMs on native office document comprehension and domain-specific reasoning. It reveals that current frontier models are far from mastering these tasks, especially in expert-level, multi-step scenarios, and that simply prompting for deeper reasoning is insufficient. The release of data, evaluation infrastructure, and a leaderboard is intended to catalyze progress on LLMs that can robustly understand and reason over real-world office documents.

title: Janus: a Playground for User-Involved Agentic Permission Management
comment: Code and data released on GitHub: https://github.com/GraceBrigham/Janus
summary: AI agents that autonomously execute tool calls on a user's behalf raise pressing questions about permission management: what role could users play, and what role should they play? Despite many proposed approaches, the user's role in agentic permission management remains under explored. We introduce Janus, a playground system for implementing and evaluating user-involved agentic permission management designs. Janus consists of two components: Janus-Core, a modular agentic system supporting a diverse spectrum of permission management designs, and Janus-Harness, an automated evaluation framework. Grounded in a conceptual model that identifies key design axes for user involvement, we implement six permission assistants spanning the design space and evaluate them across three scenarios and three synthetic responders. We demonstrate that user input is critical and can significantly strengthen privacy and security, that AI augmentation of user decisions can help reduce cognitive load, and that realistic user behavior including permission fatigue must be accounted for in system design. No single design performs optimally across all contexts, motivating a more principled and context-sensitive approach to deploying permission assistants in agentic systems. Janus is publicly available to support future investigation into this dimension of agentic system design.

AI.tldr: The paper presents Janus, a system and evaluation framework to study how users should be involved in managing permissions for AI agents that autonomously call tools.
AI.motivation: Autonomous AI agents increasingly execute actions and access sensitive data via tools, creating privacy and security risks. Existing work proposes various permission management approaches, but the concrete role of the user—how and when they should be involved in granting, reviewing, or delegating permissions—is poorly understood and under-explored. There is a need for a structured way to design, implement, and compare different user-involved permission schemes for agentic systems.
AI.method: The authors design Janus, composed of (1) Janus-Core, a modular agent framework that supports implementing different permission management schemes along key design axes of user involvement, and (2) Janus-Harness, an automated evaluation environment. They first build a conceptual model that identifies important design dimensions for user involvement in permissions. Based on this model, they implement six distinct “permission assistants” that occupy different points in the design space. They then run controlled experiments across three task scenarios and three types of synthetic user responders to systematically evaluate security, privacy, and usability trade-offs, including phenomena such as permission fatigue.
AI.result: Experiments show that involving users in permission management substantially improves privacy and security outcomes compared to fully autonomous approaches. AI-powered augmentation of user decisions can lower cognitive load and improve decision quality. However, realistic user behavior—such as susceptibility to fatigue, over-approval, or under-approval—strongly affects outcomes, and different permission designs perform variably across tasks and contexts. No single permission assistant dominates across all evaluation settings.
