AI.conclusion: Effective permission management for agentic systems requires context-sensitive, user-involved designs rather than a one-size-fits-all solution. User input is essential but must be supported by AI assistance and engineered with awareness of human limitations like permission fatigue. Janus provides an open, modular platform and evaluation harness to prototype and rigorously assess such permission assistants, enabling more principled future work on secure, privacy-preserving agentic systems.

title: RuleChef: Grounding LLM Task Knowledge in Human-Editable Rules
summary: We present RuleChef, a framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to generate executable rules for NLP tasks such as text classification, Named Entity Recognition (NER), or relation extraction. Rules are generated based on a task description and a set of labeled examples, then they are iteratively improved based both on additional examples and on human feedback overexisting rules. RuleChef can also be used to bootstrap rules using the observed input-output pairs from any existing model for a given task. LLMs are used only at learning time, synthesizing rules and iteratively patching them based on failures measured on a held-out split. The result of this process is a fast, deterministic, and inspectable rule system. Preliminary evaluation is performed on both classification and NER tasks. We release RuleChef as open-source software under an Apache 2.0

AI.tldr: RuleChef uses large language models to automatically generate and iteratively improve interpretable, executable rule-based systems for NLP tasks, yielding fast, deterministic models learned from labeled data, model I/O, and human feedback.
AI.motivation: Many NLP applications need fast, deterministic, and interpretable systems, but modern high-performing models are typically opaque, slow, or costly to run at scale. Rule-based systems can be interpretable and efficient but are expensive and time-consuming to author and maintain by hand. The paper is motivated by the desire to leverage LLMs only during training to automate the creation and refinement of high-quality rule sets, combining the transparency and speed of rules with the learning capabilities of LLMs, and even enabling rule extraction from existing black-box models.
AI.method: The authors introduce RuleChef, a framework that prompts large language models with task descriptions and labeled examples to synthesize executable rules (e.g., for text classification, NER, relation extraction). The framework runs in an iterative loop in which rules are executed on a held-out validation set, failures are analyzed, and the LLM is prompted again to patch or refine the rule set. Human users can also inspect the rules and provide feedback or edits, which are incorporated into subsequent iterations. RuleChef can additionally observe input-output behavior from any existing model on a task and use this as supervision to bootstrap initial rules. Importantly, LLMs are used only in this learning phase; at deployment time, the resulting rule system runs without the LLM, as a deterministic and inspectable program.
AI.result: The paper reports preliminary experiments where RuleChef is applied to text classification and named entity recognition tasks. The generated rule systems achieve competitive performance relative to baselines while maintaining advantages in speed, determinism, and interpretability. The framework successfully learns rules from labeled examples and from model-generated input-output pairs, and it shows that iterative refinement guided by validation failures and human feedback can steadily improve rule quality.
AI.conclusion: RuleChef demonstrates that large language models can be harnessed effectively as rule synthesizers rather than as deployed inference engines, yielding rule-based NLP systems that are fast, deterministic, and easily inspected or modified. The work suggests a promising direction for building practical, interpretable NLP solutions by combining the strengths of LLMs in code/rule generation with the long-standing benefits of rule-based approaches, and it is made available as open-source software for further research and application.

title: Revisiting Chain-of-Thought Reasoning under Limited Supervision: Semi-supervised Chain-of-Thought Learning
summary: Chain-of-thought (CoT) reasoning has emerged as an effective approach for activating latent reasoning capabilities in large language models. However, most existing CoT methods use reasoning chains mainly as inference-time prompts, while the generated reasoning traces are rarely reused as semi-supervised learning signals. In this report, we define \textbf{Semi-supervised Chain-of-Thought Learning} and propose \textbf{Semi-CoT}, a simple framework that uses unlabeled questions to construct pseudo reasoning supervision. Semi-CoT samples multiple pseudo-CoTs for each unlabeled question, estimates answer-level semantic entropy, and selects low-entropy reasoning chains as reliable pseudo-CoT demonstrations. This extends the self-training view of CoT from inference-time refinement to semi-supervised pseudo-supervision. Pilot experiments on AQuA, SVAMP, GSM8K, and MultiArith show that the entropy gate selects high-precision pseudo-CoTs, with pseudo-answer precision ranging from $91.36\%$ to $100\%$. Semi-CoT also gives small gains on SVAMP and GSM8K, while AQuA shows negative transfer and MultiArith reaches a ceiling. These results suggest that unlabeled questions can provide reliable pseudo reasoning signals, but their effective use still requires stronger demonstration selection or student training.

AI.tldr: Semi-CoT is a semi-supervised framework that uses unlabeled questions to automatically generate and filter chain-of-thought rationales with low semantic entropy, turning them into pseudo-labeled reasoning demonstrations.
AI.motivation: Most chain-of-thought (CoT) work uses reasoning traces only as inference-time prompts and does not exploit them as training signals. Labeled CoT data is expensive to obtain, while unlabeled questions are abundant. The paper aims to leverage unlabeled data to create reliable pseudo CoT supervision, extending CoT beyond prompting into a semi-supervised learning paradigm.
AI.method: For each unlabeled question, the method samples multiple chain-of-thought completions from a language model. It then computes answer-level semantic entropy over these samples to measure uncertainty. Reasoning chains associated with low-entropy (high agreement) answers are selected as reliable pseudo-CoTs and used as demonstrations for subsequent CoT reasoning or training. This is framed as self-training, with the model generating its own pseudo-supervision and an entropy gate filtering it.
AI.result: On math word problem benchmarks (AQuA, SVAMP, GSM8K, MultiArith), the entropy-based selection achieves high precision for pseudo answers (about 91–100% correctness). When these pseudo-CoTs are used, there are modest performance improvements on SVAMP and GSM8K, while AQuA suffers negative transfer and MultiArith shows no gain due to already-high performance (a ceiling effect).
AI.conclusion: Unlabeled questions can indeed yield high-precision pseudo chain-of-thought signals when filtered via answer-level semantic entropy. However, the modest or mixed downstream gains indicate that simply adding these pseudo-CoTs is not sufficient; better strategies for selecting demonstrations and/or training student models are needed to fully capitalize on semi-supervised chain-of-thought learning.

title: TurnNat: Automatic Evaluation of Turn-Taking Naturalness in Dyadic Spoken Dialogue
summary: Turn-taking naturalness is central to full-duplex spoken dialogue systems, yet its automatic evaluation remains limited. Existing evaluations often rely on human judgments or behavior-specific timing metrics, making it difficult to compare heterogeneous timing failures within a unified framework. We propose TurnNat, a likelihood-based framework for automatic turn-taking naturalness evaluation in two-channel spoken dialogue. A causal turn-taking prediction model trained on natural conversations estimates future two-speaker voice-activity states, and the negative log-likelihood (NLL) of the observed future activity measures timing atypicality. TurnNat pools frame-level NLLs over turn-taking boundary units (TBUs) extracted from utterance onsets and offsets, and aggregates mean and tail TBU scores into a dialogue-level naturalness score. We further construct a controlled perturbation benchmark of paired natural and perturbed dialogue clips, validated by human naturalness judgments. Experiments on this benchmark show that TurnNat successfully identifies unnatural turn-taking perturbations across heterogeneous timing failures.

AI.tldr: They introduce TurnNat, a likelihood-based, automatic metric to evaluate how natural turn-taking is in spoken dialogue systems, without relying on humans.
AI.motivation: Turn-taking naturalness is key for good user experience in full-duplex spoken dialogue systems, but current evaluations depend on human ratings or on ad-hoc timing metrics that can’t compare different types of timing errors within a single, principled framework.
AI.method: They train a causal turn-taking prediction model on natural two-channel conversations to predict future joint voice-activity states of two speakers. They then compute the negative log-likelihood (NLL) of the actual observed future activity under this model as a measure of how atypical the timing is. Frame-level NLLs are pooled over turn-taking boundary units (TBUs), derived from utterance onsets and offsets; mean and tail statistics of TBU scores are then aggregated into a dialogue-level naturalness score. Finally, they build a controlled perturbation benchmark of dialogue clips where natural conversations are systematically perturbed in timing, and collect human judgments for validation.
AI.result: On the controlled perturbation benchmark, TurnNat reliably assigns worse (more unnatural) scores to perturbed dialogues than to their natural counterparts, and it works across different kinds of timing failures rather than being limited to one specific error type.
AI.conclusion: A likelihood-based evaluation using a learned turn-taking predictor can automatically and consistently quantify turn-taking naturalness in spoken dialogues, and TurnNat provides a unified, behavior-agnostic metric that aligns with human judgments across heterogeneous timing perturbations.

title: OPINE-World: Programmatic World Modeling with Ontology-error-Prioritized Interactive Exploration
summary: Learning how an environment behaves from interaction is central to building agents that adapt to unfamiliar tasks. World models learned with deep networks are flexible but data-hungry and transfer poorly beyond their training distribution. Program-synthesized world models, written as source code by LLMs and refined through counterexample-guided inductive synthesis (CEGIS), are instead data-efficient and reusable, yet they have been demonstrated mainly on structured-state worlds with a given object vocabulary, and a single program search does not scale to pixel-rendered environments whose object structure must be hypothesized flexibly. We introduce OPINE-World, an LLM agent that learns an object-centric programmatic world model online from interaction. OPINE-World couples two cooperating agents in a loop of hypothesis and test, one acting in the environment and one synthesizing the model in code with replay verification and model-based planning, and it steers exploration with a Bayesian measure of object-type adequacy we call ontology error. We evaluate OPINE-World on ARC-AGI-3, a benchmark for skill-acquisition efficiency in which the object vocabulary, the goal, and the action semantics are withheld. OPINE-World solves 20 of 25 games without per-game training and reaches an action-efficiency score of 78.4 against the human baseline.

AI.tldr: OPINE-World is an LLM-based agent that learns object-centric programmatic world models online from interaction to act efficiently in unfamiliar pixel-based environments.
AI.motivation: Existing deep neural world models are flexible but require a lot of data and transfer poorly, while program-synthesized models are data-efficient but previously required pre-specified object vocabularies and didn’t scale to pixel environments. There is a need for an approach that can efficiently learn reusable, object-centric world models directly from interaction in unstructured, pixel-rendered tasks without prior knowledge of objects, goals, or action semantics.
AI.method: The authors propose OPINE-World, which runs two cooperating LLM-driven agents in a hypothesis–test loop: one interacts with the environment, and the other synthesizes and refines an object-centric programmatic world model in source code using a CEGIS-style process with replay-based verification and model-based planning. Exploration is guided by a Bayesian metric called ontology error that quantifies how adequate the current object-type ontology is, steering data collection for better model learning.
AI.result: On the ARC-AGI-3 benchmark, where object vocabularies, goals, and action semantics are not provided and tasks are pixel-rendered, OPINE-World solves 20 out of 25 games without per-game training and achieves an action-efficiency score of 78.4 relative to human performance.
AI.conclusion: An online, object-centric, program-synthesis-based world model guided by ontology error can discover and exploit reusable structure in pixel-based environments, yielding efficient skill acquisition and strong performance on previously unseen tasks without task-specific training.

title: RusFinChain: A Russian Benchmark for Verifiable Chain-of-Thought Reasoning in Finance with Fuzzy-Aligned Evaluation
summary: Multi-step symbolic reasoning is essential for robust financial analysis, yet most benchmarks neglect intermediate reasoning steps. FINCHAIN introduced verifiable Chain-of-Thought (CoT) evaluation but is limited to English. FINESSE-Bench includes a Russian block but relies on multiple-choice questions without step-level supervision. We present RusFinChain, the first Russian-language symbolic benchmark for verifiable CoT reasoning in finance. It spans 17 domains, 172 topics, and comprises 5,280 parameterized examples from executable Python templates, ensuring contamination-free evaluation. Each example includes a gold-standard reasoning chain with intermediate numeric values for automatic verification. We also introduce enhanced metrics: Fuzzy Numeric Alignment and Soft-Attention Alignment. We evaluate 8 open-weight LLMs on a stratified sample, generating 8,100 responses. Results reveal a substantial reasoning gap: models achieve Hard F1 of ~0.65 for step alignment, but only ~29% of final answers are correct. Our fuzzy and soft metrics show stronger correlation with final-answer correctness (Spearman rho approx 0.48) than the original ChainEval (rho approx 0.38-0.46), demonstrating superior diagnostic power. We release dataset, code, and evaluation framework to foster verifiable financial AI for the Russian-speaking community.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces RusFinChain, the first Russian-language benchmark for verifiable chain-of-thought reasoning in finance, plus improved metrics for evaluating step-by-step reasoning quality in LLMs.
AI.motivation: Existing financial reasoning benchmarks rarely supervise or verify intermediate reasoning steps, and those that do (like FINCHAIN) are English-only. FINESSE-Bench has Russian content but only multiple-choice format and no step-level supervision. There is a need for a Russian-language, contamination-resilient benchmark that directly evaluates symbolic, multi-step reasoning in finance with verifiable chains of thought.
AI.method: The authors construct RusFinChain, a symbolic benchmark built from executable Python templates that generate parameterized financial problems in Russian. The dataset covers 17 domains and 172 topics and yields 5,280 examples, each accompanied by a gold chain-of-thought with intermediate numeric results that can be automatically checked. They also design two new evaluation metrics—Fuzzy Numeric Alignment and Soft-Attention Alignment—to better measure how well a model’s reasoning steps align with the gold chain, beyond exact matching. They then evaluate eight open-weight LLMs on a stratified subset, collecting 8,100 model outputs and computing multiple step- and answer-level metrics and correlations between them.
AI.result: Across the evaluated models, step-level alignment appears reasonably high (Hard F1 around 0.65), yet the share of correct final answers is only about 29%, exposing a large gap between seemingly plausible reasoning steps and correct solutions. The proposed fuzzy and soft alignment metrics show higher correlation (Spearman rho around 0.48) with final-answer correctness compared to the original ChainEval metrics (rho about 0.38–0.46), indicating they are more predictive of true reasoning quality.
AI.conclusion: RusFinChain provides a novel, Russian-language, contamination-resistant benchmark for verifiable symbolic chain-of-thought reasoning in finance, filling a gap in existing evaluation resources. The enhanced fuzzy and soft alignment metrics offer superior diagnostic power over prior methods, better linking intermediate reasoning quality to final-answer correctness. The public release of dataset, code, and evaluation tools is intended to support development and assessment of more reliable financial AI systems for Russian-speaking users.

title: Scaling Trends for Lie Detector Oversight in Preference Learning
summary: Deceptive behavior in LLMs is costly to monitor and prevent, motivating approaches such as Scalable Oversight via Lie Detectors (SOLiD) (Cundy & Gleave, 2025), which uses lie detectors to identify responses for review by high-cost labelers. In this paper, we scale SOLiD to larger models and evaluate it in more diverse and realistic preference-learning settings. We find favorable scaling: undetected deception drops from 34% for 1B-parameter models to 14% for 405B-parameter models at a detector true positive rate of 99%, and expensive human labelers can be removed entirely from the fine-tuning phase without a statistically significant increase in deception. However, SOLiD is sensitive to distribution shift between detector training and preference-training data, which can drive detector false positive rates to impractical levels.

AI.tldr: The paper scales and evaluates the SOLiD framework for detecting deceptive behavior in large language models, showing improved deception detection with larger models but highlighting sensitivity to distribution shift.
AI.motivation: Monitoring and preventing deceptive behavior in large language models is expensive because it typically requires high-cost human labelers; the paper is motivated by the need for scalable, automated oversight methods that can reliably flag potentially deceptive outputs while reducing human oversight costs.
AI.method: The authors extend the SOLiD (Scalable Oversight via Lie Detectors) framework to larger models and test it in more varied, realistic preference-learning settings. They train lie detectors to identify potentially deceptive responses and measure performance across model scales, detector operating points (e.g., 99% true positive rate), and scenarios where expensive human labelers are reduced or removed. They also systematically study how distribution shift between detector-training data and preference-training data affects false positive rates and overall practicality.
AI.result: As model size increases from 1B to 405B parameters, undetected deceptive behavior at a fixed high detector true positive rate (99%) falls from 34% to 14%. The study finds that expensive human labelers can be removed from the fine-tuning phase without a statistically significant increase in deceptive behavior, suggesting that lie detectors can shoulder more of the oversight burden. However, when there is distribution shift between the data used to train the detector and the data used in preference training, false positive rates can become impractically high.
AI.conclusion: Scaling SOLiD to larger models and more realistic settings yields better deception detection and enables significant reductions in reliance on costly human oversight during fine-tuning, but the approach remains fragile to distribution shifts. For SOLiD to be practical in real-world deployments, future work must focus on making lie detectors robust to such shifts and managing the tradeoff between detection performance and false positive rates.

title: Multi-Objective Exploration and Preference Optimization via Mutual Information
summary: Aligning large language models with diverse and heterogeneous human values requires multi-objective alignment methods to effectively trade off conflicting preference dimensions. Current methods achieve this trade-off by training policies conditioned on preference vectors and leveraging online direct preference optimization. However, exploration uncertainty can cause the reward distributions of responses generated under different preference vectors to overlap, and the generated responses may fail to effectively align with the corresponding preference vectors. In this paper, we propose Multi-Objective Exploration and Preference Optimization via Mutual Information (MI-EPO), an information-theoretic framework. It unifies multi-objective exploration and alignment by maximizing the joint conditional mutual information among generated responses, preference feedback, and preference vectors. By incorporating a probabilistic routing mechanism, MI-EPO naturally decomposes objective alignment and preference-aware exploration, encouraging the model to generate responses that are distinguishable and aligned with different preference conditions. Experiments on safe alignment and helpful assistant tasks show that MI-EPO significantly improves the alignment between generated responses and preference vectors, makes the outputs more controllable, and achieves stable trade-offs across multiple objectives.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes MI-EPO, an information-theoretic method that jointly explores and aligns large language models to better follow different preference vectors in multi-objective alignment.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-objective alignment approaches for large language models condition on preference vectors but struggle when exploration uncertainty causes overlapping reward distributions, leading to responses that do not clearly reflect the intended trade-offs among conflicting human values.
AI.method: Introduce MI-EPO, a framework that maximizes the joint conditional mutual information between model responses, human preference feedback, and preference vectors. It uses a probabilistic routing mechanism to separate objective alignment from preference-aware exploration, guiding the model to generate responses that are both distinguishable and aligned under different preference conditions.
AI.result: On safe alignment and helpful assistant benchmarks, MI-EPO produces responses that align more strongly with the specified preference vectors, increases controllability of outputs, and delivers more stable trade-offs among multiple objectives than prior methods.
AI.conclusion: Maximizing mutual information between responses, feedback, and preference vectors, combined with probabilistic routing, effectively unifies exploration and multi-objective alignment, yielding better controllability and more reliable balancing of heterogeneous human values in large language models.

title: EO-Agents: A Three-Agent LLM Pipeline for Earth Observation Hypothesis Generation
comment: Accepted at the ICML 2026 AI for Science Workshop
summary: Large language models have recently been explored for scientific hypothesis generation, but most prior work relies on unstructured literature and free-form textual claims. We present a pipeline for Earth observation that grounds hypothesis generation directly in the NASA Earth Observation Knowledge Graph. A heterogeneous graph neural network trained on historical co-usage relations ranks candidate dataset pairings, and a three-agent LLM pipeline filters, generates, and evaluates structured research hypotheses. Applied to 1,475 NASA datasets, the system produces 160 hypotheses spanning multiple Earth-science domains, including ecohydrology, glaciology, aerosol--cloud interactions, vegetation phenology, and stratospheric chemistry. Model-predicted novel dataset pairings are rated nearly as plausible as held-out real co-usages from the literature, indicating that the pipeline surfaces scientifically coherent yet unexplored combinations. A 2*2*2 factorial experiment across GPT-5.2 and Claude Sonnet 4.6 shows that hypothesis rankings remain stable, while absolute scores depend strongly on judge identity, highlighting limitations of single-judge LLM evaluation.

AI.tldr: They build a graph-based and LLM-based pipeline that generates and scores structured Earth science research hypotheses grounded in NASA’s Earth Observation Knowledge Graph, showing it can suggest plausible yet unexplored dataset combinations and exposing instability in LLM-based judging.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM-driven scientific hypothesis generation mostly works from unstructured text and unconstrained, free-form claims, which can be ungrounded and hard to evaluate. In Earth observation, there is a rich, curated NASA Earth Observation Knowledge Graph and historical records of which datasets are used together in papers. The authors want to leverage this structured resource to systematically propose novel, coherent combinations of Earth-observation datasets that might support new science, and to do so in a way that is both scalable and evaluable.
AI.method: 1) Represent NASA Earth observation resources as a heterogeneous knowledge graph, including datasets and their historical co-usage in the literature. 2) Train a heterogeneous graph neural network (GNN) to predict and rank candidate dataset pairings based on historical co-usage patterns. 3) Feed high-ranked candidate pairings to a three-agent LLM pipeline in which (a) one agent filters and structures candidate problems, (b) another generates detailed, structured research hypotheses using the dataset pairing, and (c) a third agent evaluates the hypotheses. 4) Apply the pipeline to 1,475 NASA datasets and collect the resulting hypotheses. 5) Evaluate plausibility of generated hypotheses and dataset pairings by comparing model-predicted novel pairings against held-out real co-usages, including with a 2×2×2 factorial experiment varying LLM model (GPT-5.2 vs Claude Sonnet 4.6) and judge configurations to probe robustness of LLM-based evaluation.
AI.result: From 1,475 NASA datasets, the system generates 160 structured hypotheses across diverse Earth science areas such as ecohydrology, glaciology, aerosol–cloud interactions, vegetation phenology, and stratospheric chemistry. Novel dataset pairings suggested by the GNN are rated by LLM judges as nearly as plausible as actual dataset pairings observed in the literature but withheld from training, suggesting the method recovers scientifically sensible yet previously unused combinations. The factorial evaluation shows that while the relative ranking of hypotheses is fairly stable across models and judge setups, the absolute plausibility scores are highly sensitive to which LLM serves as the judge.
AI.conclusion: Grounding hypothesis generation in a structured resource—the NASA Earth Observation Knowledge Graph—combined with a heterogeneous GNN and a multi-agent LLM pipeline can propose scientifically coherent, novel dataset pairings and research hypotheses in Earth observation. The approach appears capable of surfacing unexplored but plausible directions across multiple subfields. However, the sensitivity of absolute evaluation scores to the choice of LLM judge exposes a key limitation: relying on a single LLM for hypothesis evaluation can be misleading, motivating more robust, multi-judge or human-in-the-loop evaluation strategies.

title: MultAttnAttrib: Training-Free Multimodal Attribution in Long Document Question Answering
comment: 25 pages (8 main, 17 references + appendix), 15 figures, Submitted to EMNLP 2026 Conference (Long Paper)
summary: As grounded QA systems are increasingly deployed in AI assistants, accurately attributing generated answers to evidence is critical for user trust and model safety. While unimodal attributions have been explored in depth, the multimodal setting remains relatively under-researched. As a result, we introduce MultAttnAttrib, a training-free attribution-generation method that leverages a model's prefill pass, selected attention heads, and calibrated thresholds to locate source evidence within a document. To establish baseline results for the method, we introduce MultAttrEval, a complementary benchmark dataset annotated with fine-grained, ground-truth attributions for answer components grounded in multimodal source documents. To our knowledge, this is the first evaluation dataset designed specifically for multimodal attribution in long-form documents. Experimental results show that MultAttnAttrib consistently outperforms a variety of attribution-generation methods, including several strong prompting-based approaches and matches the latest frontier models such as GPT 5.4. Our method not only substantially improves attribution accuracy for both unimodal and multimodal attribution types, but also produces attributions at up to one-seventh of the direct inference latency compared to prompting on the same base model.

AI.tldr: The paper presents MultAttnAttrib, a training-free method to attribute answers from multimodal QA systems to specific evidence in source documents, and MultAttrEval, a new benchmark for evaluating fine-grained multimodal attribution, showing improved accuracy and lower latency over prior methods and strong LLMs.
AI.motivation: Grounded QA systems in AI assistants must accurately show which parts of source documents support each piece of an answer to ensure user trust and safety. Existing work mainly handles unimodal (e.g., text-only) attribution, leaving multimodal attribution, especially for long-form documents combining text and other media, relatively unexplored. There is also a lack of standardized benchmarks to evaluate fine-grained multimodal attribution quality.
AI.method: They propose MultAttnAttrib, a training-free attribution-generation technique that uses the model’s prefill pass and carefully selected attention heads, combined with calibrated thresholds, to identify where in a multimodal source document (e.g., text and other modalities) each part of an answer comes from. They also construct MultAttrEval, a benchmark dataset containing multimodal source documents and detailed, ground-truth alignments between answer components and their supporting evidence spans, enabling systematic evaluation of attribution methods.
AI.result: Experiments on MultAttrEval show that MultAttnAttrib consistently outperforms various existing attribution methods, including strong prompting-based baselines, and reaches performance comparable to state-of-the-art frontier models (e.g., GPT 5.4) on attribution tasks. It improves attribution accuracy for both unimodal and multimodal evidence types while being more efficient, reducing attribution latency to as little as one-seventh of the latency required by prompting-based methods on the same base model.
AI.conclusion: MultAttnAttrib offers an effective, training-free way to generate accurate attributions for grounded multimodal QA, and MultAttrEval provides the first dedicated benchmark for this problem in long-form documents. Together, they demonstrate that leveraging internal attention patterns during the prefill phase can yield high-quality, low-latency attributions, advancing the reliability and efficiency of multimodal grounded AI assistants.

title: Hawk: Harnessing Hardware-Aware Knowledge for High-Performance NPU Kernel Generation
summary: Developing high-performance kernels for Neural Processing Units (NPUs) is a critical industry bottleneck, requiring developers to manually navigate implicit hardware constraints and strict memory hierarchies. While large language models offer immense automation potential, they fail catastrophically on NPUs due to a fundamental lack of hardware-specific priors. Naively transplanting code snippets from similar NPU kernels may pass the compiler, but it consistently triggers runtime crashes and performance degradation by blindly violating underlying hardware constraints. To overcome this, we introduce Hawk, a training-free framework that harnesses hardware-aware knowledge through three core modules: (1) Run-Time Knowledge Synthesis Module, which employs a Triple-Part Executable Knowledge Representation to inherently couple the error context with executable semantics; (2) Bottleneck-Aware Knowledge Retrieval Module, which implements a 2D-Retrieval paradigm to project queries into orthogonal syntactic and hardware-aligned semantic spaces; and (3) Effect-Driven Knowledge Distillation Module, which leverages LLM-driven semantic arbitration to continuously distill the knowledge by pruning errors and consolidating redundancies based on the empirical execution feedback. Extensive evaluations on real-world NPU workloads demonstrate that Hawk elevates generation accuracy from 49.4% to 80.0%, while achieving up to a 2.2x execution speedup over state-of-the-art baselines.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes Hawk, a training-free, hardware-aware framework that uses runtime feedback and structured knowledge to make large language models reliably generate efficient, correct NPU kernels, significantly improving correctness and speed over existing methods.
AI.motivation: Developing high-performance kernels for NPUs is difficult and time-consuming because developers must manually deal with implicit hardware constraints and complex memory hierarchies. Existing large language models lack NPU-specific hardware priors, so they often generate code that compiles but fails at runtime or performs poorly. There is a need for an automated approach that is intrinsically aware of hardware constraints and can iteratively improve kernel generation using execution feedback.
AI.method: Hawk is a training-free framework built around three modules. (1) The Run-Time Knowledge Synthesis Module creates a Triple-Part Executable Knowledge Representation that tightly links error context, code semantics, and execution behavior, turning runtime signals into structured knowledge. (2) The Bottleneck-Aware Knowledge Retrieval Module uses a 2D retrieval scheme, projecting queries into both syntactic space (code patterns) and hardware-aligned semantic space (performance and constraints), so the system can retrieve the most relevant prior cases. (3) The Effect-Driven Knowledge Distillation Module uses an LLM to arbitrate and refine the knowledge base by pruning error-inducing patterns and merging redundant entries, guided by empirical runtime and performance feedback. The whole process does not require retraining the LLM, instead leveraging structured retrieval and feedback-driven refinement.
AI.result: On real NPU workloads, Hawk improves the kernel generation accuracy from 49.4% to 80.0% and achieves up to 2.2x execution speedup compared with state-of-the-art methods. These results show both higher correctness (fewer runtime failures) and better performance (more efficient use of NPU hardware).
AI.conclusion: Hawk demonstrates that a training-free, hardware-aware framework that integrates runtime feedback, structured knowledge representation, and dual-space retrieval can effectively bridge the gap between generic LLMs and specialized NPU kernel development. By encoding hardware constraints into an evolving knowledge base and guiding LLM generation with it, Hawk substantially boosts both correctness and performance of NPU kernels, offering a practical path to automate kernel optimization for specialized hardware.

title: IsoSci: A Benchmark of Isomorphic Cross-Domain Science Problems for Evaluating Reasoning versus Knowledge Retrieval in LLMs
summary: We introduce ISOSCI, a benchmark of isomorphic cross-domain science problem pairs that separates reasoning ability from domain knowledge retrieval in LLM evaluation. Each pair shares identical logical structure but requires different domain-specific knowledge, enabling controlled attribution of reasoning-mode gains. Across five model pairs spanning four model families, we find that 91.3% of reasoning-mode gains are knowledge-dependent rather than structure-invariant (63/69 gains; Wilson 95% CI [82.3%, 96.0%]), directly challenging the assumption that chain-of-thought reasoning improves short-horizon procedural scientific problem-solving. Reasoning toggles on highly capable models provide less than 5 percentage points accuracy gain across all domains, and a reasoning-specialized model (o3-mini) that outperforms its standard counterpart on GPQA Diamond (+19.2 percentage points) underperforms on ISOSCI (-24.7 percentage points), showing that benchmark choice determines conclusions about reasoning utility. We release ISOSCI at https://huggingface.co/datasets/isosci/isosci

AI.tldr: ISOSCI is a benchmark of isomorphic science problem pairs designed to isolate reasoning from domain knowledge in LLM evaluation, showing that most observed gains from reasoning modes are actually knowledge-dependent and highly benchmark-sensitive.
AI.motivation: Existing evaluations often conflate improvements from chain-of-thought or reasoning modes with improvements in domain knowledge recall, making it unclear whether LLMs are genuinely better at reasoning or simply better at retrieving and applying facts. The authors want a way to disentangle these factors in scientific problem-solving tasks and to test claims that reasoning modes significantly help short-horizon procedural reasoning.
AI.method: They construct ISOSCI, a dataset of cross-domain scientific problem pairs that are isomorphic in logical structure but rely on distinct domain-specific knowledge. By comparing model performance across these isomorphic pairs under different reasoning configurations (e.g., standard vs reasoning-toggled, or standard vs reasoning-specialized models) across multiple model families, they attribute performance gains either to structure-invariant reasoning or to domain-specific knowledge. Statistical analysis (including Wilson confidence intervals) quantifies how often gains are knowledge versus structure driven.
AI.result: Across five model pairs from four families, 91.3% of the observed gains when enabling reasoning modes are attributed to domain-specific knowledge rather than structure-invariant reasoning (63 of 69 gains; Wilson 95% CI [82.3%, 96.0%]). Reasoning toggles on strong models add less than 5 percentage points of accuracy across domains. A reasoning-specialized model (o3-mini) that shows large gains on GPQA Diamond (+19.2 points) actually performs substantially worse on ISOSCI (-24.7 points) compared to its standard counterpart, indicating strong dependence on benchmark choice.
AI.conclusion: Most apparent benefits of reasoning modes in LLMs for scientific problem-solving stem from improved domain knowledge rather than generalizable reasoning structure, and conclusions about reasoning utility are highly sensitive to the benchmark used. ISOSCI provides a controlled setting to more accurately separate and evaluate reasoning vs knowledge contributions, challenging common claims that chain-of-thought universally improves short-horizon procedural reasoning tasks.

title: Safe and Adaptive Cloud Healing: Verifying LLM-Generated Recovery Plans with a Neural-Symbolic World Model
summary: As the scale and complexity of cloud-based AI systems continue to escalate, ensuring service reliability through rapid fault detection and adaptive recovery has become a critical challenge. While existing approaches integrate Large Language Models (LLMs) for semantic understanding and Deep Reinforcement Learning (DRL) for policy optimization, they often rely on sequential, loosely coupled architectures that underutilize the generative and reasoning capabilities of LLMs. In this paper, we propose a paradigm shift with PASE, a Planning-Aware Semantic self-healing engine, a novel fault self-healing framework that reconceptualizes recovery as a neuro-symbolic program synthesis task. PASE employs an LLM as a core Plan Synthesis Engine to generate structured recovery plans from a library of semantic primitives. A Neural-Symbolic World Model verifies plan feasibility through simulation, while a Meta-Prompt Optimizer, trained via DRL, learns to generate optimal prompts that guide the LLM's planning process. This tight reason-plan-verify-adapt loop enables dynamic, context-aware recovery strategy generation beyond predefined action spaces. Experiments on a real-world cloud fault injection dataset demonstrate that PASE significantly outperforms state-of-the-art methods, reducing average system recovery time by over 40% and improving fault detection accuracy in unknown fault scenarios. Our framework advances autonomous system management by unifying LLM-based reasoning with model-assisted verification and meta-learned guidance.

AI.tldr: PASE is a new self-healing framework for cloud AI systems that treats fault recovery as a neuro-symbolic program synthesis problem, tightly coupling LLM-based planning, world-model verification, and DRL-based meta-prompt optimization to achieve faster and more accurate recovery than prior methods.
AI.motivation: Cloud-based AI systems are growing in scale and complexity, making it harder to ensure reliability with fast fault detection and adaptive recovery. Existing methods that combine LLMs and DRL are architecturally loose and sequential, which underutilizes LLMs’ planning and generative strengths and limits flexibility to predefined action spaces. The authors aim to build a more integrated, autonomous self-healing mechanism that can handle unknown faults more effectively.
AI.method: They introduce PASE, a Planning-Aware Semantic self-healing engine. PASE uses: (1) an LLM as a Plan Synthesis Engine that composes recovery plans from a library of semantic primitives; (2) a Neural-Symbolic World Model that simulates and verifies the feasibility of candidate plans; and (3) a Meta-Prompt Optimizer trained via deep reinforcement learning to automatically learn prompts that best steer the LLM’s planning. These components are coupled in a closed-loop reason–plan–verify–adapt cycle that iteratively generates, checks, and refines recovery strategies.
AI.result: On a real-world cloud fault injection dataset, PASE substantially outperforms state-of-the-art self-healing approaches: it reduces average recovery time by more than 40% and improves fault detection accuracy, particularly for previously unseen fault types.
AI.conclusion: By formulating recovery as neuro-symbolic program synthesis and tightly integrating LLM reasoning with model-based verification and DRL-driven prompt optimization, PASE enables dynamic, context-aware self-healing that goes beyond fixed action spaces. This unified framework advances autonomous cloud system management, offering faster and more accurate fault detection and recovery in complex, real-world environments.

title: FaithMed: Training LLMs For Faithful Evidence-Based Medical Reasoning
summary: Faithful reasoning is essential in medicine, where clinical decisions require transparent justification grounded in reliable evidence. Current medical LLMs either lack active access to evidence or use retrieved evidence without supervising how it should be appraised and applied during reasoning. To address this, we formalize evidence-based medicine principles as process-level criteria and introduce FaithMed, a framework that combines clinician-designed, automatically refined rubrics with reinforcement learning using step-level process reward assignment and advantage grouping. Across seven medical benchmarks, FaithMed improves over agentic-search baselines (+9% on average) and outcome-only RL (+5.8%), while raising average evidence-based medicine rubric scores over agentic-search Qwen3 baselines (+15.5%). This work demonstrates that explicit step-level supervision can improve both task success and the faithfulness of the reasoning process. Code is available at https://github.com/cxcscmu/FaithMed.

AI.tldr: The paper presents FaithMed, a reinforcement learning framework that uses explicit, step-level supervision based on evidence-based medicine rubrics to make medical LLM reasoning more faithful and effective.
AI.motivation: Medical decision-making requires not just correct answers but transparent, evidence-grounded reasoning. Existing medical LLMs either do not actively retrieve evidence or, when they do, are not supervised on how to evaluate and use that evidence through the reasoning process, leading to unfaithful or opaque chains of thought.
AI.method: The authors formalize core principles of evidence-based medicine into process-level criteria, encoded as clinician-designed rubrics that are then automatically refined. They integrate these rubrics into a reinforcement learning scheme that assigns rewards at the step level of reasoning (process reward assignment) and uses advantage grouping to better optimize the model’s reasoning trajectory. The resulting system, FaithMed, guides LLMs to appraise and apply retrieved evidence explicitly during multi-step reasoning.
AI.result: On seven medical benchmarks, FaithMed outperforms strong agentic-search baselines by about 9% on average and outcome-only RL approaches by about 5.8%. It also yields substantially higher evidence-based medicine rubric scores (+15.5% over agentic-search Qwen3 baselines), indicating more faithful, guideline-aligned reasoning.
AI.conclusion: Incorporating explicit, step-level supervision derived from evidence-based medicine principles into RL training improves both the accuracy of medical LLMs and the faithfulness of their reasoning, showing that structured process supervision is valuable for high-stakes clinical applications. The authors release code to facilitate further research.

title: SemHash-LLM: A Multi-Granularity Semantic Hashing Framework for Document Deduplication
summary: Large scale document deduplication must preserve semantic equivalence while remaining efficient over massive corpora. We present SemHash LLM, a multi granularity framework that unifies semantic projection hashing, attention weighted MinHash, contrastive boundary learning, and selective LLM based adjudication. The method combines character, token, and document level signals through gated fusion, then applies a cascaded filtering pipeline for efficient candidate reduction. Semantic projection hashing learns compact binary codes in distilled LLM embedding space, while attention weighted Min- Hash suppresses boilerplate and emphasizes informative content. Adaptive decision boundaries and uncertainty estimation further improve robustness across template pollution, short text perturbation, containment, and viral fragments. Experiments show that SemHash LLM achieves strong duplicate detection quality with less than one percent neural verification cost.

AI.tldr: SemHash LLM is a multi‑stage, multi‑granularity system for large‑scale semantic deduplication that combines hashing, MinHash, contrastive learning, and selective LLM checks to find near‑duplicates efficiently.
AI.motivation: Existing large‑scale document deduplication methods struggle to simultaneously capture semantic equivalence, handle noisy or templated content, and remain computationally efficient across massive corpora. Traditional hashing or MinHash can miss semantic near‑duplicates or be distorted by boilerplate and templates, while full neural or LLM-based approaches are too expensive to run on every document pair. The paper aims to design a framework that preserves semantic fidelity, is robust to various types of text variation (e.g., templates, viral snippets, short perturbations), and scales with very low neural verification cost.
AI.method: The paper introduces SemHash LLM, a multi‑granularity framework that fuses character-level, token-level, and document-level signals via a gated fusion mechanism. It uses semantic projection hashing to learn compact binary codes derived from distilled LLM embeddings, enabling fast approximate similarity search. It incorporates attention-weighted MinHash to down‑weight boilerplate and highlight informative sections for set-similarity style deduplication. A cascaded filtering pipeline sequentially prunes candidate pairs using cheap signals first and more expensive ones later. Contrastive boundary learning is applied to learn adaptive decision boundaries between duplicate and non-duplicate pairs, while uncertainty estimation guides selective LLM-based adjudication only for ambiguous cases, greatly limiting LLM calls.
AI.result: Experiments indicate that SemHash LLM attains strong performance on duplicate detection benchmarks, robustly handling template pollution, short text perturbations, containment relations, and viral content fragments. It reaches this quality while requiring neural (LLM-based) verification on less than 1% of candidate pairs, demonstrating high scalability and efficiency compared to baselines that rely more heavily on neural or LLM inference for pairwise comparisons.
AI.conclusion: SemHash LLM offers an effective and scalable solution for semantic document deduplication at web scale by integrating semantic hashing, attention-aware MinHash, contrastive decision boundary learning, and targeted LLM adjudication in a cascaded pipeline. The approach preserves semantic equivalence detection across challenging noise and variation patterns while keeping the cost of neural verification extremely low, making it practical for large corpora and industrial settings.

title: Grounded Optimization: A Layered Engineering Framework for Reducing LLM Hallucination in Automated Personal Document Rewriting
comment: 13 pages, 1 figure. Equal contribution by both authors. Code and data: https://github.com/shashank-indukuri/grounded-optimization
summary:
Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly applied to resume optimization for applicant tracking systems, introducing hallucination failures distinct from general text generation: anachronistic technology injection, cross-domain terminology contamination, structural mutation, and content fabrication. We present Grounded Optimization, a five-layer framework combining temporal context validation, deterministic contamination detection, structural invariant enforcement, prompt-level grounding, and an evaluator agent.
  In ablation experiments across three LLMs, four temperature settings, and six layer configurations on 25 synthetic resumes spanning 14 industries, undefended baselines produce 2.48-5.36 detected hallucinations per resume. Among detectors independent of the active defenses, temporal hallucinations are reduced by 50-95% across all conditions; overall detected hallucination rate falls to 0.04-0.24. Prompt-level grounding alone achieves zero detected hallucinations at low temperature with a capable instruction-following model; higher temperatures and weaker models reveal the need for the deterministic layers as a complement. We release the contamination taxonomy, evaluation code, and raw data.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a multi-layer "Grounded Optimization" framework to reduce hallucinations when LLMs optimize resumes for applicant tracking systems, achieving large reductions in temporal and overall hallucinations across models and temperatures.
AI.motivation: When LLMs rewrite resumes for applicant tracking systems, they often introduce domain-specific hallucinations unlike generic text generation errors, such as adding impossible technologies for given dates, mixing terminology from different fields, distorting resume structure, or fabricating content. Existing hallucination work focuses on general text and does not target these resume-specific failure modes, so there is a need for a tailored, systematic defense framework that keeps outputs faithful to the original resume while still optimizing for ATS compatibility.
AI.method: The authors define a taxonomy of resume-specific hallucination types—anachronistic technology injection, cross-domain terminology contamination, structural mutation, and content fabrication—and design a five-layer framework called Grounded Optimization. The layers include: (1) temporal context validation to check date-technology consistency; (2) deterministic contamination detection to ensure domain coherence; (3) structural invariant enforcement to preserve key resume structure; (4) prompt-level grounding that constrains the model closely to original content; and (5) an evaluator agent that detects residual hallucinations. They run ablation experiments varying which layers are active, across three different LLMs, four temperature settings, and six layer configurations, using 25 synthetic resumes from 14 industries, and measure hallucinations with independent detectors.
AI.result: On undefended baselines, the models produce 2.48–5.36 detected hallucinations per resume. With Grounded Optimization layers active, temporally-related hallucinations drop by 50–95% in all tested settings, and overall hallucination rates fall to 0.04–0.24 per resume according to detectors that do not share logic with the defenses. Prompt-level grounding alone can eliminate detected hallucinations when using a strong instruction-following model at low temperature, but at higher temperatures or with weaker models, the deterministic layers (temporal checks, contamination detection, structural constraints) are necessary to maintain low hallucination rates.
AI.conclusion: Grounded Optimization is an effective, modular defense framework against hallucinations specific to resume optimization by LLMs. It dramatically reduces both temporal and overall hallucination rates across models and sampling temperatures, and shows that careful prompt-level grounding can suffice in favorable conditions, whereas deterministic validation and enforcement layers are crucial in more challenging ones. The work also contributes a contamination taxonomy, evaluation toolkit, and dataset that support further research on trustworthy, grounded resume rewriting with LLMs.

title: Profit-Based Counterfactual Explanations for Product Improvement: A Case Study of Manga Sales in Japan
summary: Counterfactual explanation (CE) is widely used to enhance the interpretability of machine learning models and support data-driven decision-making based on model predictions. However, existing CE methods typically require two exogenously specified inputs: a desired output value (target) and a distance function that quantifies changes in explanatory variables. In regression settings, neither the validity of target specification nor the practical interpretation of the distance metric has been sufficiently addressed. Furthermore, most existing CE methods focus on altering predictions rather than optimizing a decision objective, even though real-world decision-making often requires explicit objective maximization. To address these limitations, we formulate CE as a profit maximization problem in management and marketing contexts and propose a framework termed profit-based counterfactual explanation (PBCE). PBCE eliminates the need for exogenous target specification by directly maximizing profit as the primary optimization objective. Concurrently, the distance term is reinterpreted as the cost of modifying product attributes, providing a clear and economically grounded interpretation.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes profit-based counterfactual explanation (PBCE), a framework that integrates counterfactual explanations with explicit profit maximization, eliminating arbitrary target outputs and giving an economic meaning to the distance term as cost of changing attributes.
AI.motivation: Existing counterfactual explanation methods for machine learning require specifying a desired target output and an abstract distance metric between instances. In regression and real decision-making contexts, these are hard to justify: targets may be arbitrary, and distances lack practical interpretation. Moreover, most CE methods focus on changing predictions rather than directly optimizing the real business or managerial objective, such as profit. The authors aim to make counterfactual explanations more aligned with managerial decision-making, especially in marketing and product management, by grounding them in profit optimization and economically meaningful costs.
AI.method: The authors reformulate counterfactual explanation as an optimization problem where the objective is to maximize expected profit instead of achieving a pre-specified prediction target. They reinterpret the usual distance regularizer as the monetary cost of modifying explanatory variables, such as product attributes. Within this PBCE framework, a decision-maker adjusts features subject to costs and constraints to find counterfactual configurations that improve profit, using the predictive model as part of the profit function rather than as the sole optimization target. The approach is tailored to management and marketing settings, though exact algorithms are not detailed in the abstract.
AI.result: The PBCE framework removes the need to choose an exogenous target output for counterfactuals and embeds an interpretable, cost-based notion of distance between instances. It yields counterfactual recommendations that are explicitly tied to profit improvements, showing how changing certain variables (e.g., product attributes) affects both model predictions and economic outcomes. This leads to more actionable, business-oriented explanations compared to traditional CE methods.
AI.conclusion: By formulating counterfactual explanations as a profit maximization problem, PBCE aligns machine learning interpretability with managerial objectives. It replaces arbitrary target specification with direct profit optimization and redefines the distance term as a concrete cost of intervention. This makes counterfactual explanations more interpretable, economically grounded, and practically useful for decision-making in management and marketing contexts.

title: Comparing Architectures for Supervised Political Scaling
summary: Text scaling, the task of positioning political actors on an ideological scale, is a fundamental task in political analysis. To ease the need for manual analysis, various NLP methods have been proposed for this task, including classification- and regression-based approaches, showing successes as well as limitations. The goal of our paper is to consolidate the state of the art in this area. We ask two questions: (a) Can the performance of scaling methods be improved by predicting scales not individually but jointly? (b) Is there a middle ground between classification and regression?

AI.tldr: The paper studies how to better estimate ideological positions of political actors from text using NLP, focusing on joint prediction of multiple scales and exploring models between classification and regression.
AI.motivation: Text-based estimation of political ideology (text scaling) is important but current NLP approaches, mainly pure classification or pure regression, have limitations in accuracy and robustness. There is a need to consolidate and improve these methods.
AI.method: The paper conceptually and empirically compares text scaling approaches, introducing or evaluating models that jointly predict multiple ideological scales and that interpolate between classification and regression paradigms.
AI.result: Joint prediction of ideological scales and intermediate models between classification and regression can improve the performance of text scaling over existing methods that treat scales independently and use only one paradigm.
AI.conclusion: More sophisticated NLP models that jointly learn multiple ideological dimensions and use hybrid classification–regression formulations offer a stronger state of the art for text-based ideological scaling of political actors.

title: Scaling with Confidence: Calibrating Confidence of LLMs for Adaptive Test Time Scaling
summary: Training large language models (LLMs) with reinforcement learning (RL) has significantly advanced their performance on reasoning and question-answering tasks. However, prevailing RL reward designs typically prioritize response correctness, neglecting to incentivize models to express their confidence accurately. This leads to a critical problem: performance gains are often accompanied by poor calibration between confidence and accuracy, misleading models to overconfidently hallucinate when uncertain. To address this limitation, we propose $\textbf{C}$orrectness and $\textbf{C}$onfidence $\textbf{C}$alibration $\textbf{R}$einforcement $\textbf{L}$earning ($\textbf{C3RL}$), a novel RL algorithm integrating correctness, calibration and dataset-informed reference accuracy rewards together. Comprehensive evaluation across 8 text and multimodal datasets demonstrates that C3RL enhances calibration without sacrificing accuracy, outperforming the current state-of-the-art method in both performance and calibration metrics. Utilizing the well-calibrated verbalized confidence from C3RL, we further introduce $\textbf{C}$onfidence-based $\textbf{A}$daptive Test Time $\textbf{S}$caling ($\textbf{CAS}$), an adjustable inference-time strategy that allocates computational resources based on response confidence. Experiments show that CAS surpasses majority voting on both in-domain and out-of-domain datasets while reducing the inference budget by up to 12.33 times. We believe the synergy of C3RL and CAS paves the way for deploying more reliable and resource-efficient LLMs. The code, data and models will be released.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes C3RL, a reinforcement learning framework that jointly optimizes answer correctness and confidence calibration, and CAS, a confidence-based test-time scaling strategy that uses calibrated verbalized confidence to allocate compute more efficiently.
AI.motivation: Existing RL training for LLMs mostly optimizes for correctness of responses but ignores whether the model’s expressed confidence matches its true accuracy. This leads to overconfident hallucinations and poor calibration, undermining reliability and downstream decision-making. There is a need for training and inference strategies that make LLMs not only accurate but also honestly calibrated about their uncertainty, and that can leverage this calibrated confidence to save computation.
AI.method: They design C3RL, an RL algorithm whose reward combines three components: (1) correctness of the model’s answer, (2) calibration between predicted/verbalized confidence and actual correctness, and (3) a dataset-informed reference accuracy term. This encourages models to express well-calibrated confidence while remaining accurate. Using the calibrated verbalized confidence produced by C3RL-trained models, they then propose CAS, a confidence-based adaptive test-time scaling strategy that varies inference-time compute (e.g., number of samples or decoding budget) conditioned on the model’s confidence, instead of using fixed majority voting or fixed sampling schemes.
AI.result: Across 8 text and multimodal benchmarks, C3RL improves calibration metrics while maintaining or improving task accuracy, outperforming prior state-of-the-art calibration-oriented methods on both fronts. CAS, when driven by C3RL’s calibrated confidence estimates, beats majority voting on in-domain and out-of-domain evaluations while reducing inference cost by up to a factor of 12.33, indicating large computational savings with equal or better performance.
AI.conclusion: Jointly optimizing correctness and confidence calibration via C3RL produces LLMs that are both accurate and better aligned between confidence and reality. Leveraging these calibrated confidence estimates at inference time with CAS enables adaptive allocation of compute, yielding more efficient and reliable deployment. Together, C3RL and CAS demonstrate that well-calibrated confidence can be a powerful signal for both safety (reducing overconfident hallucinations) and efficiency (reducing inference budget), suggesting a practical path toward trustworthy, resource-aware LLM systems.

title: From Monolingual to Multilingual: Evaluating Mamba for ASR in South African Languages
summary: Recent advances in automatic speech recognition (ASR) have explored different sequence models, including Conformer-based models and newer state space models such as Mamba. Although prior work has evaluated these architectures in multiple languages, their effectiveness in African languages remains underexplored. In this work, we evaluate Mamba for ASR on seven South African languages. In monolingual experiments, each model is trained on 50 hours of speech per language, and we compare Mamba to a Conformer baseline of similar parameter scale. Mamba achieves similar recognition accuracy to Conformer while using fewer computational resources and training faster. We further evaluate generalization in this setting and find that both models struggle to generalize to speech that is much longer than what they were trained on. We then study multilingual ASR using Mamba models, where the baseline is pooling all languages together. On top of this, we tested three extensions: training with language-family information by adding both language and language-family embeddings as biases to the downsampled acoustic representations, and multitask learning with a CTC ASR objective and a language identification (LID) head. We find that multilingual training consistently improves performance over monolingual training. However, adding explicit language information does not improve in-domain performance but does improve cross-corpus robustness. We conducted ablation studies in low-resource multilingual settings using 5-hour and 10-hour per-language training data, where we observed gains from using language embeddings and further demonstrated that removing or altering them hurt model performance. Lastly, we analysed these embeddings and find that they do not capture linguistic similarity in a typological sense, but instead act as task-specific control vectors.

AI.tldr: The paper compares Mamba state space models with Conformer models for ASR in seven South African languages, explores multilingual and low-resource setups, and analyzes the role of language and language-family embeddings.
AI.motivation: ASR architectures like Conformer and Mamba have been tested broadly, but their performance on underrepresented African languages, especially in multilingual and low-resource settings, is not well understood. The authors aim to fill this gap and understand how such models generalize and how explicit language information affects robustness.
AI.method: They train monolingual ASR models (Mamba and a similarly sized Conformer) on 50 hours per language for seven South African languages, then evaluate accuracy, compute efficiency, and length generalization. For multilingual ASR, they pool all languages and use Mamba as baseline, then add language and language-family embeddings as biases to downsampled acoustic representations and introduce multitask learning with CTC for ASR plus a language ID head. They also run ablation studies with only 5 or 10 hours per language to test the impact of language embeddings.
AI.result: Mamba matches Conformer’s recognition accuracy while being more computationally efficient and faster to train. Both models fail to generalize well to speech much longer than the training utterances. Multilingual Mamba consistently outperforms monolingual training. Adding explicit language and language-family embeddings does not improve in-domain accuracy but enhances robustness across corpora. In low-resource settings, language embeddings provide clear gains, and removing or modifying them degrades performance. Embedding analysis shows they do not encode typological similarity but function as task-specific control vectors.
AI.conclusion: Mamba is a competitive alternative to Conformer for ASR in South African languages, offering similar accuracy with lower computational cost, but both architectures struggle with long utterances. Multilingual training is beneficial, and language-related embeddings are especially useful in low-resource and cross-corpus scenarios, acting more as control signals than as representations of linguistic typology.

title: Spatial Support Matters: Geometry-Aware Graph Fusion for Rainfall Field Reconstruction
summary: Fine-scale rainfall reconstruction is critical for urban flood modeling, but real rainfall sensing systems observe the field through incompatible spatial supports: gauges measure points, microwave links measure paths, and radar/satellite products measure gridded areas. These differences in measurement support impose geometrically distinct constraints on the rainfall field, yet existing heterogeneous graph approaches reconcile such sources in feature space, giving each its own embedding while discarding the geometry of its support. We propose a geometry-aware multi-support heterogeneous graph neural network that represents each observation according to its support type (0D point, 1D line, or 2D grid) as a distinct node layer, and fuses them through cross-support message passing into a point-support prediction layer from which the field is reconstructed. An inductive masked-node formulation decouples prediction resolution from sensing resolution, allowing the same trained model to reconstruct the field at user-defined target locations or display grids. On Singapore data, the proposed method reduces RMSE by 23.2\% over the classical interpolation baseline, inverse-distance weighting, and consistently outperforms other neural architectures such as convolutional fusion and support-agnostic heterogeneous graph baselines. A generalization study using data from Sydney, Australia lets us characterize when multi-support fusion helps: the available skill appears to depend on gauge spacing relative to the spatial correlation length of the field, so fusion delivers the largest gains where the field is under-sampled relative to its correlation length and little when it is already resolved. Code and models will be open-sourced upon paper acceptance.

AI.tldr: Geometry-aware multi-support GNN to fuse point, line, and grid rainfall observations for fine-scale rainfall field reconstruction, outperforming baselines and characterizing when multi-support fusion is beneficial.
AI.motivation: Urban flood modeling requires high-resolution rainfall fields, but available sensing systems (gauges, microwave links, radar/satellite) observe rainfall on incompatible spatial supports (points, paths, grids). Existing heterogeneous graph approaches fuse these sources in feature space but ignore the geometric nature of the supports, potentially losing important structural information. There is a need for a method that explicitly respects and leverages these geometric support differences while allowing flexible reconstruction at arbitrary resolutions.
AI.method: The paper introduces a geometry-aware multi-support heterogeneous graph neural network. Observations are organized into node layers based on support type: 0D point nodes for gauges, 1D line nodes for microwave links, and 2D grid nodes for radar/satellite products. Cross-support message passing fuses information from these layers into a dedicated point-support prediction layer used to reconstruct the continuous rainfall field. An inductive masked-node formulation lets the network decouple prediction resolution from sensor resolution so that, after training, the model can reconstruct rainfall at arbitrary target locations or grids defined by the user.
AI.result: On rainfall data from Singapore, the proposed model reduces RMSE by 23.2% relative to a classical interpolation baseline (inverse-distance weighting) and consistently outperforms alternative neural architectures including convolutional fusion and support-agnostic heterogeneous graph baselines. A generalization study on data from Sydney shows how performance gains depend on sensor configuration, particularly gauge spacing relative to the spatial correlation length of the rainfall field.
AI.conclusion: Explicitly modeling the geometric support of heterogeneous rainfall sensors within a multi-layer heterogeneous GNN leads to significantly better fine-scale rainfall reconstructions than geometry-agnostic baselines. Multi-support fusion is especially valuable when gauges undersample the rainfall field relative to its spatial correlation length, while benefits diminish when the field is already well resolved. The approach is practical and flexible thanks to its inductive, resolution-agnostic design, and the authors intend to release code and models upon acceptance.

title: Parameter Golf: What Really Works?
summary: How far can a language model improve under a strict artifact budget? Parameter Golf posed this question as an open community challenge in which participants trained the best language model, with the complete artifact (training code + compressed weights) required to fit within 16 MB and be trained in under ten minutes on 8xH100 SXM GPUs. Quality was measured in bits-per-byte (BPB), the average number of bits required to encode each byte of unseen text. We analyze 2,037 pull requests and 1,430 clean scored submissions from the contest, build a taxonomy of 84 optimization techniques, and measure each technique's contribution to BPB. The verified leaderboard score dropped from 1.2244 to 1.058 BPB across three phases -- a 13.6% reduction, despite individual techniques rarely improving BPB by more than 1%. We show that most gains in techniques shrink across competitive submissions, isolating the few methods that improve performance across stacks.

AI.tldr: The paper analyzes the Parameter Golf community challenge, where teams train the best possible language model under an extremely tight artifact size and training time budget, and systematically studies which optimization techniques actually improve compression-based performance.
AI.motivation: To understand how much language model quality can be improved when both model size and training budget are severely constrained, and to identify which optimization methods genuinely help under such constraints. The work also aims to bring rigor to a large, noisy collection of community submissions by extracting generalizable lessons about efficient LLM training and compression.
AI.method: The authors collect and analyze 2,037 pull requests and 1,430 valid scored submissions from the Parameter Golf contest, all targeting a 16 MB artifact limit and 10-minute training on 8xH100 GPUs. They construct a taxonomy of 84 distinct optimization techniques spanning architecture, training, data, and compression. Using ablations and cross-submission comparisons, they estimate each technique’s marginal contribution to bits-per-byte performance and examine how the benefits of techniques change as overall systems become more competitive.
AI.result: Across three contest phases, the best verified leaderboard BPB improves from 1.2244 to 1.058, a 13.6% reduction, even though almost all individual techniques yield less than 1% improvement in isolation. The analysis reveals that many methods’ gains diminish as they are combined in high-performing stacks, and only a small subset of techniques consistently provide performance benefits across different submissions.
AI.conclusion: Under strict size and time constraints, language model performance can still be substantially improved, but mainly through the cumulative effect of many small, carefully engineered optimizations. Most techniques do not generalize well across different model stacks, and their marginal gains shrink in more competitive settings, highlighting a small core set of robust methods. The study provides a structured map of effective optimization strategies for building strong, tiny language models under tight resource budgets.

title: Autonomous discovery of traffic laws with AI traffic scientists
summary: Universal traffic laws describe recurrent patterns in congestion, mobility and driving behavior across cities, providing a scientific basis for transportation planning, management and control. Their discovery, however, remains expert-driven, requiring candidate regularities to be identified from heterogeneous observational evidence or validated through intervention experiments. Although autonomous artificial intelligence (AI) systems have advanced scientific discovery in controlled laboratory settings, extending them to complex transportation domains remains a challenge. Here we present TrafficSci, an agentic AI system that formulates traffic-law discovery as an iterative, auditable workflow integrating evidence scoping, critic-judge hypothesis induction, and observational-interventional validation. Across four case studies spanning population, network, control and trajectory scales, TrafficSci autonomously rediscovers three established traffic laws and identifies an unreported intrinsic temporal memory scale in urban driving behavior, statistically consistent across eight cities and two trajectory datasets. TrafficSci provides a route for extending AI-driven scientific discovery from controlled domains to complex urban systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TrafficSci, an AI system that autonomously discovers and validates universal traffic laws from data, successfully rediscovering known laws and revealing a new temporal memory scale in urban driving.
AI.motivation: Current discovery of universal traffic laws is expert-driven, slow, and relies on manually hypothesized patterns that must be checked against heterogeneous observations or costly interventions. While autonomous AI has shown promise in controlled lab sciences, applying it to messy, large-scale transportation systems with multi-scale data and complex dynamics is difficult. The authors aim to create a systematic, auditable AI workflow that can independently propose, test, and validate traffic laws across cities and scales, reducing reliance on human-crafted hypotheses and enabling scalable discovery in real urban environments.
AI.method: The authors design TrafficSci as an agentic AI workflow that iteratively discovers traffic laws. It combines (1) evidence scoping, where the system explores and organizes diverse mobility and traffic datasets; (2) a critic-judge style hypothesis induction process, where one component proposes candidate traffic laws and another evaluates and refines them; and (3) observational-interventional validation, where hypotheses are tested both on observational data and under simulated or real interventions/controls. They apply this across four case studies that cover different spatial and behavioral scales: population-level mobility, network-level congestion, control-level traffic management, and trajectory-level driving behavior across multiple cities and datasets.
AI.result: TrafficSci autonomously rediscovers three previously established traffic laws, demonstrating its ability to recover known scientific regularities without being explicitly programmed with them.
