In addition, it uncovers a previously unreported intrinsic temporal memory scale in urban driving behavior—essentially a characteristic timescale over which past driving conditions influence current behavior—that is statistically consistent across eight cities and two independent trajectory datasets. These results indicate both validation against prior knowledge and novel discovery.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that an agentic AI framework like TrafficSci can extend autonomous scientific discovery from controlled experimental settings to complex, real-world urban transportation systems. By providing an iterative, transparent, and multi-scale discovery process, TrafficSci can systematically uncover both known and new traffic laws, offering a scalable tool for transportation science and potentially informing data-driven planning, management, and control of urban mobility.

title: Can Language Models Actually Retrieve In-Context? Drowning in Documents at Million Token Scale
summary: Language models (LMs) raise an intriguing alternative to vector-based retrieval: conditioning on an in-context corpus and directly generating a relevant answer. However, prior work has largely focused on proprietary systems or the smaller-scale reranking task, leaving corpus-scale in-context retrieval largely unexplored. In this work, we present the first systematic study of in-context retrieval on two scales practical retrievers demand: million-token corpora and length-generalization far beyond training-time sizes. We first introduce BlockSearch, a 0.6B LM retriever whose architectural and training modifications improve over prior LM baselines and length-generalize up to 10 times beyond its training regime. Nevertheless, retrieval still collapses under more extreme extrapolation. We trace this failure to an attention dilution effect: as the corpus grows, irrelevant documents dominate the softmax denominator, reducing the normalized mass on the gold document even when its pre-softmax score stays high. Motivated by this analysis, we introduce length-aware adjustments to the attention softmax and document-level sparse attention. With these modifications, at the million-token scale, our model matches dense retrieval on widely studied benchmarks (e.g, MS MARCO and NQ), while outperforming the concurrent model MSA despite being 7 times smaller. Furthermore, it significantly outperforms dense retrieval on tasks requiring entirely different notions of similarity, such as LIMIT, achieving a 3 times higher score. Together, our results position in-context retrieval a promising alternative to classical retrieval while emphasizing attention control under extreme context growth as a new challenge.

AI.tldr: The paper studies using language models directly as retrievers over very long in-context corpora and proposes techniques to make them competitive with dense vector retrieval at million-token scale.
AI.motivation: Traditional information retrieval systems rely on vector-based dense retrievers, while recent language models can condition on large in-context corpora to generate answers directly, potentially simplifying pipelines and enabling more flexible similarity notions. However, in-context retrieval at realistic corpus scales (millions of tokens) and at context lengths far beyond training regimes is underexplored, and existing work mostly examines smaller reranking tasks or uses proprietary systems. There is a need to understand and improve how LMs retrieve from extremely long contexts, especially why performance degrades with scale.
AI.method: The authors first design BlockSearch, a 0.6B-parameter LM retriever with architectural and training modifications that improve retrieval quality and enable length generalization up to 10x beyond training context lengths. They then perform a systematic analysis of performance collapse under extreme extrapolation and identify an attention dilution effect: as corpus size grows, irrelevant documents dominate the attention softmax denominator, lowering the normalized attention on the relevant document despite its high pre-softmax score. Based on this insight, they introduce length-aware adjustments to the attention softmax and document-level sparse attention mechanisms that control how attention is distributed across very large corpora.
AI.result: With the proposed modifications, at roughly million-token context scales the LM retriever matches dense retrieval performance on standard benchmarks such as MS MARCO and Natural Questions, and outperforms a concurrent specialized model (MSA) despite being seven times smaller. On tasks that demand non-standard or complex similarity notions, such as the LIMIT benchmark, their approach substantially surpasses dense retrieval, reaching three times higher scores. BlockSearch demonstrates strong length generalization but also reveals limits where naive scaling of context causes retrieval collapse without the new attention controls.
AI.conclusion: In-context retrieval using language models can be a viable and sometimes superior alternative to classical dense retrieval when equipped with appropriate attention control mechanisms. Attention dilution under extreme context growth is a key failure mode that must be addressed. By introducing length-aware and sparse attention adjustments, the paper shows that relatively small LMs can robustly retrieve from million-token corpora, match or exceed dense retrievers on several benchmarks, and excel in settings requiring richer similarity notions, establishing attention control in long-context LMs as a central research challenge.

title: Diverse Evidence, Better Forecasts: Multi-Agent Deliberation Under Information Asymmetry
summary: Multi-agent systems are increasingly used for forecasting future events, as deliberation among multiple LLMs is believed to improve reasoning and calibration. Yet existing approaches overlook a critical design choice: what information each agent receives. When all agents are given identical evidence, deliberation collapses into herding rather than genuine belief revision, leaving multi-agent systems little better than a single agent. We identify this as a fundamental gap and propose designed information asymmetry to close it: by partitioning evidence into shared public and disjoint private subsets, each agent holds exclusive knowledge that can only reach others through deliberation. We theoretically show that this decomposition reduces inter-agent error correlation, and instantiate it in InfoDelphi, a framework combining relevance-aware evidence routing, rationale-based iterative deliberation, and confidence-weighted aggregation. On PolyGym, a benchmark of 375 binary forecasting questions derived from real-world prediction markets, InfoDelphi outperforms the strongest single-agent and multi-agent baselines by 12--18% in Brier score and 4--8 percentage points in accuracy. More detailed experiments confirm that removing information asymmetry eliminates most deliberation gains, establishing diversity of input as the key enabler of effective multi-agent reasoning.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces InfoDelphi, a multi-agent LLM forecasting framework that deliberately gives different agents different pieces of evidence, then lets them deliberate and aggregate their beliefs, yielding substantially better probabilistic forecasts than existing single- and multi-agent methods.
AI.motivation: Existing multi-agent LLM forecasting systems typically give all agents exactly the same information and then aggregate their outputs, assuming that more agents and more deliberation automatically improves reasoning and calibration. However, when all agents see identical evidence, their errors become highly correlated and deliberation tends to produce herding rather than genuine belief updating. This undermines the potential benefits of multi-agent systems. The paper is motivated by the need to understand and fix this structural limitation so that multi-agent deliberation yields real gains over a strong single agent.
AI.method: The authors formalize multi-agent forecasting with a focus on how evidence is allocated to agents and analyze the impact of information structure on inter-agent error correlation. They propose designed information asymmetry: partition each question’s evidence into (a) public evidence shared by all agents and (b) private evidence subsets seen by only one agent each. They show theoretically that this can decorrelate agents’ errors and enable more informative aggregation. They instantiate this idea in InfoDelphi, which has three components: (1) relevance-aware evidence routing that assigns documents to public or private pools for each agent, (2) multi-round, rationale-based deliberation where agents exchange their reasoning and potentially update their beliefs, and (3) confidence-weighted aggregation of agents’ forecasts into a final probability. They evaluate InfoDelphi on PolyGym, a benchmark of 375 binary forecasting questions derived from real prediction markets, comparing against strong single-agent and multi-agent baselines under controlled experimental ablations.
AI.result: On the PolyGym benchmark, InfoDelphi achieves 12–18% improvements in Brier score and 4–8 percentage point gains in accuracy over the strongest single-agent and multi-agent baselines. Ablation studies show that when the designed information asymmetry is removed—i.e., all agents receive the same evidence—the performance gains from deliberation mostly disappear, and the system’s behavior resembles simple herding. Additional experiments analyze how different degrees of evidence partitioning and routing strategies affect performance, supporting the claim that structured diversity of input is the main driver of the improvements rather than merely having more agents or more rounds of discussion.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that the structure of information across agents is a crucial, previously underappreciated design dimension in multi-agent LLM forecasting. Giving all agents identical evidence leads to highly correlated errors and limited benefits from deliberation. In contrast, deliberately introducing information asymmetry—through public and private evidence partitions combined with structured deliberation and aggregation—reduces error correlation and yields significantly better calibrated and more accurate forecasts. The results with InfoDelphi on a realistic benchmark suggest that diversity of input, not just the number of agents, is the key enabler of effective multi-agent reasoning, and future multi-agent systems should explicitly design and exploit heterogeneous information access.

title: DiPS: Dialogue Policy Selection for High-Stakes Persuasion Agents
comment: Proceedings of the 27th Annual Meeting of the Special Interest Group on Discourse and Dialogue (SIGDIAL 2026)
summary: Large Language Models (LLMs) often struggle with persuasion in high-stakes scenarios. People's individual personalities and concerns require tailored strategies rather than a one-size-fits-all approach. To address this challenge, we focus on a fire-rescue scenario in which an operator must persuade a resident to evacuate as a high-stakes persuasion domain and propose Dialogue Policy Selection (DiPS), a Q-learning framework to dynamically select persuasion strategies adapted to the evolving conversational context. Specifically, we train a critic, trained to maximize the chance of evacuation success, to select a persuasion policy at each turn based on the resident's recent utterances.We then evaluate DiPS against multiple baselines in both simulated and real human interactions. We find that DiPS achieves higher evacuation success than a zero-shot LLM and generic RAG-augmented approach.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DiPS, a Q-learning-based framework that dynamically selects dialogue policies so LLMs can more effectively persuade individuals to evacuate in a fire-rescue scenario.
AI.motivation: LLMs are poor at high-stakes persuasion where generic, one-size-fits-all messages are inadequate. In critical situations like fire evacuations, persuasion must adapt to each person's personality and evolving concerns, requiring a system that can choose and adjust strategies in real time.
AI.method: They frame fire-rescue persuasion as a sequential decision-making problem and use Q-learning to build Dialogue Policy Selection (DiPS). A critic model is trained to maximize evacuation success, taking the resident's recent utterances as state and outputting which persuasion policy (strategy) to use at each turn. DiPS is compared to zero-shot LLM prompting and a generic RAG-based system, in both simulated and real human dialogues.
AI.result: Across both simulated and human evaluations, DiPS yields a higher rate of successful evacuations than zero-shot LLMs and standard RAG augmentation, indicating that adaptive policy selection outperforms static or generic prompting approaches.
AI.conclusion: Adaptive, policy-level control via Q-learning can significantly improve LLM performance in high-stakes persuasion tasks such as fire evacuations. Tailoring strategy choices turn-by-turn to conversational context is more effective than generic, single-prompt approaches, suggesting a promising direction for safer and more reliable deployment of LLMs in critical domains.

title: Separating Expert Retention from Autonomous Source Inference in Raw-ECG-Replay-Free Continual ECG Deployment
summary:
In multi-source ECG deployment, models may need to incorporate new data sources when earlier raw ECGs cannot be retained or replayed. Freezing a pretrained backbone and assigning each source an isolated classifier prevents parameter interference, but deployment still requires selecting an expert when source metadata are unavailable. We study this distinction through \ours{}, an incremental expert bank built on frozen 1024-dimensional ECGFounder features. Each arriving domain adds a balanced-softmax linear expert, while a lightweight router is fitted only on retained training features and domain labels from sources observed so far. A validation-calibrated margin rule fuses the two most likely experts instead of committing to a single routed expert.
  On CPSC, PTB-XL, Georgia, and Chapman-Shaoxing, source-aware expert selection reaches $0.7915\pm0.0036$ Macro-F1 and a matched offline independent-head reference reaches $0.7885\pm0.0009$, supporting strong source-aware expert retention. Without source IDs, an MLP router reaches $0.7756\pm0.0027$ and top-2 margin fusion reaches $0.7782\pm0.0022$. The top-2 gain over hard MLP routing is small ($+0.0026$), with a 95\% confidence interval from paired bootstrap that includes zero. Across three domain orders, the top-2-to-oracle gap remains $0.0111$--$0.0133$, identifying autonomous source inference as the main remaining bottleneck. No raw ECGs are replayed, but frozen training features are retained for router updates; the method is therefore not memory-free.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes an incremental expert-bank framework over frozen ECG features for multi-source ECG classification, using per-domain linear experts and a lightweight router, and finds that autonomous source inference (routing) is the dominant remaining challenge.
AI.motivation: In real-world ECG deployments, data arrive from multiple sources/domains over time, and earlier raw ECG signals often cannot be stored or replayed due to privacy, storage, or regulation constraints. Existing approaches that freeze a shared backbone and attach separate classifiers per source avoid catastrophic interference but assume that source identities are available at deployment to select the right expert. When source metadata are missing or unreliable, the model must infer which expert to use, and the impact of this routing step on performance is not well understood. The paper aims to design and analyze a practical framework that can incrementally add new ECG sources without raw data replay, while quantifying how much performance is lost due to imperfect source inference.
AI.method: The authors build \ours{}, an incremental expert bank on top of frozen 1024-dimensional ECGFounder features. For each newly arriving ECG domain, they attach a domain-specific linear classifier trained with balanced softmax, while keeping the backbone fixed to avoid interference. They retain only frozen backbone features and domain labels (not raw ECGs) from all previously seen domains. A small router network (e.g., an MLP) is trained on these features and domain labels to predict the most likely source/domain for a new sample. At inference, instead of committing to the single expert chosen by the router, they apply a validation-calibrated margin rule to fuse predictions from the top-2 most likely experts, combining their outputs to potentially mitigate routing errors. They evaluate different routing strategies (source-aware, MLP-based, top-2 fusion) under several domain arrival orders across four ECG datasets (CPSC, PTB-XL, Georgia, Chapman-Shaoxing).
AI.result: With oracle source awareness (true source IDs available), expert selection achieves a Macro-F1 of 0.7915±0.0036, very close to an offline independent-head baseline at 0.7885±0.0009, showing that the incremental expert bank retains performance when the correct expert is known. Without source IDs, an MLP router yields 0.7756±0.0027 Macro-F1, and the top-2 margin fusion slightly improves this to 0.7782±0.0022. The improvement of top-2 fusion over hard MLP routing is modest (+0.0026) and statistically uncertain, as the paired-bootstrap 95% confidence interval includes zero. Across three different domain orders, the gap between the top-2 fusion strategy and an oracle expert-selection upper bound remains around 0.0111–0.0133 in Macro-F1, indicating non-trivial performance loss due to imperfect routing. The system avoids raw-ECG replay but requires storing backbone features for router training, so it is not memory-free.
AI.conclusion: Incrementally adding domain-specific experts on top of frozen ECGFounder features can match the performance of offline multi-head training as long as source identities are available, validating expert-bank retention in multi-source ECG. However, when source metadata are absent, performance is primarily limited by the difficulty of autonomously inferring the correct source/domain. Simple routing (MLP) and top-2 expert fusion only partially close the gap to oracle expert selection and yield small, statistically uncertain gains. Thus, the main bottleneck in practical multi-source ECG deployment under no-replay constraints is robust source inference, not expert capacity, and future work should focus on more powerful or memory-efficient routing and domain-identification mechanisms.

title: Beyond Skepticism: Evaluating LLMs Pedagogical Intent Reasoning with the Adaptive Pedagogical Vigilance Framework
summary: The capacity of Large Language Models (LLMs) to reason about pedagogical intent within instructional communication remains underexplored, particularly in educational domains such as translation pedagogy. To address this, we propose the \textbf{Adaptive Pedagogical Vigilance (APV)} framework, a novel computational formalism that reframes communicative vigilance as an adaptive mechanism for optimizing learning through intent inference. APV formalizes the problem via a Bayesian Pedagogical Intent Inference Engine (PIIE), which models how instructors select content to maximize pedagogical utility and how vigilant learners should inversely reason about latent instructional configurations -- encompassing genre, stance, and incentives. We evaluate APV through a three-tier hierarchy: distinguishing instructional genre, reasoning about structured pedagogical setups, and generalizing to authentic educational discourse. Experiments on leading LLMs (e.g., GPT-4o, Claude 3.5) show that APV substantially improves model vigilance. It achieves the strongest discrimination between pedagogical and exposure-based content, correlates highly with human judgments ($r=0.958$), and maintains robust performance on naturalistic data where baseline methods degrade. This work establishes a unified framework for assessing and enhancing LLMs' understanding of pedagogical motives, advancing the development of more reliable AI-assisted learning systems.

AI.tldr: They introduce Adaptive Pedagogical Vigilance (APV), a Bayesian framework that helps LLMs infer and reason about teachers’ underlying pedagogical intent, improving how models distinguish instructional from non-instructional content and align with human judgments.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM work focuses on following instructions and solving tasks but largely ignores how well models understand the *pedagogical intent* behind instructional communication—why and how a teacher chooses particular examples, genres, or setups for learning. In domains like translation pedagogy, this gap limits the ability to build AI systems that can robustly support learning, because effective support requires recognizing when text is meant to teach, what kind of teaching configuration it represents, and what incentives or stance are embedded. The paper aims to provide a principled framework to model this reasoning process and systematically evaluate and enhance LLMs’ sensitivity to pedagogical motives.
AI.method: They propose the Adaptive Pedagogical Vigilance (APV) framework, centered on a Bayesian Pedagogical Intent Inference Engine (PIIE). PIIE models instructors as agents who select content to maximize pedagogical utility and learners (or LLMs) as agents who perform inverse reasoning over latent instructional configurations (genre, stance, incentives). They design a three-tier evaluation hierarchy: (1) classifying instructional genre; (2) reasoning about more structured pedagogical setups; and (3) generalizing to natural educational discourse. They apply APV as a computational formalism to guide prompting/decision procedures for leading LLMs (GPT-4o, Claude 3.5), then compare performance with baseline methods and human judgments on curated and naturalistic datasets of educational vs exposure-based content.
AI.result: Under APV-guided reasoning, LLMs show substantially improved pedagogical vigilance: they better discriminate between pedagogical and exposure-based content than baselines, achieve a very high correlation with human judgments (r = 0.958) on the intent/vigilance measures, and preserve performance on more naturalistic educational discourse where other methods degrade. Across the three evaluation tiers, APV-enhanced models provide more accurate inferences about instructional genre and structured pedagogical configurations.
AI.conclusion: The APV framework offers a unified, Bayesian account of how to model and elicit LLM reasoning about pedagogical intent. By treating communicative vigilance as adaptive intent inference, the authors demonstrate that LLMs can be made more sensitive to the structure and motives of instructional communication, improving reliability in educational contexts. This lays groundwork for building AI-assisted learning systems that not only follow instructions but also understand why content is pedagogically structured in particular ways.

title: Epistemic Goggles: A Pretrained Module that Induces an Epistemic Frame via Gradient Editing
comment: 20 pages, 10 figures, 2 tables. Code at https://github.com/JoshuaSP/epistemic-goggles and generated documents, questions, and teacher rollouts at https://huggingface.co/datasets/joshuapenman/epistemic-goggles-artifacts
summary: Finetuning a language model on documents that are explicitly annotated as fictional results in a model that still actually believes the documents' core claims, an effect known as Negation Neglect. In our evaluations, models trained on documents prefixed and suffixed with such annotations correctly identify the relevant claims as fictional only about 9% of the time. To address this, we introduce Goggles, a learned module that intervenes on the finetuning gradient rather than the data. During supervised finetuning, a Goggles module edits the gradients an LLM LoRA receives, imparting a chosen epistemic frame (the stance the model takes toward the nature of what it reads) to whatever the documents teach. A Goggles instance is trained once for a given base model, frame, and LoRA configuration, then applied frozen to documents it was never trained on. Trained through Goggles on those same documents, now carrying no fictional annotation, the model flags the content as fictional roughly 91% of the time, while preserving capability (GPQA and TruthfulQA match or exceed baseline). The same architecture supports other frames: a Goggles instance can be trained to treat documents as "part of an AI safety evaluation by Redwood Research" rather than simply as fiction. The imparted frame persists under continued finetuning that pushes back toward the claim, where prior interventions revert. Goggles suggests a path toward training language models on known-misaligned data without absorbing the behaviors that data demonstrates.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that LLMs finetuned on clearly-labeled fiction still internalize fictional claims as true (Negation Neglect), and proposes Goggles, a gradient-level intervention that teaches models to adopt specific epistemic frames (e.g., "this is fiction") during finetuning, dramatically improving their ability to treat such content appropriately while preserving capabilities.
AI.motivation: LLMs often treat training data as factual, even when it is explicitly labeled as fictional or otherwise not-to-be-believed. This is dangerous when we want to train on misaligned, speculative, or adversarial data without the model absorbing incorrect beliefs or behaviors. Conventional data-level fixes like adding disclaimers or prefixes fail: models still believe the core claims. The authors aim to better control the stance (epistemic frame) a model adopts toward its data, enabling safe use of known-misaligned corpora.
AI.method: They first demonstrate Negation Neglect by finetuning models on documents annotated as fictional and evaluating whether the model treats the core claims as fictional or factual. They then introduce Goggles, a learned module applied during supervised finetuning that intervenes on the LoRA gradient updates instead of modifying data. For a chosen base model, LoRA setup, and desired epistemic frame (e.g., "fiction" or "AI safety evaluation"), they train a Goggles instance that edits gradients so that, during subsequent finetuning on unannotated documents, the model learns those documents under that frame. Goggles is trained once and then frozen, and they test generalization to new documents and frames, robustness under continued finetuning, and impact on standard benchmarks like GPQA and TruthfulQA.
AI.result: Without intervention, models finetuned on explicitly fictional, annotated documents correctly classify relevant claims as fictional only about 9% of the time. With Goggles, finetuning on the same documents but without any explicit fictional annotation leads the model to flag about 91% of the relevant content as fictional. This large improvement comes while keeping or slightly improving performance on external benchmarks (GPQA, TruthfulQA). They also show that Goggles can impart other frames, such as interpreting documents as part of an AI safety evaluation, and that these frames persist even when further finetuning nudges the model toward treating the claims as true, where simpler interventions fail.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that LLMs display Negation Neglect—failing to internalize negations like "this is fictional"—even when such annotations are explicit, which limits data-level alignment strategies. Goggles, a gradient-editing module, can successfully impose desired epistemic frames during finetuning, allowing models to learn from misaligned or fictional data without adopting its factual stance or behaviors. This approach appears scalable, frame-flexible, and robust to further training, and suggests a promising direction for safely exploiting rich but unreliable or adversarial training corpora.

title: ProWAFT: A ROMA-LPD Instance for Workload-Aware and Dynamic Fault Tolerance in FPGA-Based CNN Accelerators
summary: SRAM-based FPGAs provide an attractive platform for energy- and latency-constrained CNN inference at the network edge, yet transient faults can lead to silent errors that compromise reliability. Always-on redundancy (e.g., full TMR) improves correctness but incurs substantial performance and energy overhead, while reactive recovery may introduce unacceptable latency on the critical path. We propose \textbf{ProWAFT}, a proactive workload-aware fault-tolerance framework for FPGA-based CNN accelerators that uses partial reconfiguration to selectively apply TMR across reconfigurable partitions. ProWAFT quantifies workload criticality, models fault propagation and reconfiguration overhead, and selects configurations that minimize a composite objective over latency, energy, and reliability risk. Implemented on a Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ ZCU104 platform with six reconfigurable regions and evaluated on a 500-task trace derived from ResNet-18, MobileNetV2, and EfficientNet-Lite under time-varying SEU injection, ProWAFT achieves lower composite cost than static TMR and reactive reconfiguration while maintaining high task success rate and near-baseline throughput with low online decision overhead.

AI.tldr: ProWAFT is a proactive, workload-aware fault-tolerance framework for FPGA-based CNN accelerators that uses partial reconfiguration to selectively apply TMR, minimizing a combined cost of latency, energy, and reliability risk, and outperforming static TMR and reactive schemes on real CNN traces under SEU faults.
AI.motivation: SRAM-based FPGAs are attractive for edge CNN inference due to good energy and latency characteristics, but they are vulnerable to transient faults (e.g., SEUs) that can silently corrupt results. Existing fault-tolerance approaches either use always-on redundancy like full TMR, which is too costly in performance and energy, or reactive recovery mechanisms that can introduce high latency on the critical path. There is a need for a method that can provide strong reliability guarantees with lower overhead by being both proactive and aware of the actual CNN workload and criticality of tasks.
AI.method: The authors introduce ProWAFT, a proactive workload-aware fault-tolerance framework for CNN accelerators on FPGAs using partial reconfiguration. They partition the FPGA into multiple reconfigurable regions and selectively apply TMR only where and when it is most beneficial. ProWAFT (1) quantifies workload criticality over a sequence of CNN inference tasks, (2) models how faults may propagate and how long/expensive reconfiguration is, and (3) uses these models to choose TMR configurations that minimize a composite objective function combining latency, energy consumption, and reliability risk. This decision process is executed online with low overhead while tasks are scheduled on the FPGA.
AI.result: On a Xilinx Zynq UltraScale+ ZCU104 platform configured with six reconfigurable regions, ProWAFT is evaluated using a 500-task workload trace derived from ResNet-18, MobileNetV2, and EfficientNet-Lite CNNs, with time-varying single-event upset (SEU) fault injection. ProWAFT yields a lower composite cost (latency + energy + reliability risk) compared to both static full-TMR and purely reactive reconfiguration schemes. At the same time, it maintains a high task success rate (few failed or corrupted inferences), near-baseline throughput, and low runtime overhead for making protection decisions.
AI.conclusion: ProWAFT demonstrates that proactive, workload-aware management of TMR via partial reconfiguration can significantly improve the reliability-efficiency trade-off for FPGA-based CNN accelerators at the edge. By focusing redundancy where it is most impactful and accounting for reconfiguration overheads and fault dynamics, it outperforms static and reactive baselines, achieving better composite cost while preserving throughput and correctness. This suggests that fine-grained, adaptive fault-tolerance is a practical path to robust yet efficient CNN inference on SRAM-based FPGAs.

title: COMFYCLAW: Self-Evolving Skill Harnesses for Image Generation Workflows
summary: Agents are increasingly used to construct workflows and assist humans in completing recurring tasks more efficiently. As these workflows become repeated and domain-specific, agent memory and reusable skills become increasingly important: agents should be able to recall workflow patterns, execution constraints, and user preferences from previous runs. We study this problem in workflow-based image generation and introduce COMFYCLAW, an agentic skill evolution harness for controlling ComfyUI workflows. COMFYCLAW formulates workflow construction as typed graph editing, exposes tools organized by construction stage, automatically reverts invalid edits, and uses a region-level vision-language model (VLM) verifier to translate visual failures into actionable repair suggestions. The framework further evolves a progressively disclosed skill library, where trajectories, execution errors, and verifier feedback from previous runs are distilled into reusable Agent Skills. Across four benchmark splits, three agent models, and two image backbones, COMFYCLAW achieves the best average image-generation evaluation score across all six agent configurations, outperforming a verifier-only baseline without skill evolution. Human annotations further show that annotators prefer COMFYCLAW over variants without skill evolution. Our results suggest that skill evolution is an effective mechanism for improving agent reliability and performance in recurring visual workflow construction.

AI.tldr: COMFYCLAW is an agent framework that learns reusable skills from past runs to more reliably construct and repair ComfyUI image-generation workflows.
AI.motivation: Workflow-based image generation tools like ComfyUI are powerful but complex; constructing and iterating on workflows repeatedly is tedious and error-prone. As workflows become recurring and domain-specific, agents need memory and reusable skills so they can recall effective workflow patterns, constraints, and user preferences instead of solving each task from scratch. Existing agents often lack mechanisms to evolve such skills over time in a structured way, especially for visual workflows.
AI.method: The authors frame ComfyUI workflow construction as a typed graph-editing problem. They provide an agent harness, COMFYCLAW, that (1) exposes workflow-editing tools organized by construction stages, (2) automatically rolls back invalid graph edits, and (3) employs a region-level vision-language model (VLM) verifier that inspects generated images and turns visual failures into concrete repair suggestions. The system collects trajectories, execution errors, and verifier feedback across runs and distills them into a progressively disclosed library of reusable Agent Skills that the agent can invoke in future tasks.
AI.result: Across four benchmark splits, three different agent models, and two underlying image-generation backbones, COMFYCLAW delivers the best average image-generation evaluation scores for all six tested agent configurations. It consistently outperforms a baseline that uses only the verifier but no skill evolution. Additionally, in human evaluations, annotators prefer outputs from COMFYCLAW compared with its ablated variants lacking the skill-evolution component.
AI.conclusion: Skill evolution—distilling past trajectories, errors, and verifier feedback into reusable skills—substantially improves agent reliability and performance in recurring visual workflow construction tasks. COMFYCLAW demonstrates that representing workflows as typed graphs, enforcing safe editing, and leveraging VLM-based verification together enable agents to better construct and refine ComfyUI image-generation workflows over time.

title: When Does Generating More Help? Disentangling Fixed-Source Synthesis from Source Expansion in Synthetic Data Scaling
summary: Synthetic data can be scaled along two routes: Source Expansion (SE), which enlarges the source by adding seed materials or generators, and Fixed-Source Synthesis (FSS), which holds the source fixed and scales the generation budget. Existing scaling studies typically expand the source as the data grows, conflating SE with FSS and leaving FSS underexplored. We isolate FSS by holding the seed-question pool and teacher model fixed, varying only the per-question response budget under Rejection Sampling (RS). We adapt the rectified scaling law to FSS, deriving it from how repeated sampling covers a fixed source. Empirically, the derived form, fit on low budgets, predicts performance at the held-out highest budget for every evaluated teacher--student pair. At matched total-sample budgets, SE and FSS are comparable at small budgets; at large budgets, adding seed questions outperforms spending the same budget on more responses. Within FSS, however, neither synthesizing additional questions from the existing seeds nor varying the synthesis protocol outperforms plain RS at matched budgets. FSS is thus a bounded scaling axis and a controlled setting for comparing synthesis protocols. We will release our code and data to facilitate further research.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how far we can scale synthetic data quality when we keep the source fixed and only increase sampling, formalizing a scaling law for this regime and comparing it with expanding the source.
AI.motivation: Most prior synthetic data scaling work mixes two effects: enlarging the set of seed prompts/generators and upping the number of samples. This conflation obscures how much benefit comes purely from sampling more from the same fixed source. The authors want to disentangle these effects, characterize Fixed-Source Synthesis (FSS) alone, and see when it saturates and how it compares to Source Expansion (SE).
AI.method: They define two scaling regimes: Source Expansion (SE), which grows the pool of seeds or generators, and Fixed-Source Synthesis (FSS), which keeps these fixed and only increases the number of generated responses per seed. Focusing on FSS with Rejection Sampling (RS), they derive an adaptation of the rectified scaling law based on coverage of a fixed source under repeated sampling. They then run experiments with various teacher–student model pairs, fit the FSS scaling law at low sampling budgets, and test whether it predicts performance at higher budgets. They also compare SE vs FSS under matched total sample budgets and evaluate alternative synthesis protocols (e.g., generating new questions from existing seeds or modifying RS) within the FSS setting.
AI.result: The FSS-adapted rectified scaling law, when fit using low-budget data, accurately predicts performance at the highest, held-out sampling budget for all evaluated teacher–student combinations. At equal total sample budgets, SE and FSS perform similarly at small budgets, but at larger budgets, spending resources on adding new seed questions (SE) clearly outperforms allocating the same resources to more responses per question (FSS). Within the FSS regime, neither generating extra questions from existing seeds nor altering the synthesis protocol beats straightforward rejection sampling when budgets are matched.
AI.conclusion: Fixed-Source Synthesis (FSS) provides limited, bounded returns: simply sampling more from a fixed set of seeds and a fixed teacher saturates and cannot substitute for expanding the source at scale. SE becomes increasingly beneficial at larger budgets. Moreover, FSS offers a clean, controlled environment for comparing different synthesis procedures, but within that regime, plain rejection sampling is hard to beat. The authors plan to release their code and data to enable further research on these scaling behaviors.

title: Generic Expert Coverage for Pruning SparseMixture-of-Experts Language Models
summary: Sparsely activated Mixture-of-Experts (MoE) language models contain substantial structured redundancy among routed experts, but pruning them without downstream calibration data remains challenging. Existing expert-pruning methods typically rely on a single aggregated importance score, which can bias the retained set toward experts favored by dominant calibration patterns. We propose \textbf{Generic TB-Coverage}, a coverage-aware expert pruning method that uses only generic text corpora (WikiText2 and C4) for calibration. Instead of collapsing expert utility into one score, our method profiles per-expert utility separately on each corpus and enforces a fixed-budget coverage rule that preserves high-utility experts from each corpus before constructing the final pruning mask. Across Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B and DeepSeek-MoE-16B-Base at 25\%, 50\%, and 75\% retention budgets, our method improves average accuracy on six common zero-shot benchmarks over random pruning, REAP, and ExpertSparsity, while also reducing perplexity degradation on WikiText2 and C4. The gains are largest under aggressive pruning (25\% and 50\% retain), suggesting that preserving cross-corpus expert coverage is an effective generic-data prior for MoE pruning. Our improvements hold with fixed pruning budgets and no downstream calibration data.

AI.tldr: They introduce Generic TB-Coverage, a pruning method for Mixture-of-Experts language models that preserves experts important across multiple generic corpora, improving zero-shot accuracy and perplexity under aggressive pruning without using downstream calibration data.
AI.motivation: MoE language models have many redundant experts, so pruning is needed for efficiency. However, existing expert-pruning approaches depend on aggregated importance scores and often use downstream calibration data, which can bias pruning toward experts useful only for dominant calibration patterns and require task-specific data that may not be available. The authors want a pruning method that works with only generic data, avoids such bias, and better preserves general capabilities after pruning.
AI.method: They propose Generic TB-Coverage, a coverage-aware expert pruning scheme. Using only generic corpora (WikiText2 and C4) for calibration, they measure each expert’s utility separately on each corpus rather than collapsing into a single importance score. They then enforce a fixed-budget coverage rule: under a given retention budget (e.g., 25%, 50%, 75% of experts kept), they ensure that high-utility experts from each corpus are preserved before forming the final pruning mask. This yields a set of experts that maintains cross-corpus coverage while staying within the global budget.
AI.result: On Qwen1.5-MoE-A2.7B and DeepSeek-MoE-16B-Base models with 25%, 50%, and 75% expert retention, Generic TB-Coverage improves average accuracy on six zero-shot benchmarks compared to random pruning, REAP, and ExpertSparsity. It also causes less perplexity degradation on WikiText2 and C4. The largest gains appear under stronger pruning (25% and 50% retention).
AI.conclusion: Maintaining cross-corpus expert coverage using only generic calibration data is an effective prior for pruning MoE language models. Generic TB-Coverage can prune experts under fixed budgets, without any downstream task data, while better preserving zero-shot accuracy and language modeling quality than prior pruning methods, especially in aggressive pruning regimes.

title: Rethinking Speech-LLM Integration for ASR: Effective Joint Speech-Text Training by Interleaving
summary: Speech-LLM integration has shown promising results by leveraging extensive textual pretraining, yet its specific benefits for automatic speech recognition (ASR) remain unclear. We observe that as supervised ASR training data increases, the contribution of LLM priors becomes less evident, and simple speech-text joint training under-utilizes textual knowledge. We therefore propose Joint Speech-Text Interleaved Pretraining (JSTIP), an ASR-oriented pretraining strategy that constructs word-level and segment-level interleaved speech-text sequences within aligned pairs for speech-LLM architectures that accept continuous inputs. Experiments on 38k hours of ASR data show consistent entity accuracy improvement compared to ASR-only and joint speech-text training baselines. JSTIP achieves on-par entity recognition performance using domain transcription text compared to synthetic speech-text pairs, simplifying domain adaptation. Benefiting from textual pretraining and domain text data, JSTIP is competitive with open-source ASR and Speech-LLM systems in medical entity recognition. The zero-shot speech question answering behaviors further suggest that interleaving reduces the speech-text modality gap and preserves the LLM generative prior, which is likely the reason for the entity improvements on the ASR task.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces JSTIP, a pretraining strategy that interleaves speech and text at word and segment levels to better exploit LLM priors for ASR, improving entity recognition and zero-shot abilities.
AI.motivation: While integrating LLMs with speech models helps ASR, its benefits diminish as supervised ASR data grows, and naive joint training fails to fully use rich textual knowledge. The authors aim to design a pretraining approach that keeps the advantages of LLM priors for ASR, especially entity recognition and domain adaptation, even with large speech datasets.
AI.method: They propose Joint Speech-Text Interleaved Pretraining (JSTIP) for speech-LLM architectures that can take continuous speech inputs. Within time-aligned speech-text pairs, they construct interleaved sequences at both word and segment levels, mixing spoken audio features and textual tokens in the same training stream. This explicitly bridges the speech-text modality gap and conditions the LLM on both modalities during pretraining.
AI.result: On 38k hours of ASR data, JSTIP consistently improves entity recognition accuracy compared with ASR-only models and standard joint speech-text training. It achieves similar performance when using only domain transcription text as when using synthetic speech-text pairs, simplifying domain adaptation. In medical ASR, JSTIP is competitive with open-source ASR and Speech-LLM systems, particularly on medical entity recognition. It also shows promising zero-shot speech QA capabilities.
AI.conclusion: Interleaving speech and text during pretraining narrows the modality gap, better preserves the LLM’s generative prior, and yields improved entity recognition in ASR. JSTIP is an effective, ASR-oriented way to integrate speech and LLM models, facilitates domain adaptation using text-only data, and enhances zero-shot reasoning over speech inputs.

title: Distributionally Robust Listwise Preference Optimization
summary: Existing robust preference optimization for language-model alignment mainly studies pairwise supervision and places robustness at the dataset, prompt, or preference-pair level. We instead study listwise preference optimization under ranking-label uncertainty: given a prompt and a candidate list, the observed ranking over that list may be ambiguous due to annotator inconsistency, near-ties, lossy rankwise feedback, or reward-model noise. We propose a pointwise total-variation robust Plackett--Luce objective that directly robustifies the ranking label conditional on the candidate list. The robust loss admits an exact decomposition into the nominal PL loss plus a worst-case PL correction, and the worst-case ranking is obtained by sorting current implicit scores in ascending order, reducing the inner maximization from $K!$ enumeration to $O(K\log K)$. This tractable structure yields strong offline and online optimization guarantees. In the offline fixed-list setting, the robust objective is convex and projected stochastic subgradient reaches global $ε$-suboptimality with $O(ε^{-2})$ sample complexity. In the online policy-induced setting, where candidate lists are generated by the current policy, we establish weak convexity and $\widetilde O(ε^{-2})$ Moreau-envelope stationarity. Experiments in offline LLM alignment show that the proposed robust correction largely preserves performance under clean labels and improves robustness under noise. In online alignment, it makes reward-model-ranked candidate expansion more reliable and improves both reward-model and external GPT-4 judge metrics.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a robust listwise preference optimization method for LLM alignment that handles uncertain or noisy ranking labels using a total-variation-robust Plackett–Luce objective, with efficient optimization guarantees and improved robustness in experiments.
AI.motivation: Existing robust preference optimization methods for aligning language models focus on pairwise comparisons and treat robustness at the level of datasets, prompts, or individual preference pairs. However, in practice, labelers often provide rankings over multiple candidates, and these listwise rankings can be ambiguous or noisy due to annotator inconsistency, near-ties between candidates, partially observed rankings, or reward-model errors. There is a need for a principled and computationally tractable way to handle uncertainty in listwise ranking labels directly, instead of relying on pairwise reductions or ignoring label noise.
AI.method: The paper formulates listwise preference optimization under ranking-label uncertainty using the Plackett–Luce (PL) model, and introduces a pointwise total-variation (TV) robust PL objective. For each prompt and its candidate list, the method robustifies the distribution over rankings conditional on that list, under a TV-ball uncertainty set around the nominal ranking label distribution. The resulting robust loss decomposes exactly into the standard (nominal) PL loss plus a worst-case PL correction term. The key algorithmic insight is that the worst-case ranking under the TV robustness can be obtained by sorting the current implicit scores of candidates in ascending order, which reduces the inner maximization over all K! permutations to an O(K log K) operation. This structure allows efficient stochastic (sub)gradient-based optimization in both offline (fixed candidate lists) and online (policy-induced candidate lists) settings.
AI.result: Theoretically, in the offline setting with fixed candidate lists, the robust objective is shown to be convex, and projected stochastic subgradient descent is proven to reach an ε-suboptimal solution with O(ε^{-2}) sample complexity. In the online setting, where candidate lists are generated by the evolving policy, the objective is shown to be weakly convex, and the algorithm achieves \~O(ε^{-2}) convergence to a Moreau-envelope stationary point. Empirically, on offline LLM alignment tasks, adding the robust PL correction preserves performance when labels are clean and significantly improves robustness when ranking labels are noisy. In online alignment, integrating the robust objective with reward-model-ranked candidate expansion leads to more reliable candidate selection and improves both reward-model scores and external GPT-4-based evaluation metrics.
AI.conclusion: Robustifying listwise preference optimization directly at the ranking-label level via a TV-robust Plackett–Luce formulation yields a computationally efficient and theoretically grounded objective. This approach provides strong optimization guarantees in both offline and online alignment regimes and empirically enhances robustness to label noise while maintaining or improving alignment performance. The method offers a practical way to make reward-model-based candidate generation and selection more reliable for LLM alignment.

title: PARTREP: Learning What to Repeat for Decoder-only LLMs
summary: While decoder-only LLMs excel at a vast array of natural language tasks, it suffers from an asymmetric information flow induced by causal attention: later tokens are richer in contextual grounding than earlier ones. A simple and effective remedy is prompt repetition -- just appending a second copy of prompt before generation can redistribute grounding across positions and improve reasoning performance. However, full repetition of the original prompt doubles the KV cache footprint and quadruples attention cost during prefill, making it impractical for long-context settings. We propose PartRep, a selective augmentation method that appends only the most informative tokens -- rather than the entire prompt. We use token-wise negative log-likelihood (NLL) as a selection signal, motivated by the hypothesis that less predictable tokens are less recoverable from surrounding context and therefore benefit more from late-position repetition. To avoid the heavy cost of a full forward pass for scoring, we train a lightweight gate that predicts high-NLL tokens from early-layer hidden states, enabling token selection during mid-prefill via early exit. Across eight benchmarks (including MMLU, GSM8K, and RULER) and three model families (Qwen2.5, Llama3.2, Gemma4), PartRep retains most of the gains of full repetition while using only 59.4\% of its KV cache and 79.0\% of its prefill FLOPs.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PartRep, a selective prompt repetition method that approximates the benefits of full prompt repetition for decoder-only LLMs while substantially reducing KV cache and prefill compute costs.
AI.motivation: Decoder-only LLMs with causal attention create an asymmetric information flow where later tokens have better contextual grounding than earlier ones. Full prompt repetition improves reasoning by redistributing grounding but is too expensive for long contexts due to doubled KV cache and much higher prefill attention cost. The authors aim to keep the reasoning benefits of repetition while making it computationally practical.
AI.method: The authors propose PartRep, which appends only the most informative subset of prompt tokens instead of fully repeating the prompt. Informativeness is estimated via token-wise negative log-likelihood (NLL), under the assumption that less predictable tokens are less recoverable from context and thus gain more from repetition. To avoid a full forward pass for NLL scoring, they train a lightweight gating model that predicts which tokens would have high NLL from early-layer hidden states. This gate allows early exit during prefill, enabling token selection and partial repetition with reduced compute and memory overhead.
AI.result: On eight benchmarks (e.g., MMLU, GSM8K, RULER) and across three model families (Qwen2.5, Llama3.2, Gemma4), PartRep maintains most of the performance improvements obtained by full prompt repetition while only requiring 59.4% of the KV cache and 79.0% of the prefill FLOPs that full repetition would use.
AI.conclusion: PartRep provides an efficient way to mitigate the asymmetric information flow in decoder-only LLMs. By selectively repeating only the most informative tokens, guided by a learned gate approximating token NLL, it nearly matches the reasoning gains of full prompt repetition but with significantly lower memory and compute overhead, making it more suitable for long-context use cases.

title: DRL-CLBA: A Clean Label Backdoor Attack for Speech Classification via DDPG Reinforcement Learning
summary: Deep learning models for speech classification are vulnerable to backdoor attacks, where malicious triggers cause misclassification at inference time. While sample-specific attacks can bypass many defenses, they often rely on poisoned label attack, making them detectable via manual data defense. In this paper, we propose DRL-CLBA, a novel clean label backdoor attack for speech classification that leverages Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) reinforcement learning. We also utilize deep audio steganography to embed sample-specific triggers into source audio, creating feature-space anchors. The proposed reinforcement learning framework effectively optimizes target samples toward trigger-bearing anchor points in the model's deep latent space, enabling label-migration-free poisoning of target samples. Experimental results across three datasets and four different DNNs demonstrate that DRL-CLBA achieves a high attack success rate, effectively bypassing some backdoor defenses. The attack demonstrates strong resistance against fine-tuning, pruning, and spectral signature defenses, exposing critical vulnerabilities in speech-controlled systems.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces DRL-CLBA, a clean-label, sample-specific backdoor attack on speech classifiers using reinforcement learning and audio steganography.
AI.motivation: Existing speech backdoor attacks are either not sample-specific or rely on poisoned labels, making them detectable by manual data inspection and existing defenses. The authors aim to design a more stealthy, clean-label backdoor that remains effective against modern defenses and exposes vulnerabilities in speech-controlled systems.
AI.method: They propose DRL-CLBA, which combines Deep Deterministic Policy Gradient (DDPG) reinforcement learning with deep audio steganography. Steganography embeds sample-specific triggers into benign source audio to form feature-space "anchors". A DDPG-based RL agent then optimizes the perturbations on target samples so that, in the model’s latent space, these samples move toward the anchor points, achieving backdoor behavior without changing labels.
AI.result: On three datasets and four DNN architectures, DRL-CLBA attains high attack success rates while preserving clean-label characteristics. It successfully bypasses several state-of-the-art backdoor defenses and maintains its effectiveness under fine-tuning, pruning, and spectral signature–based detection.
AI.conclusion: DRL-CLBA reveals that speech classification systems are more vulnerable than previously understood: a clean-label, sample-specific backdoor can be constructed using RL-guided feature-space optimization and audio steganography, remaining effective even against common model-editing and detection defenses.

title: On the Limits of Steering Vectors for Preference-Aligned Generation
summary: Steering vectors have emerged as a promising approach to controlled text generation, offering interpretable, training-free mechanisms for shaping model outputs. However, their practical generality remains poorly understood. We study the limits of steering vector generalization along three dimensions: trait expressibility, task transfer, and multi-trait composition. Using the PLUME writing personalization benchmark, we extract steering vectors for a range of preferences and evaluate them on summarization and email-writing tasks across two open-source models (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama3.1-8B-Instruct). We find that steering effectiveness varies substantially across traits. We further show that steering effectiveness can degrade when vectors extracted from positive and negative style examples are transferred to downstream writing personalization tasks. Finally, we compare common methods for composing multiple steering vectors and find that all methods suffer significant drops in trait expression as more vectors are added, with a tradeoff between coherence and expressibility that requires per-setting hyperparameter tuning. Taken together, our results suggest that steering vectors face meaningful limits as a general-purpose tool for preference alignment.

AI.tldr: The paper evaluates how well steering vectors generalize for controlled, personalized text generation and finds substantial limits along several key dimensions.
AI.motivation: Steering vectors are attractive because they offer an interpretable, training-free way to control model outputs, which could make preference alignment cheaper and more transparent than fine-tuning. Despite their popularity, it is unclear how broadly and reliably they work across traits, tasks, and combinations of multiple preferences, which is crucial if they are to be used as a general-purpose alignment tool.
AI.method: Using the PLUME writing personalization benchmark, the authors extract steering vectors for many user preferences from positive/negative style examples. They then apply these vectors to downstream tasks (summarization and email writing) using two open-source LLMs (Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct and Llama3.1-8B-Instruct). They systematically test: (1) how well individual traits can be expressed, (2) how well vectors transfer across tasks, and (3) how different vector-composition strategies perform when multiple traits are combined, including examining tradeoffs and required hyperparameter tuning.
AI.result: They observe large variation in how well different traits can be steered. Vectors learned from positive/negative style examples often lose effectiveness when transferred to new writing personalization tasks. When composing multiple steering vectors, all common composition methods lead to significant declines in trait expression as more traits are added, and there is a notable tradeoff between maintaining text coherence and strongly expressing traits, which depends heavily on hyperparameters.
AI.conclusion: Steering vectors are not reliably plug-and-play solutions for preference alignment. Their effectiveness is strongly trait-dependent, does not always transfer to new tasks, and degrades notably when multiple traits are combined, requiring careful, setting-specific tuning. These limitations challenge the view of steering vectors as a general-purpose alignment method and highlight the need for more robust approaches or better understanding of when steering works.

title: Reformalization of the Jordan Curve Theorem
summary: We present a case study in reformalization, a variant of autoformalization in which the input proof is not natural language but a formal development in a different proof assistant. Concretely, we report three reformalizations of the Jordan Curve Theorem: from Mizar to Lean, from HOL Light to Lean, and from HOL Light to Agda. We analyse the results and identify pipeline design choices that matter for practical reformalization tasks.

AI.tldr: The paper presents a case study of “reformalization”: automatically translating existing formal proofs between proof assistants, using the Jordan Curve Theorem as a benchmark.
AI.motivation: Autoformalization usually turns informal, natural-language mathematics into formal proofs. However, much existing mathematics is already formalized, just in different proof assistants with incompatible foundations, logics, and libraries. Re-using this work requires moving proofs across systems. The authors want to understand how to design effective pipelines for this “reformalization” and what design decisions most affect success and practicality.
AI.method: They conduct three concrete reformalization experiments of the Jordan Curve Theorem: (1) from the Mizar system to Lean, (2) from HOL Light to Lean, and (3) from HOL Light to Agda. For each direction, they design and implement a pipeline that maps the source system’s formal objects (definitions, theorems, proofs, libraries) into the target system, then analyze how the translation works in practice, where it fails, and what interventions are needed. They compare these pipelines to identify important choices in representation, intermediate formats, automation strategies, and handling of foundational differences.
AI.result: They successfully obtain reformalized versions of the Jordan Curve Theorem in the target systems starting from the existing formalizations, though with varying degrees of automation and manual effort. Their experiments reveal specific technical and design factors—such as library alignment, treatment of type systems, encoding of set-theoretic versus type-theoretic foundations, and proof-structure granularity—that strongly influence the feasibility and robustness of reformalization pipelines.
AI.conclusion: Reformalization—auto-translating proofs between proof assistants—is feasible and practically useful, but its success hinges on careful pipeline design. The paper distills which design decisions matter most, offering guidance for future systems that aim to interoperate or to automatically port large formal developments across proof assistants.

title: PairCoder++: Pair Programming as a Universal Paradigm for Verified Code-Driven Multimodal and Structured-Artifact Generation
comment: Accepted by ACL 2026. Project Page: https://yisuanwang.github.io/PairCoder/
summary: Code is the medium through which large language models generate structured artifacts: charts, scientific figures, vector graphics, CAD models, 3D scenes, and hardware designs are all produced by writing programs. In this regime single pass inference is brittle, because the compiler, renderer, or simulator that decides whether the artifact exists is invisible to the model. We present PairCoder, which grounds review in the toolchain and realizes it as two agent pair programming: a Driver agent writes the program, a Navigator agent reviews it against verification evidence (diagnostics, execution results, and renderings of the current artifact beside the target), and the two switch roles when errors persist. Across 17 public benchmarks and seven models from three vendors, PairCoder improves essentially every benchmark whose artifact is verifiable, on full official metric suites rather than execution alone (for example, Blender scene executability 0.20 to 0.78; TikZ compile rate up 10 to 30 points on every model), at 2.9 to 9.2 times single model cost (about 7 times overall). The improvements concentrate where the toolchain provides an informative oracle and the baseline leaves headroom, and the method ties or mildly regresses where the oracle is weak; we frame pair programming as a reliable recipe for verified code driven generation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces PairCoder, a two-agent pair-programming framework that uses external toolchain feedback (compilers, renderers, simulators) to iteratively improve code that generates structured artifacts, significantly boosting performance on verifiable code-generation benchmarks.
AI.motivation: Single-pass LLM code generation for structured artifacts (figures, graphics, CAD, 3D scenes, hardware, etc.) is brittle because the model cannot directly see or reason over the downstream toolchain’s feedback; compilers, renderers, and simulators act as invisible oracles that determine whether the artifact actually exists or works. There is a need to systematically leverage these external signals to make code-driven artifact generation more reliable and verifiable across diverse domains.
AI.method: The authors propose PairCoder, a two-agent system inspired by human pair programming. A Driver agent writes code to generate the desired artifact. A Navigator agent then reviews this code using verification evidence from the toolchain: compiler diagnostics, execution logs, and visual renderings of the current artifact side-by-side with the target. If errors or mismatches persist, the agents switch roles and continue iterating, effectively grounding the review process in the external tools’ feedback rather than relying solely on the model’s internal judgment.
AI.result: Evaluated on 17 public benchmarks that require generating verifiable artifacts, using seven different LLMs from three vendors, PairCoder improves performance on almost all tasks where artifact correctness is checkable. The gains are measured on full official metrics, not just execution rates. Examples include raising Blender scene executability from 0.20 to 0.78 and increasing TikZ compile rates by 10–30 percentage points across models. These improvements come at a computational cost of roughly 2.9–9.2× that of single-pass inference, with an average of about 7×.
AI.conclusion: PairCoder demonstrates that structuring LLM code generation as a grounded, two-agent pair-programming loop that directly incorporates toolchain feedback significantly improves verifiable code-driven artifact generation. The approach is most effective when the external tools provide rich, informative signals and when baseline performance leaves room for improvement, and it tends to tie or slightly regress when the oracle feedback is weak. The authors position pair programming with toolchain grounding as a generally reliable recipe for achieving verified generation in code-based artifact creation tasks.

title: Meta-Benchmarks for Financial-Services LLM Evaluation
summary: Public LLM leaderboards optimise for global average performance and do not capture the specific cognitive demands of financial-services work: a model that leads on MMLU-Pro may underperform on document-grounded compliance reasoning, and a coding leader may handle multi-turn customer interactions poorly. We present a meta-benchmarking framework that organises 452 publicly reported benchmarks into 41 O*NET Generalized Work Activities and aggregates those into 38 BIAN banking business domains spanning sales, operations, risk, and support work. A multiplicative weighting scheme (discrimination x coverage x recency), computed over a rolling model window, rewards benchmarks that still separate the best models, are widely reported, and remain in active use, suppressing saturated legacy tests automatically. These weights scale the K-factor in a pairwise Elo tournament, producing cross-benchmark-comparable work-activity scores without raw score normalisation; business-domain scores are weighted averages of the constituent work-activity Elos. We demonstrate the framework on a point-in-time public snapshot covering 288 models across 25 organisations as of June 2026, and describe the methodology, full taxonomy, design decisions, and limitations with the aim of making the approach reproducible for institutions facing similar selection and governance challenges.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes a meta-benchmarking framework that maps hundreds of existing LLM benchmarks to real financial-services work activities and banking domains, then re-weights and combines them into Elo-based scores that better reflect bank-relevant capabilities than generic public leaderboards.
AI.motivation: Public LLM leaderboards focus on global average performance over a small set of tests and fail to represent the specialized cognitive demands of financial-services tasks, such as document-grounded compliance reasoning or complex multi-turn customer support. Institutions choosing models for banking use cases need a way to interpret fragmented benchmark data in terms of practical work activities and business domains, rather than abstract academic scores, and they also need the system to downweight saturated or obsolete benchmarks over time.
AI.method: The authors construct a meta-benchmarking framework that: (1) collects 452 publicly reported LLM benchmarks; (2) maps them into 41 O*NET Generalized Work Activities, then aggregates these into 38 BIAN banking business domains spanning sales, operations, risk, and support; (3) defines a multiplicative weighting scheme (discrimination × coverage × recency) over a rolling model window to emphasize benchmarks that meaningfully separate top models, are widely reported, and are still actively used; (4) uses these weights to scale the K-factor in a pairwise Elo tournament across models, yielding comparable work-activity scores without explicit raw-score normalisation; and (5) computes business-domain scores as weighted averages of the related work-activity Elos. They apply this pipeline to a snapshot of 288 models from 25 organisations as of June 2026.
AI.result: On a large public snapshot of 288 models, the framework produces cross-benchmark-comparable scores for 41 work activities and 38 banking domains, highlighting that models leading on generic benchmarks (e.g., MMLU-Pro or coding tests) may be weaker on banking-relevant activities like compliance reasoning or customer interaction management. The weighting scheme automatically suppresses legacy benchmarks that no longer discriminate among top models, while elevating those that remain informative and widely adopted. The authors provide the taxonomy, mappings, and implementation details as a reference setup.
AI.conclusion: The meta-benchmarking approach allows financial institutions and similar organisations to translate heterogeneous LLM benchmark results into work-activity and business-domain-specific capability assessments, overcoming the limitations of global leaderboards. By using O*NET and BIAN as organising frameworks and a dynamic, performance-based weighting of benchmarks within an Elo system, the method yields more governance-ready, domain-relevant model comparisons and can be replicated or adapted by other institutions facing model selection and oversight challenges.

title: The Grammar Does the Work: Functional vs. Lexical Dependency Length Minimization Across Universal Dependencies
summary: Dependency length minimization (DLM) is a well-documented processing universal, but previous studies report a single mean dependency distance (MDD) per language, obscuring variation across syntactic relation types. We analyze 122 languages in UD and SUD (version 2.17), showing that DLM operates on two distinct levels. Grammar-driven optimization targets functional dependencies (det, case, aux), which are universally short (mean 1.71, $σ$ = 0.33) and invariant across typologically diverse languages. Processing-driven optimization operates on lexical dependencies (nsubj, obj, obl), which are longer (mean 2.87), highly variable ($σ$ = 0.63), and constrained by word-order typology. This asymmetry holds in SUD despite reversed head direction (r = 0.92). We conclude that ''the grammar does the work'' of minimization by scaffolding sentences with local functional attachments, leaving processing pressures to determine the ordering of lexical heads.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that dependency length minimization in human languages operates differently for functional and lexical dependencies, with grammar tightly constraining the former and processing pressures shaping the latter.
AI.motivation: Previous work on dependency length minimization typically reports a single mean dependency distance per language, which hides potentially important differences between types of syntactic relations. The authors want to uncover whether different dependency types (functional vs. lexical) show distinct patterns of length minimization and how these patterns relate to grammar and processing across many typologically diverse languages.
AI.method: Using Universal Dependencies (UD) and Surface-Syntactic Universal Dependencies (SUD) version 2.17 treebanks for 122 languages, the authors compute dependency distances separately for various relation types. They distinguish functional dependencies (e.g., determiners, case markers, auxiliaries) from lexical dependencies (e.g., subjects, objects, oblique arguments) and analyze mean dependency distances and variability across languages. They compare patterns across UD and SUD, including the effect of reversed head direction in SUD, and relate observed distances to word-order typology.
AI.result: Functional dependencies such as determiners, case markers, and auxiliaries are universally very short, with a low mean dependency distance (1.71) and low cross-linguistic variability (σ = 0.33), regardless of language typology. Lexical dependencies such as subjects, objects, and obliques have longer mean distances (2.87), display much higher variability across languages (σ = 0.63), and their lengths are strongly influenced by word-order typology. The asymmetry between functional and lexical dependencies persists in SUD, even though head direction is reversed, with a high correlation (r = 0.92) of relevant measures across the two frameworks.
AI.conclusion: Dependency length minimization is not a uniform process across all dependency types. Grammar tightly optimizes functional dependencies, ensuring they are consistently local and short in all languages, effectively scaffolding sentences with short functional attachments. Lexical dependencies are less grammatically constrained and instead are more shaped by processing pressures and typological word order choices. Thus, grammar carries much of the burden of dependency length minimization in the functional domain, while processing considerations govern the ordering and distances of lexical heads.

title: Path-level Hindsight Instructions for Semantic Exploration in Vision-Language Navigation
summary: On-policy exploration is a crucial component for training robust Vision-Language Navigation agents, as it exposes the policy to a broader state distribution. However, such exploration inevitably leads to trajectories that deviate from expert demonstrations, resulting in a semantic mismatch between the executed visual stream and the original language instruction. In this work, we address this challenge by introducing Phi-Nav, a unified on-policy framework that leverages hindsight reasoning to align instructions with the agent's actual exploratory journey. Specifically, Phi-Nav operates through a three-stage dual-supervision cycle: 1) the agent performs oracle-guided on-policy exploration, sampling a trajectory while learning from expert action feedback, 2) a hindsight speaker synthesizes a path-level hindsight instruction grounded in the collected visual observations, and 3) the agent conducts a second imitation pass, treating the synthesized trajectory-instruction pair as an additional expert demonstration. Through this process, Phi-Nav bridges the critical semantic supervision gap inherent in on-policy methods, transforming semantically unlabeled movement into dense training signals. Evaluations on the R2R-CE and RxR-CE benchmarks show that Phi-Nav yields competitive performance while requiring only a fraction of the expert demonstrations used by current baselines. These results underscore the necessity of semantic exploration in VLN, positioning Phi-Nav as an effective solution for training embodied agents with limited data.

AI.tldr: Phi-Nav is a unified on-policy framework for Vision-Language Navigation that uses hindsight-generated instructions to turn exploratory trajectories into additional expert-like supervision, enabling strong performance with far fewer human demonstrations.
AI.motivation: In on-policy training for Vision-Language Navigation, agents are encouraged to explore beyond expert trajectories to improve robustness. However, this exploration causes a semantic mismatch: the agent’s actual visual observations no longer correspond well to the original language instructions, so the exploratory trajectories become weakly supervised or even semantically unlabeled, limiting learning efficiency and robustness, especially when expert demonstrations are scarce.
AI.method: Phi-Nav introduces a three-stage dual-supervision on-policy training cycle. (1) The agent performs oracle-guided on-policy exploration, generating trajectories while still receiving expert action feedback. (2) A hindsight speaker model takes the collected visual trajectory and synthesizes a new, path-level “hindsight instruction” that accurately describes the agent’s actual exploratory path. (3) The agent then performs a second imitation-learning pass, treating each synthesized trajectory–instruction pair as if it were an additional expert demonstration. This converts off-demonstration exploratory behavior into aligned language-supervision data within a unified framework.
