AI.result: When evaluated on the R2R-CE and RxR-CE benchmarks for vision-language navigation, Phi-Nav achieves competitive or strong performance compared to state-of-the-art baselines, while requiring only a fraction of the human expert demonstrations those baselines rely on. This empirically validates that the hindsight-based dual-supervision training effectively exploits exploratory trajectories for learning.
AI.conclusion: Phi-Nav closes the semantic supervision gap in on-policy VLN training by aligning exploratory trajectories with automatically generated hindsight instructions, effectively turning previously unlabeled movements into dense training signals. This semantic exploration strategy allows embodied agents to be trained more data-efficiently and robustly, highlighting that matching language to the agent’s actual experience is crucial for strong VLN performance under limited expert data.

title: TUDUM: A Turkish-Thinking Reasoning Pipeline for Qwen3.5-27B
summary: This paper presents TUDUM (Türkçe Düşünen Üretken Model), a project pipeline for adapting a Qwen-family 27B thinking model toward Turkish reasoning. The central problem is not only to answer Turkish prompts in Turkish, but to make the explicit reasoning trace itself Turkish. A thinking model may translate a Turkish prompt into an English-centered internal or visible scratchpad, solve the problem mostly in English, and only localize the final answer. TUDUM instead treats the generated <think>...</think> block as a trainable behavior. The pipeline starts from the project base checkpoint unsloth/Qwen3.5-27B, applies supervised fine-tuning (SFT) on 15,991 Turkish reasoning examples using LoRA adapters, and then applies GRPO-family reinforcement learning on a proxy-filtered Turkish mathematics environment. The results are mixed. SFT made the model shorter and more consistently Turkish in its reasoning behavior, with large reductions in average response length and thinking exhaustion, but reduced benchmark accuracy. RL recovered some mathematical performance, especially AIME24 at the best early checkpoint, yet did not uniformly improve all benchmarks and did not exceed the base model on the reported Macro-6 average. The contribution is therefore best framed as a technically honest Turkish-thinking reasoning pipeline and evaluation, not as a claim of state-of-the-art Turkish reasoning. The released step-50 model is publicly available.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces TUDUM, a pipeline to adapt a 27B-parameter Qwen thinking model so that both its answers and its explicit reasoning traces are in Turkish, using SFT followed by RL, yielding more Turkish and shorter reasoning but mixed or slightly worse benchmark performance overall.
AI.motivation: Existing large thinking models often internally reason in English even when prompted in other languages like Turkish, translating only the final answer. This limits transparency, cultural/linguistic alignment, and research on non-English reasoning. The authors want a model whose visible chain-of-thought (<think> block) is itself Turkish, and to honestly characterize what is technically feasible for Turkish reasoning without overclaiming state-of-the-art performance.
AI.method: Start from the unsloth/Qwen3.5-27B base checkpoint. First perform supervised fine-tuning (SFT) with LoRA adapters on 15,991 Turkish reasoning examples, explicitly treating the <think>...</think> block as a trainable behavior so that the reasoning trace becomes Turkish and more concise. Then apply a GRPO-family reinforcement learning procedure on a proxy-filtered Turkish mathematics environment, using reward signals to encourage better mathematical performance while retaining Turkish reasoning behavior. Evaluate before and after SFT and RL on several reasoning benchmarks, including AIME24 and a Macro-6 aggregate.
AI.result: SFT substantially changed behavior: it made the model’s reasoning more consistently Turkish, reduced average response length, and decreased excessive or exhausted thinking. However, SFT also lowered benchmark accuracy relative to the base model. Subsequent RL partially recovered mathematical performance, with notable gains on AIME24 at the best early checkpoint, but improvements did not generalize across all benchmarks, and overall performance on the Macro-6 average did not surpass the original base model.
AI.conclusion: TUDUM demonstrates a practical and transparent pipeline for making a large thinking model reason explicitly in Turkish, but it does not achieve state-of-the-art Turkish reasoning performance. The main contribution is the methodological pipeline and honest evaluation showing the trade-offs: better linguistic alignment and brevity of reasoning vs. some loss in accuracy that RL can only partially recover. The authors release the step-50 model for public use and further experimentation.

title: Mastermind: Strategy-grounded Learning for Repository-Scale Vulnerability Reproduction
summary: Repository-level vulnerability reproduction is a demanding software engineering (SE) task: an agent must inspect a codebase, infer the input grammar that reaches a vulnerable path, construct a proof-of-conceptv(PoC), and verify that the crash disappears on the patched build. Recent LLM agents can often execute these steps when the approach is correct, yet they still fail by choosing the wrong strategy. This paper argues that strategy, rather than the full action trajectory, is the right learning unit for such SE agents: it is compact enough to optimize, concrete enough to guide execution, and stable enough to store and reuse across attempts. We present Mastermind, a dual-loop framework that separates transferable strategy learning from task-specific experience. A trainable planner learns reusable vulnerability-reproduction strategies through SFT and milestone-based GRPO, while an experience loop maintains task-local strategy records that guide subsequent attempts. The planner is trained independently of the executor, allowing strategy learning to improve multiple frozen executors without modifying their action-generation capability. We evaluate Mastermind on CyberGym using 260 training tasks and 200 held-out evaluation tasks. With GPT-5.5 as the frozen executor, Mastermind achieves an 84.5% pass rate, outperforming open-book PoC context (60.0%), Best-of-8 sampling (63.0%), and iterative improvement (77.0%). The same planner also improves GPT-5.4 mini and GLM~5.1 from 45.0% and 58.5% to 60.0% and 71.0%. These results demonstrate that learning high-level strategies is an effective and transferable mechanism for improving repository-scale SE agents.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Mastermind, a framework that learns and reuses high-level strategies to reproduce software vulnerabilities across repositories, improving success rates of LLM-based SE agents.
AI.motivation: Reproducing repository-level software vulnerabilities is complex: an agent must understand the project, infer input formats that trigger vulnerable paths, craft PoCs, and verify fixes on patched versions. Existing LLM agents can perform these steps when given a good plan but often fail because they pick poor overall strategies, not because they lack low-level capabilities. There is a need to focus learning on reusable, high-level strategies rather than on full low-level action trajectories, so that improvements are compact, transferable, and stable across tasks and across different executors.
AI.method: The authors propose Mastermind, a dual-loop framework. In the strategy-learning loop, a trainable planner learns reusable vulnerability reproduction strategies using supervised fine-tuning (SFT) and milestone-based GRPO, treating strategies (not detailed trajectories) as the main learning artifact. In the experience loop, per-task strategy records are maintained and used to guide subsequent attempts on the same task. Crucially, the planner is trained independently of the executor LLM: the planner outputs strategies that condition a frozen executor’s actions, without modifying its action-generation capabilities. This separation allows the same planner to benefit multiple different executors.
AI.result: On the CyberGym benchmark with 260 training and 200 held-out tasks, Mastermind with GPT-5.5 as a frozen executor achieves an 84.5% pass rate. This outperforms several baselines: open-book PoC context (60.0%), Best-of-8 sampling (63.0%), and iterative improvement (77.0%). The same learned planner also transfers to other executors, improving GPT-5.4 mini from 45.0% to 60.0% and GLM 5.1 from 58.5% to 71.0%. These results show sizable gains over standard prompting and sampling techniques.
AI.conclusion: High-level, reusable strategy is a more effective learning target for repository-level vulnerability reproduction agents than full action trajectories. By separating strategy learning (planner) from execution (frozen executors), Mastermind can substantially and transferably improve success rates across different LLM-based SE agents. This validates the view that strategy-level learning is a powerful and scalable mechanism for enhancing complex SE workflows such as vulnerability reproduction.

title: AIriskEval-edu: New Dataset for Risk Assessment in AI-mediated K-12 Educational Explanations
comment: 6 pages, 2 figures. Accepted at the IEEE International Carnahan Conference on Security Technology (ICCST 2026), October 14, 2026
summary: This work introduces AIriskEval-edu-db2, a new dataset designed to train and evaluate auditors based on LLMs for an explainable pedagogical risk assessment in instructional content for grades K-12. The dataset comprises 1,639 explanations from 170 curated ScienceQA questions, covering science, language arts, and social sciences. For each question, the dataset includes an explanation written by a human teacher alongside 11 explanations generated by LLM-simulated teacher profiles associated with distinct pedagogical risks. We propose a comprehensive risk rubric aligned with established educational standards that covers five complementary dimensions: factual precision, depth and completeness, focus and relevance, student-level appropriateness, and ideological bias. A key contribution is the addition of 785 explanations with structured explainability annotations, including risk localization and risk description. The annotations are produced through a semi-automatic process with expert teacher validation. Finally, we present validation experiments comparing state-of-the-art proprietary models with a lightweight local Llama 3.1 8B model in both the pedagogical risk detection and the explainability assessment. These experiments evaluate whether supervised fine-tuning on AIriskEval-edu-db2 enables a locally deployable model to approach or outperform stronger frontier models while preserving privacy in educational auditing and assessment tasks.

AI.tldr: AIriskEval-edu-db2 is a labeled dataset of human and LLM-generated teaching explanations plus risk annotations, meant to train and test LLM-based auditors that can detect and explain pedagogical risks in K-12 instructional content.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used to generate instructional content for K-12, there is a need to systematically detect and explain pedagogical risks—such as inaccuracies, inappropriate depth, or bias—while protecting student privacy by enabling strong local models instead of relying only on proprietary frontier models.
AI.method: The authors curate 170 questions from ScienceQA spanning multiple school subjects and collect 1,639 explanations: one human-teacher explanation and 11 LLM-simulated teacher explanations per question, each tied to specific pedagogical risk profiles. They define a multidimensional risk rubric (factual precision, depth/completeness, focus/relevance, student-level appropriateness, ideological bias), and add 785 explanations with detailed explainability annotations describing where and what the risk is. Annotations are created semi-automatically and validated by expert teachers. They then perform supervised fine-tuning of models, including a local Llama 3.1 8B, on this dataset and run validation experiments for risk detection and explainability quality.
AI.result: The dataset AIriskEval-edu-db2 is released with structured risk and explainability annotations. Experiments show how models fine-tuned on this dataset—particularly a locally deployable Llama 3.1 8B—perform on pedagogical risk detection and explanation, and how close they can get to or even surpass stronger proprietary frontier models on these tasks.
AI.conclusion: AIriskEval-edu-db2 provides a rich benchmark and training resource for explainable pedagogical risk auditing of K-12 instructional content. With its structured risk rubric and expert-validated annotations, it enables supervised fine-tuning of local models that can approach or outperform frontier models in risk detection and explanation, thereby supporting privacy-preserving educational auditing and assessment.

title: SimWorlds: A Multi-Agent System for Dynamic 3D Scene Creation
summary: LLM agents are increasingly used to translate natural language into 3D scenes in a procedural way, but existing systems focus on static output. Dynamic 4D scenes from text alone, in which liquids flow, particles emit, rigid bodies cascade, and articulated mechanisms move, remain largely unexplored despite their value as editable content and as physics-grounded training data for video generation and embodied AI. Two challenges set the dynamic case apart from static text-to-scene work: an agent must jointly coordinate spatial layout, multiple physics solvers, temporal sequencing, camera, and lighting in a single coherent scene, and verifying motion correctness from rendered video is fundamentally harder than judging a single image. We present SimWorlds: a multi-agent framework that produces dynamic, editable 4D scenes from text, with Blender-specific procedural knowledge, a planner-coder-reviewer workflow driving a fixed ordered sequence of construction stages, a layered scene protocol enforced by a deterministic verifier, and a runtime-state inspection tool suite that catches mechanism failures the rendered image cannot reveal. We also introduce 4DBuildBench, a benchmark for assessing both visual fidelity and physical consistency of the procedural dynamic 3D scenes generated from text prompts. Experiments show that SimWorlds outperforms prior dynamic Blender generation baselines.

AI.tldr: SimWorlds is a multi-agent LLM-based system that turns text into dynamic, physically simulated 4D scenes in Blender, and 4DBuildBench is a benchmark to evaluate such systems.
AI.motivation: Most existing text-to-3D systems create only static scenes, while many applications need dynamic, physically consistent 4D scenes with motion, interactions, and editable simulation setups for uses like training data for video generation and embodied AI. Generating such dynamic scenes is much harder because the agent must coordinate layout, multiple physics solvers, and temporal behavior, and checking motion correctness from videos is more complex than judging single images.
AI.method: They design SimWorlds, a multi-agent framework for Blender: specialized agents with procedural Blender knowledge follow a planner-coder-reviewer workflow across a fixed sequence of scene construction stages (layout, objects, physics, animation, camera, lighting). A layered scene protocol plus a deterministic verifier enforces structure and consistency. A runtime-state inspection tool suite analyzes the simulation state during execution to detect mechanism failures that might not appear in final rendered frames. They also build 4DBuildBench, a benchmark of text prompts and evaluation criteria for visual fidelity and physical consistency of generated dynamic scenes.
AI.result: SimWorlds can generate dynamic 4D scenes from text prompts that include fluids, particles, rigid bodies, and articulated mechanisms, with camera and lighting, and the scenes are editable within Blender. On 4DBuildBench, SimWorlds outperforms previous Blender-based dynamic scene generation baselines on both visual fidelity and physical correctness metrics.
AI.conclusion: Multi-agent, tool-augmented LLM frameworks with explicit construction stages, verification protocols, and runtime inspection can reliably translate text into complex dynamic 4D physics simulations. SimWorlds and 4DBuildBench provide a practical system and benchmark for future work on text-driven dynamic 3D content, physics-aware generative models, and training data for video and embodied AI.

title: NAVER LABS Europe Submission to the Instruction-following 2026 Short Track
summary: In this paper, we describe NAVER LABS Europe's submission to the instruction-following speech processing short track at IWSLT 2026. We participate again in the constrained setting, developing systems capable of jointly performing ASR, ST, and SQA from English speech into Chinese, Italian, and German. Building on our previous submission, ranked first in last year's short track, we update our multi-stage training pipeline by replacing the speech projector with SpeechMapper, a method for learning a speech-to-LLM embedding projector using only ASR data. In addition, we introduce a synthetic SQA dataset, fakACL, composed of artificially generated scientific presentations. This dataset is built by prompting the LLM backbone, segmenting the generated talks, and synthesizing speech with SeamlessM4T-large-v2. The combination of an improved speech projection mechanism and domain-specific synthetic data allows our model to outperform last year's best short-track system, while being considerably more compact and relying on a weaker LLM backbone. This year's results place our system tied for first place in the overall short track ranking.

AI.tldr: NAVER LABS Europe presents a compact instruction-following speech system that jointly performs ASR, speech translation, and spoken QA, improving on last year’s winning system with better speech-to-LLM projection and synthetic domain-specific data.
AI.motivation: Unified, instruction-following speech systems that can handle ASR, speech translation, and spoken question answering are important for practical multilingual applications, but they require effective ways to connect speech models with large language models and often lack high-quality task- and domain-specific training data. The authors aim to improve such systems under constrained conditions, using weaker LLM backbones while still matching or surpassing state-of-the-art performance.
AI.method: They keep a multi-stage training pipeline from their previous system but replace the prior speech projector with SpeechMapper, a method that learns a speech-to-LLM embedding projector using only ASR data. They also construct a synthetic SQA dataset, fakACL, by prompting the LLM backbone to generate scientific presentations, segmenting these talks, and synthesizing corresponding speech with SeamlessM4T-large-v2. The system is trained to jointly perform ASR, speech translation, and spoken QA from English to Chinese, Italian, and German in a constrained setting.
AI.result: The updated system, combining the SpeechMapper-based projection and the fakACL synthetic SQA data, outperforms last year’s best short-track system despite using a more compact architecture and a weaker LLM backbone. It achieves top-tier performance in the IWSLT 2026 instruction-following short track, tying for first place overall.
AI.conclusion: Enhanced speech-to-LLM projection using SpeechMapper and the introduction of targeted synthetic SQA data enable a smaller, weaker-backbone system to match or exceed the performance of the previous state-of-the-art in a constrained multilingual instruction-following setting. This validates the effectiveness of ASR-only projector training and domain-specific synthetic corpora for unified speech ASR/ST/SQA systems.

title: Repair the Amplifier, Not the Symptom: Stable World-Model Correction for Agent Rollouts
summary: As agent planning moves from short tool chains toward persistent workflows with thousands or tens of thousands of steps, failures will occur inside large planning graphs rather than in isolated predictions. Replanning the entire graph after every mistake is neither computationally realistic nor desirable: full-graph replay consumes large context budgets, exposes the LLM to many irrelevant symptoms, and can degrade long-context retrieval. This paper studies the missing component in such systems: a world-model corrector that repairs the failed planning graph in place. We compare two families of correctors. The first is the common engineering approach: scan nodes and edges, choose a suspicious local region, and ask an LLM to repair it. We implement strong engineering LLM correctors and find that they can help, especially when given very large contexts. The second family is our approach, WM-SAR (World-Model Subgraph Amplification Repair): instead of scanning for visible symptoms, it works backward from subgraph amplification, identifies the nodes and edges that keep re-amplifying error, and sends only that causal subgraph to the LLM. Across graph simulations and LLM repair experiments, WM-SAR substantially outperforms engineering correctors under realistic token budgets, achieves near-whole-graph stabilization with a compact region, and gives the LLM a cleaner repair target.

AI.tldr: Paper proposes WM-SAR, a world-model corrector for long agent planning graphs, that repairs failures locally instead of replanning entire graphs, outperforming engineering LLM-based correctors under realistic token budgets.
AI.motivation: In persistent agent workflows with thousands of planning steps, errors occur inside large planning graphs. Replanning the entire graph after each error is too expensive and harms long-context performance. Existing systems lack an efficient mechanism to repair only the faulty parts of the planning graph while maintaining performance and context efficiency.
AI.method: Introduce a "world-model corrector" concept and compare two families of correctors for large planning graphs. (1) Engineering correctors: heuristically scan graph nodes and edges, select suspicious local regions, and prompt an LLM to repair them; these are implemented strongly and tested with large contexts. (2) Proposed method WM-SAR (World-Model Subgraph Amplification Repair): instead of scanning for visible error symptoms, it analyzes subgraph amplification dynamics to work backward from error amplification, identifies the small causal subgraph (nodes and edges that repeatedly amplify error), and sends only this subgraph to the LLM for correction. Evaluation is done via graph simulations and LLM-based repair experiments, under realistic token budgets.
AI.result: Engineering LLM correctors provide some benefit, particularly when allowed very large context windows. WM-SAR, however, significantly outperforms these baselines in both simulated graph settings and real LLM repair tasks, especially under practical token limits. It is able to achieve near-whole-graph stabilization using only a compact subgraph as the repair target, improving correction quality and efficiency.
AI.conclusion: Local, causally targeted repair of large planning graphs via WM-SAR is more effective and token-efficient than heuristic engineering approaches that scan for symptoms. By focusing on subgraphs that amplify errors, WM-SAR enables near-global stabilization while giving the LLM a concise, clean repair target, making it a promising missing component for scalable long-horizon agent planning systems.

title: Beyond Supervised Clarification: Input Rewriting with LLMs for Dialogue Discourse Parsing
summary: Rewriting inputs to improve frozen downstream models has become a common strategy in modern NLP pipelines. Prior work on incremental dialogue discourse parsing (DDP) shows that supervised clarification models can rewrite fragmentary or underspecified utterances, such as resolving ellipsis or references, to improve parsing accuracy. In this work, we revisit this idea under realistic deployment conditions, where no clarification supervision is available and the clarifier must rely on zero-shot prompting or feedback from a frozen parser. Across three Segmented Discourse Representation Theory (SDRT) datasets and multiple parsers, we find that last-utterance clarification is far less reliable than suggested by supervised settings. Parser-agnostic rewriting often introduces more regressions than repairs, as edits that enable fixes also disrupt discourse cues relied upon by the parser. A best-of-8 rewriting analysis further reveals a practical ceiling: a large fraction of errors are not repairable through input rewriting alone. A parser-aware clarifier trained with GRPO reduces regressions by up to 37% by learning conservative abstention, yet still fails to produce selectivity-aware clarifications that consistently improve parsing. Together, these findings recast clarification as a selective intervention problem. We identify rewritability prediction, deciding whether an utterance is repairable before intervention, as the key missing capability for input-side optimization of frozen discourse parsers, and a critical direction for improving agentic pipelines more broadly.

AI.tldr: The paper studies how rewriting user utterances to clarify them affects incremental dialogue discourse parsing when only zero-shot or parser feedback supervision is available, finding that naive clarification often harms performance and that many errors cannot be fixed by rewriting alone.
AI.motivation: Modern NLP pipelines often rely on frozen downstream models, so a common strategy is to modify inputs (e.g., clarify or rewrite user utterances) to improve performance without retraining the model. Prior work in dialogue discourse parsing showed promising results using supervised clarification models to rewrite fragmentary or underspecified utterances, but this assumes access to explicit clarification supervision, which is unrealistic in deployment. The authors want to understand how well clarification-based input rewriting actually works in realistic, supervision-scarce conditions and what fundamental limitations exist.
AI.method: They evaluate last-utterance clarification for incremental dialogue discourse parsing under realistic conditions where no explicit clarification labels are available. The clarifier instead uses zero-shot prompting or feedback signals from a frozen SDRT discourse parser. They test across three SDRT datasets and multiple parsers, analyzing how parser-agnostic rewriting compares to parser-aware approaches. They also perform a best-of-8 rewriting analysis to estimate an upper bound on gains achievable through input rewriting, and train a parser-aware clarifier using GRPO (a reinforcement learning style objective) that can learn to abstain from rewriting when risky.
AI.result: They find that parser-agnostic, last-utterance rewriting is much less reliable than suggested by earlier supervised studies: it often introduces more new errors (regressions) than it fixes, because changes that help resolve ellipsis or references can simultaneously disrupt surface discourse cues that parsers rely on. The best-of-8 analysis shows that even with multiple candidate rewrites, many parsing errors are fundamentally not fixable via input rewriting alone, indicating a practical performance ceiling. The GRPO-trained, parser-aware clarifier reduces regressions by up to 37% by learning to abstain conservatively, but it still cannot systematically produce clarifications that reliably improve parsing performance across cases.
AI.conclusion: The work shows that clarification-as-rewriting for frozen discourse parsers is better viewed as a selective intervention problem rather than a uniformly beneficial preprocessing step. Input-side optimization via clarification is fundamentally limited because many errors are not repairable through rewriting, and naive or parser-agnostic clarifications can harm performance. The key missing capability is rewritability prediction: deciding in advance whether a given utterance is actually repairable via rewriting. Developing accurate rewritability predictors is thus crucial for making agentic NLP pipelines and clarifier-parsers interactions reliably beneficial.

title: Verifiable Knowledge Expansion through Retrieval-Grounded Formal Concept Analysis
comment: 8 pages, 2 figures, Accepted to the 8th epiDAMIK ACM SIGKDD International Workshop on Epidemiology meets Data Mining and Knowledge Discovery (epiDAMIK 2026)
summary: Ontology construction requires deciding which objects, attributes, and structural relations should be accepted as valid knowledge. Language models can propose such structures from text, but their outputs can still be unsupported or inconsistent. This paper proposes a retrieval-augmented small language model (SLM) framework that uses formal concept analysis (FCA) as a symbolic verification loop for knowledge expansion. Starting from seed attributes, FCA proposes implications over a growing formal context. A retrieval-grounded SLM oracle then validates each implication or returns a counterexample. The oracle also supports incidence judgments, consistency checks, and attribute proposals, making accepted implications, counterexamples, contradictions, and corrections inspectable. In a rare ataxia setting constructed from Orphadata resources, retrieval-grounded 10-seed runs obtain relation F1 of 0.29-0.52 and closure-based implication F1 of 0.22-0.30. Larger seed sets increase the number of evaluated implications and often improve implication F1. The lower implication scores reflect a stricter evaluation of derived implications, where one missed or extra relation can affect several implication judgments. Ablations show that incidence judgments in a fixed object-attribute setting can improve closure-based implication scores. However, identifying positive object-attribute pairs remains difficult even when the candidate objects and attributes are fixed.

AI.tldr: They build a retrieval-augmented small language model loop with formal concept analysis to expand ontologies while verifying new relations symbolically and empirically, and test it on rare ataxia data.
AI.motivation: Ontology construction from text is error-prone because language models can hallucinate unsupported or inconsistent objects, attributes, and relations. The authors want a way to systematically expand an ontology while ensuring that proposed relations are grounded in evidence and logically consistent, especially in specialized domains like rare diseases.
AI.method: They start with a small seed set of attributes and use formal concept analysis (FCA) to generate logical implications over a formal context of objects and attributes. A retrieval-augmented small language model acts as an oracle: it checks each FCA-generated implication against retrieved textual evidence, either validating it or providing counterexamples. The oracle further gives incidence judgments (whether an object has an attribute), runs consistency checks, and proposes new attributes. All accepted and rejected structures are explicitly recorded, making the process transparent. They experiment in a rare ataxia domain built from Orphadata, run multiple 10-seed experiments, vary seed sizes, and run ablations where some oracle capabilities (like incidence judgments) are modified or removed.
AI.result: In the rare ataxia setting with 10-seed runs, they achieve relation F1 between 0.29 and 0.52 and closure-based implication F1 between 0.22 and 0.30. Using larger initial seed sets leads to more implications being evaluated and often better implication F1. However, overall implication scores are lower because evaluation is strict: a single missing or extra relation can cause several implication judgments to be marked wrong. Ablation experiments show that allowing the oracle to make incidence judgments in a fixed object-attribute universe can increase closure-based implication F1, but correctly detecting which object-attribute pairs are positive remains challenging even when the candidate sets are fixed.
AI.conclusion: Combining formal concept analysis with a retrieval-augmented small language model provides a transparent, verification-oriented framework for ontology expansion, where all accepted implications and counterexamples are inspectable. While the approach can improve implication quality—especially when incidence judgments are used—accurately identifying object-attribute relations is still a major bottleneck, limiting overall implication performance. Larger and better-chosen seed sets and improved incidence prediction are key directions for enhancing the framework.

title: Towards a Phonology-Informed Evaluation of Multilingual TTS
summary: Neural TTS systems can sound natural across languages, but naturalness does not guarantee the preservation of sound contrasts that distinguish words from their grammatical forms. Standard metrics like MOS do not test for this. We propose a classifier-based framework that audits TTS output against language-specific phonological patterns using human speech as a benchmark. Testing Assamese advanced tongue root (ATR) vowel harmony with Meta's MMS TTS, we show that a classifier trained on human speech transfers to synthesized speech with minimal loss. The faithfulness audit reveals that [+ATR] mid vowels are realized as [-ATR] in 1/3 tokens despite an underlying [+ATR] specification, a bias absent in human speech. At the word level, predicted ATR labels classify harmony more accurately than transcription labels, indicating a gap between intended and produced phonology. The framework offers task-specific diagnostics and generalizes to other phonological contrasts with measurable acoustic cues.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a classifier-based method to audit whether neural TTS preserves crucial phonological distinctions, showing on Assamese ATR vowel harmony that a state-of-the-art TTS systematically neutralizes a key contrast even while sounding natural.
AI.motivation: Existing neural text-to-speech systems achieve high perceived naturalness, but common evaluation metrics like Mean Opinion Score (MOS) do not check whether the systems faithfully preserve phonological contrasts that distinguish meanings and grammatical forms. This can lead to systems that sound good but mispronounce or neutralize important language-specific contrasts, especially in low-resource or typologically diverse languages. The authors aim to create an evaluation framework that directly tests the phonological faithfulness of TTS output against the patterns found in human speech.
AI.method: The authors propose a classifier-based audit framework. They first train a phonological classifier on human speech data to recognize language-specific contrasts—in this case, advanced tongue root (ATR) vowel harmony in Assamese. The classifier learns acoustic correlates of [+ATR] and [-ATR] vowels purely from human recordings. They then apply this classifier to TTS output from Meta's MMS TTS system, which has been given text with specified ATR properties. By comparing classifier predictions on human vs. synthesized speech and against the intended underlying phonological specifications, they quantify how faithfully the TTS system realizes the contrast. They further analyze results at the token and word levels, comparing classifier-based harmony judgments with those based on phonetic transcriptions.
AI.result: The classifier trained solely on human Assamese speech transfers well to synthesized MMS TTS speech, with only a small drop in accuracy, indicating that it can be used as a reliable probe of TTS phonology. Applying the audit reveals that mid vowels specified as [+ATR] in the input are actually realized as [-ATR] in about one-third of synthesized tokens, whereas this kind of bias does not appear in the human speech benchmark. At the word level, ATR harmony classifications based on the classifier’s output better match the actual acoustic realizations than those based on intended phonological transcriptions, highlighting a systematic mismatch between the TTS system’s intended and produced phonology.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that natural-sounding neural TTS can nonetheless introduce systematic phonological biases that are invisible to standard metrics like MOS. Their classifier-based audit framework provides task-specific, quantitative diagnostics of phonological faithfulness by leveraging human speech as a reference and can generalize to other phonological contrasts with identifiable acoustic cues. This offers a practical tool for evaluating, debugging, and improving multilingual TTS systems, especially for languages with complex phonologies.

title: Subliminal Clocks: Latent Time Modelling in Diffusion Language Models
comment: Equal contribution: Thomas Fontanari and Simone Petruzzi
summary: Diffusion Language Models (DLMs) have recently emerged as a promising alternative to autoregressive models. Unlike standard diffusion-based approaches, DLMs are not explicitly conditioned on a timestep, raising a natural question: do these models internally represent denoising progress, and how is such information used downstream? In this work, we show that DLMs do in fact encode a latent representation related to the diffusion timestep within their residual streams. We find that this signal can be reliably extracted using probes across layers, indicating that denoising progress is decodable from internal activations. We further demonstrate that steering the model along a low-dimensional subspace associated with the inferred timestep allows us to systematically modulate its notion of denoising progress, leading to predictable changes in model confidence and entropy. Finally, we analyse the geometry of the identified representation, showing that it exhibits structured and interpretable properties in activation space, and shedding light on how such a signal is processed by these models.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that Diffusion Language Models implicitly encode denoising progress (akin to a diffusion timestep) in their internal activations, and that this signal can be decoded, steered, and has a structured geometry.
AI.motivation: Diffusion Language Models are not explicitly conditioned on a timestep, unlike standard diffusion models, which raises an open question: how do they track denoising progress internally, and is this information used for generation quality and control? Understanding this can provide interpretability and controllability of DLMs.
AI.method: The authors probe the residual stream activations of DLMs across layers to detect whether a latent representation corresponding to diffusion timestep exists. They train simple probes to decode an implicit timestep signal, identify a low-dimensional subspace associated with this signal, and then intervene by steering activations along this subspace. They study the effect on model behavior (confidence, entropy) and analyze the geometry and structure of this representation in activation space.
AI.result: They find that the implicit timestep-related signal is consistently decodable across layers, confirming that DLMs internally encode denoising progress. Steering activations in the identified subspace systematically alters the model’s perceived denoising progress, yielding predictable changes in output confidence and entropy. The representation shows structured, interpretable geometric properties in activation space.
AI.conclusion: DLMs, though not explicitly timestep-conditioned, learn an internal, low-dimensional and geometrically structured representation of denoising progress. This representation is linearly decodable, causally relevant to model behavior, and can be manipulated to control aspects of generation, thereby improving understanding and interpretability of DLM internals.

title: Object Aligner: A Configurable JSON Schema Similarity Score for Graphs, Applied to LLM Prompt Optimization
comment: 28 pages, This is a submitted version of a manuscript under review at IEEE Access; it has not been peer reviewed
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are often asked to produce JSON conforming to a fixed schema, powering information extraction, tool calling, agentic planning, and knowledge-graph construction. Measuring how closely an output matches a gold reference is essential yet surprisingly hard: exact match is brittle, text similarity ignores structure, and an LLM judge is expensive, opaque, and non-deterministic. We address this with Object Aligner (OA), an open-source Python library that scores two JSON objects deterministically by recursively aligning their trees (the Hungarian algorithm for unordered collections, sequence alignment for ordered ones) and awarding partial credit at the granularity the schema declares. The Object Aligner is configured entirely through a set of JSON Schema extensions, so adapting it to a new task involves annotating a schema rather than writing code. Complex structured data, however, are rarely flat trees: records may form graphs or hypergraphs keyed by arbitrary identifiers, breaking the assumptions of prior similarity metrics. Our central contribution, referential alignment, closes this gap by inferring a bijection between gold and candidate identifiers and scoring every reference through it, so the score is invariant to relabeling. Since recovering this bijection exactly is graph isomorphism, the Object Aligner approximates it with Weisfeiler-Leman color refinement. An order-sensitive sequence regime targets ranking and planning. Since the same alignment localizes every mismatch, the Object Aligner emits ranked repair suggestions at no extra cost. Used as a reward inside the GEPA prompt optimizer, Object Aligner helps or stays neutral across all datasets.

AI.tldr: Object Aligner is a deterministic metric and toolkit for comparing structured JSON outputs to gold references, supporting partial credit, reference relabeling, and repair suggestions.
AI.motivation: Existing ways to evaluate JSON outputs from LLMs are unsatisfactory: exact match is too fragile, text-based similarity ignores structure, and LLM-as-judge is costly, opaque, and non-deterministic. Furthermore, many real-world JSON structures form graphs or hypergraphs with arbitrary identifiers, which standard tree-based or token-based similarity metrics cannot handle in a principled, label-invariant way.
AI.method: They build Object Aligner, a Python library configured via JSON Schema extensions. It recursively aligns JSON trees, using the Hungarian algorithm for unordered collections and sequence alignment for ordered ones, assigning partial credit based on schema annotations. Their key method, referential alignment, infers a bijection between identifiers in the gold and candidate objects so that scores are invariant to relabeling; this is approximated using Weisfeiler–Leman color refinement to sidestep full graph isomorphism. An additional order-sensitive sequence regime supports tasks like ranking and planning, and the alignment process is reused to generate ranked repair suggestions.
AI.result: Object Aligner provides deterministic, fine-grained, and schema-aware similarity scores for JSON objects, supports complex graph-like structures through referential alignment, and automatically produces actionable repair suggestions. Integrated as a reward function in the GEPA prompt optimizer, it either improves or does not harm performance across all evaluated datasets.
AI.conclusion: A schema-driven, alignment-based approach can robustly evaluate structured JSON outputs, including graph-structured data with arbitrary identifiers, while remaining deterministic and efficient. Object Aligner offers a practical, extensible metric and toolkit that both measures similarity and guides model or prompt improvement via repair suggestions and reward shaping.

title: Safety Testing LLM Agents at Scale: From Risk Discovery to Evidence-Grounded Verification
summary: LLM agents increasingly perform autonomous actions through external tools, leading to complex and evolving safety risks. However, existing safety testing targets expert-designed safety violations, and the corresponding outcomes are evaluated by hard-coded rules, making them costly to extend as agents evolve. To this end, we present Vera, an end-to-end automated safety testing framework that instantiates software engineering testing principles for non-deterministic agents through a three-stage, self-reinforcing pipeline. First, a literature-driven exploration continuously discovers and structures emerging risks into taxonomies of safety risks, attack methods, and tool execution environments. Second, combinatorial composition across taxonomy dimensions produces executable safety cases, each specifying a concrete safety goal, a programmatically constructed initial state, and a deterministic verification predicate grounded in observable artifacts. Third, adaptive execution runs heterogeneous agents in isolated sandboxes where a control agent steers multi-turn interaction based on runtime observations, while evidence-grounded verifiers judge outcomes from environment state and tool-call evidence rather than model self-report. We evaluate Vera on four production agent frameworks (OpenClaw, Hermes, Codex, Claude Code), revealing substantial safety weaknesses, with average attack success rates reaching 93.9\% under multi-channel attacks; we also release Vera-Bench, comprising 1600 executable safety cases spanning 124 risk categories across three execution settings. These results indicate that modular, executable testing infrastructure is essential for rigorous and maintainable safety evaluation of rapidly evolving agentic systems at scale. The code is publicly available at https://github.com/Yunhao-Feng/Vera.

AI.tldr: Vera is an automated, modular safety-testing framework for LLM-based agents that use tools, generating and executing large numbers of concrete, verifiable safety test cases to uncover evolving risks at scale.
AI.motivation: LLM agents increasingly take autonomous actions via external tools, which introduces complex and evolving safety risks that are hard to anticipate and test. Existing safety evaluations rely on a fixed set of expert-designed attacks and hand-written evaluation rules, making them brittle, costly to maintain, and unable to keep up with rapid changes in agent capabilities, tools, and environments. There is a need for a scalable, systematic, and extensible way to discover new risks, generate realistic safety tests, and evaluate agent behavior based on observable effects rather than model self-report.
AI.method: Vera implements a three-stage automated pipeline. (1) Literature-driven exploration: it continuously mines and structures emerging safety risks, organizing them into taxonomies along three dimensions—risk types, attack methods, and tool execution environments. (2) Combinatorial composition: it systematically combines elements across these taxonomy dimensions to generate executable safety cases. Each safety case specifies a concrete safety goal, a programmatically constructed initial system state, and a deterministic verification predicate based on observable artifacts (e.g., environment state, tool outputs). (3) Adaptive execution: it runs different LLM-based agents inside isolated sandboxes, where a dedicated control agent conducts multi-turn interactions and adapts based on runtime observations. Independent, evidence-grounded verifiers then judge success or failure using tool-call and environment evidence, rather than relying on the agent’s own description of its behavior.
AI.result: Using Vera, the authors evaluate four real-world agent frameworks (OpenClaw, Hermes, Codex, Claude Code). Across these systems, Vera uncovers serious safety vulnerabilities, with average attack success rates up to 93.9% under multi-channel (more complex, combined) attacks. They also construct and release Vera-Bench, a benchmark of 1600 fully executable safety cases that cover 124 distinct risk categories across three different execution settings, enabling reproducible and comparable evaluations.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that current LLM agent systems exhibit substantial, underexplored safety weaknesses when subjected to systematic, tool-based attacks. It argues that modular, executable, and scalable testing infrastructure like Vera is crucial for rigorous, maintainable safety assessment of rapidly evolving agentic systems. By grounding evaluations in observable environment and tool-call evidence and leveraging taxonomic, compositional test generation, such frameworks can better keep pace with new tools, risks, and agent architectures, and provide a foundation for ongoing safety monitoring and research.

title: Using embeddings to predict spoken word duration and pitch in Mandarin monosyllabic words
summary: Time-normalized f0 contours of Mandarin words in conversational speech have been shown to be predictable in part from their contextualized embeddings (CEs). The present study investigates whether CEs also predict spoken word duration for 7470 tokens of Mandarin monosyllabic CV words extracted from a Mandarin corpus of spontaneous speech. We show that CEs indeed are predictive for duration, above chance level, not only at the type level, but also at the level of individual tokens, as indicated by the results obtained with the type-wise and token-wise permutation baselines. We also show that the predicted durations are sufficiently precise to back-transform predicted f0 contours in [0,1] normalized time to contours on the ms time scale. The resulting predicted contours approximate empirical contours and also outperform a permutation baseline.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that contextualized embeddings can predict both duration and pitch contours of Mandarin monosyllabic words in conversational speech.
AI.motivation: Previous work showed that contextualized embeddings predict time-normalized f0 contours in Mandarin, but it was unclear whether they also carry information about segmental timing (word duration) needed to map normalized contours back to real time in milliseconds.
AI.method: They analyze 7470 tokens of monosyllabic CV Mandarin words from a spontaneous speech corpus, compute contextualized embeddings for each token/word type, and use them in predictive models of spoken word duration. They evaluate performance against type-wise and token-wise permutation baselines and test whether predicted durations can be used to back-transform normalized [0,1] f0 contours into millisecond time, comparing those contours against empirical f0 contours and permutation-based baselines.
AI.result: Contextualized embeddings predict word duration reliably above chance at both type and token levels, outperforming permutation baselines. Using the predicted durations to scale normalized f0 contours back to real time yields predicted f0 trajectories that closely approximate actual contours and are better than those obtained from permutation baselines.
AI.conclusion: Contextualized embeddings encode information not only about pitch shape but also about temporal realization (duration) of words in spontaneous Mandarin, to an extent that allows reconstruction of millisecond-scale f0 contours from normalized representations. This supports the view that CEs capture rich prosodic and temporal properties of spoken language and can be used for detailed modeling of conversational speech prosody.

title: MMIR-TCM: Memory-Integrated Multimodal Inference and Retrieval for TCM Clinical Decision Support
summary: Traditional Chinese Medicine (TCM) diagnosis, particularly through tongue inspection, faces persistent challenges in subjectivity and reproducibility. The application of multimodal artificial intelligence to TCM clinical tasks, such as syndrome differentiation and prescription generation, is significantly hampered by the semantic gap between visual tongue features and textual reasoning, as well as the lack of large-scale, standardized datasets. To address these challenges, we introduce MMIR-TCM, a novel framework that emulates the diagnostic process of TCM experts by integrating multimodal large language model(MLLM) with memory-augmented segmentation and retrieval-augmented generation (RAG). Employing a three-stage architecture, MMIR-TCM integrates a training-free Memory-SAM module for robust tongue extraction, a fine-tuned Qwen3-VL model for structured tongue diagnosis generation, and a Qwen3-based RAG component for evidence-grounded clinical decision support generation. The framework was developed and validated using MedTCM, a new large-scale multimodal dataset that we introduce specifically for advanced TCM research. To properly evaluate our framework's clinical accuracy, which existing metrics fail to capture, we also developed TDEU, a domain-specific evaluation metric incorporating semantic understanding and diagnostic importance. Our comprehensive experiments demonstrate that MMIR-TCM significantly outperforms leading models, including GPT-4o and Gemini 2.5 Flash.

AI.tldr: They propose MMIR-TCM, a multimodal AI framework plus a new dataset and evaluation metric to make tongue-based TCM diagnosis more objective and accurate, outperforming leading models.
AI.motivation: Tongue diagnosis in Traditional Chinese Medicine is highly subjective and hard to reproduce, and current multimodal AI struggles due to a semantic gap between tongue images and textual reasoning, as well as missing large, standardized datasets and suitable evaluation metrics for clinical accuracy.
AI.method: They design a three-stage multimodal framework (MMIR-TCM): (1) a training-free Memory-SAM module to reliably segment and extract tongue regions from images; (2) a fine-tuned Qwen3-VL multimodal large language model to generate structured tongue-diagnosis descriptions from the extracted images; and (3) a Qwen3-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) module that, using external knowledge, provides evidence-grounded clinical decision support. They also construct MedTCM, a large multimodal dataset of TCM tongue cases, and define a new evaluation metric, TDEU, that combines semantic similarity with the clinical importance of diagnostic elements.
AI.result: On the MedTCM dataset and related tasks, MMIR-TCM achieves substantially better performance in tongue diagnosis and clinical decision support than state-of-the-art baselines, including GPT-4o and Gemini 2.5 Flash, when evaluated with the new TDEU metric and other measures.
AI.conclusion: By combining robust tongue segmentation, tailored multimodal reasoning, RAG-based clinical support, a new MedTCM dataset, and a domain-specific TDEU evaluation metric, MMIR-TCM provides a more objective, clinically aligned AI framework for TCM tongue diagnosis and outperforms existing general-purpose multimodal models.

title: EduArt: An educational-level benchmark for evaluating art history knowledge in large language models
summary: Large language models now score near ceiling on general benchmarks, but these aggregate measures reveal little about how models behave within single disciplines. Existing art-focused evaluations rely on synthetic questions and rarely report item-level properties. This paper introduces EduArt, an educational-level benchmark for art-historical knowledge and visual reasoning in multimodal LLMs. EduArt comprises 871 human-authored questions from Italian secondary-school exercises and US Advanced Placement Art History exams, spanning two languages and seven formats from multiple choice to in-text word placement and error identification. Twelve models from six provider families were evaluated under a default answer-only condition and a motivation condition requiring written justification, and characterized using Classical Test Theory and a logistic regression isolating the effects of format, language, image presence, and model. The benchmark showed strong psychometric properties (mean discrimination 0.514, 82.3 percent good discriminators), while multiple-choice accuracy saturated near ceiling for six models, showing recognition formats alone cannot distinguish frontier models. Format was a strong independent predictor of accuracy: models exceeding 94 percent on multiple choice fell to 23.9 percent on open completion (Claude Opus 4.6) and 6.2 percent on error identification (Claude Sonnet 4.6). The motivation condition changed accuracy in a predominantly negative, family-dependent direction. These dissociations indicate that art-historical knowledge and the ability to deploy it are distinct capabilities, and that single-format benchmarks overestimate what models can reliably do. Mapping this capability profile is a precondition for responsible use of multimodal LLMs in art-historical scholarship, where tasks demand producing and manipulating content rather than selecting from fixed options.

AI.tldr: EduArt is a psychometrically grounded benchmark that evaluates multimodal LLMs’ real art-history capabilities across diverse, realistic school and exam tasks instead of just synthetic multiple-choice items.
AI.motivation: Benchmark scores for large language models are near ceiling and too coarse-grained to reveal how models behave within specific academic disciplines like art history. Existing art-related benchmarks mostly use synthetic questions, emphasize recognition-style formats such as multiple choice, and rarely analyze item-level properties or the effects of question format and language. There is a need for a realistic, educationally grounded benchmark that tests both art-historical knowledge and the ability to use it in varied, authentic tasks that resemble what students and scholars actually do.
AI.method: The authors built EduArt, a benchmark of 871 human-authored art-history questions taken from Italian secondary-school exercises and US AP Art History exams. The dataset spans two languages (Italian and English), includes visual material, and covers seven task formats (e.g., multiple choice, open completion, in-text word placement, error identification). They evaluated 12 multimodal LLMs from six model families under two conditions: an answer-only condition and a motivation condition where models had to give written justifications. Using Classical Test Theory, they examined psychometric properties such as item discrimination, and applied logistic regression to isolate how accuracy is affected by format, language, presence of images, and model family.
AI.result: EduArt displayed solid psychometric quality, with an average item discrimination of 0.514 and 82.3% of items classed as good discriminators. Many models reached near-ceiling performance on multiple-choice items, indicating that recognition-style tasks are insufficient to differentiate strong models. However, when task format changed, accuracy dropped sharply: models that scored above 94% on multiple choice fell below 24% on open completion (Claude Opus 4.6) and as low as 6.2% on error identification (Claude Sonnet 4.6). The requirement to provide motivations typically worsened performance in ways that varied by model family.
AI.conclusion: Art-historical knowledge in LLMs and the ability to flexibly apply that knowledge across realistic task formats are distinct capabilities. Single-format, recognition-heavy benchmarks substantially overestimate what models can reliably do in art history. EduArt’s multi-format, psychometrically analyzed design reveals large gaps between multiple-choice success and open-ended performance, underscoring the need for more nuanced benchmarks. Such capability profiling is essential for deploying multimodal LLMs responsibly in art-historical education and scholarship, where tasks involve producing, explaining, and manipulating content rather than merely choosing from fixed options.

title: Pre-Flight: A Benchmark for Evaluating Large Language Models on Aviation Operational Knowledge
comment: 9 pages, 1 figure, 2 tables. Benchmark available in inspect_evals (UKGovernmentBEIS/inspect_evals)
summary: Large language models (LLMs) are increasingly proposed for aviation business operations, from documentation and training generation to customer facing assistants. General purpose benchmarks do not measure whether a model reasons safely and correctly about aviation specific operational knowledge, and the high stakes, regulated nature of the domain makes that gap consequential. We present Pre-Flight, an open source benchmark of 300 multiple choice questions drawn from international standards and airport ground operations material, covering international airport ground operations, ICAO and US FAA regulations, aviation general knowledge and complex operational scenarios. Questions were authored and reviewed by practitioners with experience in air traffic management, ground operations and commercial flying. We evaluate a range of contemporary commercial and open weight models using the Inspect evaluation framework, scoring by accuracy under a standard multiple choice protocol, and we maintain the leaderboard on a rolling basis as new models are released. Against an informal expert reference of around 95%, obtained from a low sample quiz of aviation professionals at a conference, even the strongest model evaluated (released in 2026) reaches 82.7%, having improved only gradually from roughly 75% in early 2025. A substantial and persistent gap below expert level reliability therefore remains. We release the dataset, the evaluation harness and the results, and the benchmark is available within the community evaluations package distributed with inspect_evals. We argue that domain specific evaluation of this kind is a necessary precondition for responsible deployment of generative AI in non safety critical aviation operations.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces Pre-Flight, an open aviation-specific benchmark of 300 practitioner-authored multiple choice questions to assess how safely and accurately large language models reason about airport ground operations and regulations, showing that current models still lag well behind expert performance.
AI.motivation: General-purpose LLM benchmarks fail to capture whether models can reason correctly and safely about specialized, high-stakes aviation operational knowledge (e.g., regulations, ground operations, complex scenarios). Because aviation is highly regulated and safety-critical, this evaluation gap is consequential for any proposed use of LLMs in aviation-related business operations.
AI.method: The authors construct Pre-Flight, an open-source benchmark consisting of 300 multiple choice questions derived from international standards and airport ground operations materials. The questions span airport ground operations, ICAO and US FAA regulations, general aviation knowledge, and complex operational scenarios. They are authored and reviewed by practitioners with experience in air traffic management, ground operations, and commercial flying. Using the Inspect evaluation framework and a standard multiple-choice accuracy protocol, they evaluate multiple contemporary commercial and open-weight LLMs, maintaining a rolling leaderboard as new models appear.
AI.result: An informal expert baseline from aviation professionals is approximately 95% accuracy. The best-performing LLM evaluated (from 2026) achieves 82.7% accuracy, improving only marginally from about 75% in early 2025, indicating slow progress. No evaluated model reaches expert-level performance on the benchmark, and a sizeable performance gap persists across the model cohort.
AI.conclusion: Pre-Flight demonstrates that even state-of-the-art LLMs remain notably less reliable than human experts on aviation operational knowledge and regulatory reasoning. The authors conclude that such domain-specific benchmarks are essential prerequisites for responsible deployment of generative AI in non–safety-critical aviation contexts, and they support this by releasing the dataset, evaluation harness, and ongoing results as part of the inspect_evals community evaluations package.

title: OpenSafeIntent: Evaluating Intent-Calibrated Safe Completion Across Dual-Use Prompt Sets
summary: Safe completion requires models to provide useful assistance without enabling harm, but this behavior is difficult to evaluate with isolated prompts. We introduce OpenSafeIntent, a benchmark of controlled prompt-sets that vary intent while holding the underlying task fixed. Each datapoint contains benign, dual-use, and malicious variants of the same task. This design lets us evaluate whether models calibrate assistance across intent shifts, rather than merely appearing safe on average. Across a broad model suite, we find that prompt-level safety hides important failures: models often fail to remain safe across matched intent variants, dual-use behavior is brittle under paraphrase, high-level answers on risky topics are not reliably safe, and responses that reframe ambiguous requests into safer tasks are substantially less likely to cross the safety boundary. Our results suggest that safe completion should be evaluated as intent-calibrated behavior over controlled task variants, not as a single safety-helpfulness tradeoff over independent prompts.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces OpenSafeIntent, a benchmark to test whether language models adjust their level of assistance based on user intent while performing the same task.
AI.motivation: Existing safety evaluations typically use isolated prompts, which can make models look safe on average while hiding failures when user intent changes from benign to malicious for the same underlying task. The authors want a more precise way to test whether models actually calibrate their help according to user intent.
AI.method: They build OpenSafeIntent, a dataset where each data point is a set of prompts that share the same underlying task but differ only in intent: benign, dual-use, or malicious, including paraphrased versions. They then evaluate a broad range of language models on how their responses change or fail to change across these matched variants and how often they cross predefined safety boundaries.
AI.result: The evaluation shows that safety measured at the single-prompt level hides systematic issues: models frequently fail to remain safe across matched intent variants; safety behavior on dual-use prompts is fragile to paraphrasing; high-level responses to risky topics are often not truly safe; and when models try to “reframe” ambiguous prompts into safer tasks, they are indeed less likely to violate safety, but this comes with its own tradeoffs.
AI.conclusion: Safe completion should be conceptualized and evaluated as intent-calibrated behavior across controlled variants of the same task, instead of as a simple global tradeoff between safety and helpfulness on independent prompts. Benchmarks must explicitly test how models respond to shifts in user intent while holding the task constant to uncover subtle but important safety failures.

title: Actual causality in fault trees
summary: Fault trees are a widely used as effective risk models for complex systems, answering the question "what can go wrong?", especially through minimal cut set analysis. We study fault trees from the perspective of Halpern & Pearl's theory of actual causality. This allows us to use fault trees to answer the question "why has it gone wrong?", which is fundamental to failure diagnostics. We give a complete classification of each of the different notions of actual causality in terms of the fault tree's graph structure and logical structure, and show how minimal cut sets give rise to actual causes.

AI.tldr: Connects fault tree analysis with Halpern & Pearl’s actual causality to explain not just what can go wrong but why it went wrong in concrete failures.
AI.motivation: Fault trees are standard tools to analyse system risk and identify combinations of component failures that can lead to system failure (minimal cut sets), but they mainly answer predictive or hypothetical questions (what can go wrong?) rather than diagnostic questions about specific observed failures (why did this failure occur?). There is a gap between risk analysis and causal explanation in failure diagnostics.
AI.method: Adopt Halpern & Pearl’s formal theory of actual causality and apply it to the structural and logical representation of fault trees. Provide a complete characterization that maps different formal notions of actual causality to patterns in the fault tree’s graph and Boolean structure. Show how minimal cut sets within the fault tree correspond to actual causes under these causality definitions.
AI.result: A structural and logical classification is derived that, for any given fault tree, specifies which events/combinations qualify as actual causes according to the various Halpern–Pearl causality notions. It is shown that minimal cut sets are systematically related to actual causes—under precise conditions, they instantiate (sets of) actual causes for the top-level failure.
AI.conclusion: By enriching fault tree analysis with a rigorous theory of actual causality, the paper turns traditional "what can go wrong?" risk models into tools that can also answer "why did it go wrong?" for observed failures, thereby supporting more principled and automated failure diagnostics based on the existing fault tree structure.

title: SPLIT: Cross-Lingual Empathy and Cultural Grounding in English and Ukrainian LLM Responses
comment: 19 pages, 5 figures, 3 tables. Benchmark paper introducing SPLIT for evaluating empathy, linguistic naturalness, and cultural grounding in English and Ukrainian LLM responses
summary: Large Language Models are increasingly deployed in emotional-support contexts and crisis-related situations. Nevertheless, their cross-lingual abilities in these circumstances remain underexplored. Existing benchmarks emphasize multilingual performance but rarely examine crisis-related empathy and cultural grounding in low-to-mid-resource languages. We introduce SPLIT, a 500-prompt benchmark designed to evaluate LLM consistency in generating emotionally grounded responses across five categories: Stress, Panic, Loneliness, Internal Displacement, and Tension. We evaluate three technically diverse LLMs across three dimensions: Empathetic Accuracy, Linguistic Naturalness, and Contextual & Cultural Grounding. The framework aims to assess and compare the quality of LLM responses in both English and Ukrainian languages, as well as to explore the reliability of the LLM-as-a-jury paradigm. Our findings reveal that Gemini-2.5-Flash and LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct degrade when transitioning to Ukrainian, while DeepSeek-V3 remains comparatively stable within our benchmark. We additionally find that human and AI evaluators agree weakly on empathy and naturalness but diverge on cultural grounding. We further argue that producing Ukrainian text is not equivalent to producing Ukrainian emotional support. Our findings may assist in the future development of more culturally tailored benchmark designs, as well as encourage a stronger emphasis on human-centered evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SPLIT, a 500-prompt benchmark to test how consistently large language models provide emotionally grounded support in English and Ukrainian across five crisis-related categories, revealing cross-lingual performance degradation and gaps between human and AI evaluation, especially for cultural grounding.
AI.motivation: As LLMs are increasingly used for emotional support and crisis scenarios, there is little understanding of how well they transfer these abilities across languages, particularly low-to-mid-resource ones. Existing multilingual benchmarks rarely focus on empathy, emotional grounding, or cultural specificity in such contexts. The authors aim to fill this gap by systematically testing models’ emotional-support capabilities in English versus Ukrainian and examining whether automatic, model-based evaluation aligns with human judgments.
AI.method: The authors construct SPLIT, a 500-prompt benchmark grouped into five crisis-related categories: Stress, Panic, Loneliness, Internal Displacement, and Tension. They evaluate three technically diverse LLMs in both English and Ukrainian along three dimensions: (1) Empathetic Accuracy, (2) Linguistic Naturalness, and (3) Contextual & Cultural Grounding. They also use an LLM-as-a-jury evaluation setup and compare AI-based scores with those from human annotators to measure agreement levels across the three dimensions.
AI.result: Gemini-2.5-Flash and LLaMA-3.3-70B-Instruct exhibit noticeable performance degradation when moving from English to Ukrainian on the SPLIT benchmark, while DeepSeek-V3 maintains relatively stable performance across languages. Agreement between human evaluators and AI judges is weak for empathy and naturalness and even more misaligned for cultural grounding, indicating that current automatic evaluation methods struggle to capture culturally nuanced emotional support quality.
AI.conclusion: SPLIT exposes important cross-lingual weaknesses in LLMs’ emotional-support capabilities, especially when moving to Ukrainian, and demonstrates that simply generating text in a target language does not ensure culturally appropriate emotional support. The misalignment between human and AI evaluations—particularly around cultural grounding—suggests that LLM-as-a-jury approaches are unreliable for this kind of assessment. The authors call for more culturally aware, human-centered benchmarks and evaluation methods to guide the development of emotionally competent multilingual LLMs.

title: CLAP: Closed-Loop Training, Evaluation, and Release Control for Domain Agent Post-training
comment: 6 pages, 1 figure. Accepted to CRAE 2026; to appear in SPIE Proceedings. Best Poster Award
summary: Domain agents often face noisy business data, uncertain post-training gains, offline/application mismatch, and adapter-release risk. This paper presents CLAP (Closed-Loop Agent Post-training), a closed-loop method that converts business data into structured SFT samples, decision-preference samples, holdout sets, risk diagnostics, and release-gate records. CLAP combines data validation, target/evidence normalization, reward/KL diagnosis, offline gates, and application-chain replay to decide whether an adapter is suitable for the target application chain. On five anonymized manufacturing-scenario batches, QLoRA-style LoRA-SFT yields modest average gains: overall score increases by 0.0098, pass rate by 0.0240, and evidence accuracy by 0.0280, while hallucination and wrong facts decrease. Yet only 3 of 5 batches improve, some batches regress, and GRPO exposes high KL risks. Application-chain replay further shows that RAG is necessary for factual extraction; under the same 3B backbone and 100 replay cases, an application-RAG-oriented LoRA-SFT adapter improves value, core fields, and answer-evidence doc/page matching over base+RAG, but increases latency. These results support managing domain-agent post-training through an integrated data-training-evaluation-release loop rather than relying on training completion or a single offline score.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces CLAP, a closed-loop framework for safely and effectively post-training domain agents, turning raw business data into structured training, evaluation, and release artifacts and showing that naive post-training can be risky and inconsistent.
AI.motivation: Domain agents trained on business data often suffer from noisy labels, uncertain real-world benefits, mismatch between offline metrics and production behavior, and risks when releasing adapters. The authors want to systematically manage these issues instead of relying on ad-hoc training and single offline scores to decide deployment readiness.
AI.method: They propose CLAP (Closed-Loop Agent Post-training), which converts business logs into structured SFT data, decision-preference data, holdout evaluation sets, and risk diagnostics. CLAP includes data validation and normalization, reward and KL (divergence) diagnostics, offline gating criteria, and application-chain replay to test how a trained adapter behaves in the target workflow (including RAG chains) before release. They apply QLoRA-style LoRA-based SFT and GRPO-based methods on several manufacturing-domain batches and evaluate across multiple metrics and risk indicators.
AI.result: Across five anonymized manufacturing batches, LoRA-SFT yields small average improvements in overall score (+0.0098), pass rate (+0.0240), and evidence accuracy (+0.0280), while reducing hallucinations and factual errors. However, only 3 of 5 batches improve and some regress, and GRPO shows high KL divergence risk. Application-chain replay demonstrates that RAG is essential for factual extraction. With a 3B backbone and 100 replay cases, an application-RAG-oriented LoRA-SFT adapter outperforms base+RAG on business-value and factual-matching metrics but with higher latency.
AI.conclusion: Post-training domain agents is non-trivial: naive use of noisy business data and a single offline metric can lead to regressions and safety risks. CLAP’s integrated data–training–evaluation–release loop provides a more reliable way to judge whether an adapter is suitable for deployment in a given application chain, especially when RAG is involved. The results suggest organizations should adopt closed-loop processes with diagnostics, gates, and chain-level replay rather than treating training completion as a sufficient release signal.

title: HaloGuard 1.0: An Open Weights Constitutional Classifier for Multilingual AI Safety
summary: We present HaloGuard 1.0, an open-weights implementation of the constitutional-classifier paradigm for input safety. It achieves state-of-the-art performance on English and multilingual prompt-safety benchmarks at roughly one-tenth the model size of current leading open guard models. The safety constitution is the organising structure of the corpus: a natural-language constitution of 46 policies and 2,940 subcategories drives synthetic data generation, with exhaustive one-to-one paired counterfactuals that hold topic and vocabulary fixed while flipping intent, a two-tier harmless design that separately targets boundary and baseline false positives (FPs), and balanced multilingual materialisation across 46 languages that treats language as a surface form appearing on both sides of the boundary rather than as an adversarial signal. Across seven prompt-safety benchmarks, HaloGuard 1.0-0.8B attains the best average F1 (90.9) of any open guard we evaluate, outperforming baselines up to 27B parameters (over 30 times larger) while holding false-positive rate (FPR) to 4.3 and false-negative rate (FNR) to 9.5. The HaloGuard 1.0-4B variant reaches average F1 of 92.1 and FPR of 3.5, spending its extra capacity on precision rather than recall. A structured adjudication of the remaining failures indicates that most apparent missed-harm cases are benchmark mislabels rather than genuine model misses. An always-on adversarial red-teaming protocol continuously hardens the guard against both content-level and agentic attacks. We release the models as open weights.

AI.tldr: HaloGuard 1.0 is a compact, open-weight safeguard model that sets state-of-the-art results on English and multilingual prompt-safety benchmarks using a constitution-driven data pipeline and balanced multilingual training.
