AI.motivation: Existing open guardrail/constitutional-classifier models for prompt safety are large, less efficient, and often struggle with multilingual robustness and false positives. The authors want a smaller, open, high-precision safety classifier that can reliably distinguish harmful from harmless prompts, including in many languages, and that is resilient to adversarial attacks and benchmark labeling noise.
AI.method: They design a detailed safety constitution with 46 policies and 2,940 subcategories, and use it to generate a large synthetic training corpus. Each harmful example is paired with a counterfactual harmless example that preserves topic and wording but flips intent, helping the model learn fine-grained distinctions. They introduce a two-tier harmless design aimed at reducing both boundary and baseline false positives. The dataset is carefully balanced across 46 languages so language itself is not a signal of harm. They train smaller (0.8B and 4B parameter) classifiers on this data, continuously improved via an adversarial red-teaming protocol that targets content-level and agentic attacks. They then benchmark against multiple open guard models on seven prompt-safety datasets.
AI.result: The 0.8B-parameter HaloGuard model achieves an average F1 score of 90.9 across seven safety benchmarks, outperforming much larger open guard models up to 27B parameters, with low false-positive (4.3) and false-negative (9.5) rates. The 4B-parameter variant further improves average F1 to 92.1 and reduces FPR to 3.5, trading some recall for higher precision. Their analysis of errors shows many supposed misses are actually mislabeled benchmark items rather than real safety failures.
AI.conclusion: Constitution-driven synthetic data with balanced multilingual coverage enables small, open-weight safety classifiers to match or surpass the performance of much larger guard models while maintaining low error rates. HaloGuard 1.0 demonstrates that efficient, open, and robust prompt-safety classifiers are feasible, and its ongoing adversarial red-teaming and open-weight release provide a strong foundation for practical deployment and further research.

title: Safety Targeted Embedding Exploit via Refinement
summary: Safety training for large language models (LLMs) is conducted predominantly in English, leaving uncertain how well safety mechanisms generalize to low-resource languages and mixed-language code-switching. We show that this creates an epistemic gap in which models confidently generate harmful responses for inputs that fall outside the distribution of their safety training. To study this phenomenon, we introduce STEER (Safety Targeted Embedding Exploit via Refinement), a gradient-guided attack that identifies words contributing most strongly to the model's refusal behavior and iteratively translates them into low-resource languages to suppress refusal while preserving harmful intent. Across six open-source 8B-parameter models, STEER achieves attack success rates of up to 93.0% on JailbreakBench and 96.7% on AdvBench, outperforming random code-switching and Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG). The resulting prompts also transfer to GPT-4o-mini, achieving a 35.5% attack success rate without requiring access to the target model, suggesting that the underlying weakness is not specific to a single architecture. These findings demonstrate that safety mechanisms aligned primarily on English cannot be assumed to generalize across multilingual inputs. We argue that improving multilingual safety requires broader coverage during alignment and mechanisms that explicitly detect and abstain on out-of-distribution inputs.

AI.tldr: The paper shows that safety alignment trained mostly in English fails for low-resource and code-switched languages. They propose STEER, a gradient-based multilingual jailbreak method that systematically weakens refusal behavior by translating key safety-triggering words into low-resource languages, achieving very high attack success and transferability, and arguing for multilingual-aware safety training.
AI.motivation: Most LLM safety training is done in English, but users interact in many languages and often mix them. It is unclear whether English-centric safety mechanisms generalize to low-resource languages or code-switching, raising concerns that models may still produce harmful content in these settings. The authors want to rigorously test and exploit this weakness to quantify the risk and understand how safety alignment fails in multilingual contexts.
AI.method: They introduce STEER (Safety Targeted Embedding Exploit via Refinement), a gradient-guided jailbreak attack. STEER identifies which input tokens most contribute to the model’s refusal behavior via gradients in the embedding space, then iteratively translates these high-impact safety-triggering words into low-resource languages while keeping the underlying harmful request unchanged. They evaluate STEER against six open-source 8B models on JailbreakBench and AdvBench, compare it to random code-switching and Greedy Coordinate Gradient (GCG), and also test transferability by applying the crafted prompts to GPT-4o-mini without white-box access.
AI.result: STEER substantially suppresses refusal behavior and elicits harmful responses with very high attack success: up to 93.0% on JailbreakBench and 96.7% on AdvBench across six 8B open-source models, outperforming random code-switching and GCG. The prompts crafted on open models also partially transfer to GPT-4o-mini, achieving a 35.5% attack success rate in a black-box setting, indicating that the identified vulnerability is not confined to one model family or architecture.
AI.conclusion: Safety alignment that focuses mainly on English leaves a significant epistemic and practical gap: models remain vulnerable to multilingual and code-switched jailbreaks, especially involving low-resource languages. The demonstrated STEER attack exploits this gap systematically, revealing that refusal mechanisms do not robustly generalize across languages. The authors conclude that robust multilingual safety requires broader, language-diverse coverage in alignment and the development of mechanisms that can detect and abstain from out-of-distribution or unfamiliar linguistic patterns, rather than assuming English-tuned safety will generalize automatically.

title: Unlocking Speech-Text Compositional Powers: Instruction-Following Speech Language Models without Instruction Tuning
summary: Instruction tuning for speech language models (SLMs) is substantially more challenging than for text-based large language models (LLMs), as it requires learning a new modality and a wide range of speech-specific instructions in addition to those supported by text LLMs. Existing SLM training approaches largely replicate the text LLM training paradigm by synthesizing large-scale speech pre-training and instruction-tuning datasets. However, this strategy is difficult to scale, since speech sequences are significantly longer than text sequences. In this paper, we propose SpeechCombine, an instruction-following speech language model trained without any instruction tuning, using only a single round of speech pre-training on 30k hours of data. Starting from a text LLM base model, we perform continuous pre-training on speech utterances to obtain a speech-adapted model, and then directly combine its weights with the weight difference between the instruction-tuned and base versions of the text LLM. Our results show that this simple combination strategy not only preserves the knowledge and capabilities of the original text LLM, but also effectively transfers them to the speech domain. These findings suggest a new direction for SLM training that avoids reliance on massive speech data.

AI.tldr: SpeechCombine is a speech language model that follows instructions without explicit instruction-tuning on speech, by combining a speech-pretrained model with the instruction-tuned weight delta of a text LLM.
AI.motivation: Instruction tuning for speech language models is hard and expensive because speech is a different, longer modality and requires many speech-specific instructions, making large-scale speech instruction datasets difficult to scale.
AI.method: Start from a base text LLM, continuously pre-train it on 30k hours of speech to obtain a speech-adapted model, then merge this model’s weights with the difference between the instruction-tuned and base text LLM weights, effectively transferring instruction-following abilities to the speech model without separate speech instruction tuning.
AI.result: The combined model preserves the text LLM’s knowledge and instruction-following ability and successfully transfers these capabilities to the speech domain, performing well without needing dedicated speech instruction-tuning data.
AI.conclusion: A simple weight-combination strategy can yield an instruction-following speech language model using only speech pre-training, reducing reliance on massive speech instruction datasets and suggesting a more scalable path for training SLMs.

title: CamoNAS: Neural Architecture Search for Enhanced Camouflaged Object Detection
comment: Published in The Visual Computer. Author manuscript version
summary: Camouflaged Object Detection (COD) aims to locate and segment objects that blend into their surroundings, presenting challenges due to weak edge cues and ill-defined boundaries. Traditional COD models rely on hand-designed architectures and multi-scale feature fusion, which are often guided by intuition rather than systematic search. This paper introduces CamoNAS, a frequency-aware multi-resolution Neural Architecture Search (NAS) framework for COD. CamoNAS automatically searches both cell-level operations and network-level downsampling paths, forming a hierarchical search space tailored to detect camouflaged objects. Additionally, it adopts an RGB frequency dual-stream architecture, where a learnable wavelet transform complements the RGB spatial stream. CamoNAS achieves state-of-the-art performance on four COD benchmarks (CAMO, COD10K, NC4K, CHAMELEON), highlighting the effectiveness of NAS for COD. Our code is available at https://github.com/rendaweiSIMIT/CamoNAS.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes CamoNAS, a NAS-based, frequency-aware, multi-resolution architecture that achieves SOTA in camouflaged object detection.
AI.motivation: Camouflaged object detection is difficult due to weak edges and unclear boundaries, and existing models rely on manually designed, intuition-driven architectures and multi-scale fusions, which may be suboptimal. There is a need for a principled, automated way to design COD architectures that better capture subtle visual cues.
AI.method: The authors propose CamoNAS, a Neural Architecture Search framework specifically tailored for COD. It searches a hierarchical space including both cell-level operations and network-level downsampling paths to discover effective multi-resolution architectures. The model uses an RGB frequency dual-stream design, where a learnable wavelet transform branch processes frequency information in parallel with a standard RGB spatial stream, enabling frequency-aware feature learning.
AI.result: CamoNAS attains state-of-the-art performance on four established COD benchmarks: CAMO, COD10K, NC4K, and CHAMELEON, outperforming previous hand-designed methods.
AI.conclusion: Automated architecture design via NAS, combined with a frequency-aware dual-stream structure, is highly effective for camouflaged object detection, suggesting that task-specific NAS can surpass hand-crafted COD architectures and that exploiting frequency information is beneficial for detecting camouflaged objects.

title: Challenges and Recommendations for LLMs-as-a-Judge in Multilingual Settings and Low-Resource Languages
summary: LLM-as-a-Judge has become the dominant evaluation paradigm for many natural language generation tasks, due to shortcomings of conventional metrics and high correlations with human judgment, albeit mostly in English. There are now attempts to extend LLM-as-a-Judge to multilingual settings including low-resource languages. However, LLMs have limited proficiency in low-resource languages, and there is often no adequate human validation in these settings. To highlight the scope of the problem and current practices, we explore the use of LLM-as-a-Judge evaluators in ACL Anthology papers focusing on multilingual settings and low-resource languages across a diverse set of tasks. Out of 650 papers mentioning LLM-as-a-judge, only 33 of them focus on low-resource or multilingual settings. Our in-depth analysis of these papers indicates inconsistent evaluation outcomes, a tendency to overtrust LLM judgments in multilingual settings, and the widespread reliance on a single judge model per study. To help the NLP community further, we conclude with recommendations about how to use LLM-as-a-Judge in multilingual and low-resource settings.

AI.tldr: The paper examines how LLM-as-a-Judge is being used to evaluate multilingual and low-resource NLP tasks, finding inconsistent and over-trusted evaluations, and offers recommendations for more reliable use in these settings.
AI.motivation: LLM-as-a-Judge is popular for evaluating natural language generation because traditional metrics are weak and LLM judgments correlate well with humans, but this evidence is mostly for English. For low-resource and multilingual settings, LLM proficiency is lower and human validation is scarce, raising concerns that current evaluation practices may be unreliable and under-scrutinized.
AI.method: The authors survey ACL Anthology papers that use LLM-as-a-Judge, identifying 650 papers and filtering to 33 that focus on multilingual or low-resource settings. They conduct an in-depth qualitative and quantitative analysis of how LLM judges are used in these 33 papers, examining aspects such as evaluation consistency, dependence on a single judge model, and the extent of human validation or cross-checking of LLM judgments.
AI.result: Among 650 papers using LLM-as-a-Judge, only 33 address multilingual or low-resource contexts. Within these 33 papers, the authors find inconsistent evaluation practices and outcomes, a prevalent tendency to over-rely on LLM assessments without sufficient human or multi-model validation, and a dominant practice of using a single LLM judge model per study. These issues suggest that current evaluation results in such settings may be less reliable than reported.
AI.conclusion: LLM-as-a-Judge is being quickly adopted in multilingual and low-resource NLP without sufficient attention to its limitations in those languages, leading to potentially unreliable evaluation results. The authors recommend more cautious and robust usage, including better human validation, using multiple judge models, and adopting stricter experimental protocols when applying LLM-as-a-Judge beyond high-resource languages like English.

title: SkillCoach: Self-Evolving Rubrics for Evaluating and Enhancing Agentic Skill-Use
summary: Skills are becoming a reusable operational layer for LLM agents, encoding SOPs, domain rules, tool workflows, scripts, and validation routines. In realistic skill repositories, overlapping skills make reliable skill-use difficult. Final verifier success is too coarse for both evaluation and training, since an agent may pass through trial and error while selecting distractor skills, skipping required steps, composing workflows incorrectly or omitting final checks. We introduce SkillCoach, a self-evolving rubric framework for evaluating and enhancing agentic skill-use. SkillCoach derives skill-grounded process rubrics from real rollouts and evaluates trajectories along four dimensions: skill selection, skill following, skill composition, and skill-grounded reflection. It keeps the external verifier as a separate outcome signal, allowing process quality to be distinguished from accidental task success. The evolved rubrics further serve as process supervision for selecting high-quality training trajectories. Experiments show that evolved rubrics substantially improve evaluation quality, expose failures hidden by final accuracy, and provide stronger supervision signals than outcome-only filtering for enhancing agentic skill-use.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces SkillCoach, a rubric-based framework to evaluate and improve how LLM agents use reusable skills, going beyond simple final-task success metrics.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents rely on skill repositories (SOPs, domain rules, workflows, scripts, checks), but these repositories often contain overlapping or redundant skills. This makes it hard for agents to reliably choose and execute the right skills. Current evaluation focuses mainly on whether the final answer passes an external verifier, which is too coarse: agents might succeed by trial-and-error while misusing skills, skipping steps, or composing workflows incorrectly. This lack of fine-grained process evaluation hurts both diagnosis and training of better agents.
AI.method: The authors propose SkillCoach, a self-evolving rubric framework. It (1) derives skill-grounded process rubrics from real agent rollouts; (2) uses these rubrics to evaluate each trajectory along four dimensions: skill selection (choosing appropriate skills), skill following (adhering to each skill’s procedure), skill composition (correctly chaining and integrating multiple skills), and skill-grounded reflection (using skills to reflect and self-correct); and (3) keeps the external verifier as a separate outcome signal rather than merging it with process scores. The rubrics are iteratively evolved based on data, and then used as process-level supervision to select high-quality trajectories for training.
AI.result: Empirical experiments show that the rubrics learned by SkillCoach lead to much higher-quality evaluations than relying on final-task accuracy alone. They reveal failure modes that are invisible when looking only at pass/fail outcomes and provide finer-grained diagnostics of agent behavior. When used for data selection and training, these process-based signals outperform outcome-only filtering in improving how agents use skills.
AI.conclusion: SkillCoach enables a more structured, process-oriented way to evaluate and train LLM agents in skill-rich settings. By separating process quality from final-task success and using self-evolving, skill-grounded rubrics, it uncovers hidden weaknesses in agentic skill-use and supplies stronger supervision, leading to more reliable and effective skill-based agents.

title: BamiBERT: A New BERT-based Language Model for Vietnamese
summary: In this paper, we introduce BamiBERT, a new BERT-based pre-trained language model for Vietnamese that addresses key limitations of PhoBERT -- the current de facto Vietnamese text encoder. Trained from scratch on a 129GB corpus of general-domain Vietnamese text for 20 epochs, BamiBERT supports an extended context length of up to 2048 tokens and operates directly on raw input, eliminating the need for external word segmentation. Across 8 Vietnamese benchmarks, it achieves the best score on 11 of 15 metrics and the second-best on 3 others, setting a new state of the art among "base"-sized Vietnamese encoders and demonstrating strong cross-domain generalization. We release BamiBERT at: https://huggingface.co/Qualcomm-AI-Research/BamiBERT

AI.tldr: They propose BamiBERT, a new Vietnamese BERT model that outperforms PhoBERT on most benchmarks and supports longer context without word segmentation.
AI.motivation: Existing Vietnamese encoders like PhoBERT have limitations: they typically need an external word segmenter and have shorter context windows, which can hurt practicality and performance, especially for longer texts and cross-domain tasks. There is a need for a stronger, more flexible general-purpose Vietnamese language encoder.
AI.method: They pre-train a BERT-style transformer model from scratch on a large 129GB Vietnamese corpus for 20 epochs. The model uses subword tokenization to work directly on raw text (no external word segmentation) and is architected to handle sequences up to 2048 tokens. They then evaluate it on 8 Vietnamese benchmarks covering multiple tasks and compare against PhoBERT and other base-size encoders.
AI.result: On 8 Vietnamese benchmarks, BamiBERT obtains the best performance on 11 of 15 metrics and the second-best on 3 others, surpassing PhoBERT and other comparable models. It also shows strong cross-domain generalization in these evaluations.
AI.conclusion: BamiBERT is a new state-of-the-art base-sized Vietnamese text encoder, offering better accuracy than existing models like PhoBERT, longer context handling (up to 2048 tokens), and simpler usage by removing the need for external word segmentation. The model is released publicly on Hugging Face for community use.

title: Spec-AUF: Accept-Until-Fail Training under Train-Inference Misalignment for Masked Block Drafters
summary: Speculative decoding accelerates autoregressive generation by drafting a block of tokens that the target model verifies left-to-right, committing only the longest accepted prefix. Block (DLM-style) drafters predict the whole block in parallel, which is fast but trained with a full-block cross-entropy that supervises every position against the gold continuation -- even though inference discards every token after the first rejection. Recent acceptance-aware objectives patch this by reweighting the full-block loss; we instead use teacher-forced learning as a motivation for how supervision should concentrate on the accepted prefix. A mask-only block drafter has no input-side channel for gold-prefix conditioning, so AUF approximates that prefix-sensitive supervision on the loss side by keeping the cross-entropy support only through the drafter's first predicted failure. AUF is a single, detached change to the CE support -- no auxiliary objective, no verifier rollouts, and no change to the inference pipeline or the exactness contract. Within fixed drafter backbones and serving settings on Qwen3-8B, AUF raises the DFlash drafter's average emitted length $τ$, averaged over six benchmarks, from 2.40 to 2.61, with a gain on every benchmark, and transfers to Domino's two-branch head (2.56 to 2.68). Two findings sharpen the picture: the decay-only baseline reaches higher token accuracy on the shared block mask yet decodes worse, and on DFlash, once AUF truncates the support, the standard exponential position-decay weighting becomes empirically inert.

AI.tldr: The paper proposes AUF, a modified training scheme for speculative decoding drafters that focuses loss only on the prefix that would be accepted at inference, leading to better decoding efficiency without changing inference or model architecture.
AI.motivation: Speculative decoding uses a fast drafter to propose blocks of tokens that a larger target model verifies, but standard block-wise cross-entropy trains all positions equally even though tokens after the first rejected one are never used. Recent fixes reweight the loss but still supervise unreachable positions. The authors want a training objective that better matches the actual acceptance behavior and improves emitted length (throughput) without adding complexity to inference.
AI.method: They introduce AUF (Accepted-Until-Failure), which modifies the cross-entropy loss support for block-style drafters. During training, the loss is computed only up to the drafter’s first predicted failure position, mimicking teacher-forced supervision on the prefix that would be accepted at inference. AUF is applied as a simple masking/truncation of the CE support, without adding auxiliary losses, verifier rollouts, or changing the inference procedure. They evaluate AUF on Qwen3-8B with DFlash and Domino drafters under fixed backbones and serving conditions.
AI.result: On Qwen3-8B, AUF increases the drafter’s average emitted length τ across six benchmarks for the DFlash drafter from 2.40 to 2.61, improving every benchmark, and also improves Domino’s two-branch head from 2.56 to 2.68. They observe that a decay-only baseline can obtain higher token accuracy on the masked positions yet leads to worse decoding performance, and that for DFlash, once AUF truncates the loss support, the usual exponential position-decay weighting has no measurable effect.
AI.conclusion: Focusing cross-entropy supervision only on the prefix that would be accepted during speculative decoding (AUF) improves effective decoding efficiency without modifying the inference pipeline or exactness guarantees. Traditional accuracy metrics over the full block mask can be misleading for speculative drafters, and position-decay weighting becomes largely irrelevant once loss support is properly truncated to accepted tokens.

title: CheckRLM: Effective Knowledge-Thought Coherence Checking in Retrieval-Augmented Reasoning
summary: Reasoning Language Models (RLMs) have significantly improved performance on complex tasks by extending the reasoning chain. However, these chains are prone to containing factual errors, particularly in knowledge-intensive tasks. To address this issue, we propose CheckRLM, a framework that improves the reliability of the reasoning process through Retrieval-Augmented Generation (RAG) by timely checking and correcting factual errors. Specifically, CheckRLM extracts factual claims from the reasoning chain to identify and localize subtle knowledge inconsistencies during inference. Upon detection of errors, a refinement mechanism performs minimal-cost yet precise corrections by leveraging external knowledge, ensuring coherence between the reasoning chain and correct knowledge. Extensive experiments demonstrate that CheckRLM substantially outperforms existing baselines, exhibiting a strong capability to mitigate error accumulation in long-horizon reasoning with lower costs. The code and data are available at https://github.com/AI9Stars/CheckRLM.

AI.tldr: CheckRLM is a framework that makes reasoning language models more reliable by detecting and correcting factual errors in their step-by-step reasoning using retrieval-augmented generation.
AI.motivation: Reasoning language models solve complex, multi-step problems but their long reasoning chains often contain subtle factual errors, especially in knowledge-heavy tasks. These errors can accumulate and degrade final performance. There is a need for a method that can monitor, detect, and fix such factual inconsistencies during inference without incurring excessive computational cost.
AI.method: CheckRLM integrates Retrieval-Augmented Generation into the reasoning process of language models. It (1) extracts explicit factual claims from each step or segment of the model’s reasoning chain, (2) checks these claims against external knowledge sources to identify inconsistencies or errors, and (3) invokes a refinement mechanism that applies minimal, targeted corrections to the reasoning chain. The corrections are designed to be precise and low-cost while maintaining coherence of the overall reasoning process.
AI.result: Experiments on knowledge-intensive and long-horizon reasoning tasks show that CheckRLM outperforms existing baselines. It more effectively mitigates error accumulation along extended reasoning chains and does so with lower computational or annotation cost than prior approaches.
AI.conclusion: By continuously checking and minimally correcting factual claims in the reasoning chain via retrieval-augmented generation, CheckRLM significantly improves the reliability and accuracy of reasoning language models on complex, knowledge-intensive tasks. The approach demonstrates that active, fine-grained verification during inference is an effective way to control error propagation in long-form reasoning.

title: Rethinking Complexity Metrics for LLM-Integrated Applications: Beyond Source Code
summary: LLM-integrated applications blend natural language prompts with program code, and much of their runtime behavior originates in the prompt layer rather than in the code itself. Existing complexity metrics, however, operate solely at the code level and therefore overlook this behavioral logic entirely. We present HECATE, the first tool designed to assess complexity in both the prompt and code layers of such applications. Central to HECATE is Prompt-as-Specification, a Hoare-logic-inspired formalism that interprets every prompt as a specification of intended behavior. Grounded in 25 complexity dimensions identified across published taxonomies, the tool generates 52 candidate metrics. We assess each metric against 118 components collected from 18 open-source repositories, relying on maintenance activity derived from version history as an empirical proxy for complexity, and discard any metric that loses significance once code size is accounted for. Only ten metrics withstand this test. Seven belong to our newly introduced set; rather than measuring sheer volume, each tallies structurally distinct elements, such as LLM call sites, memory attributes, and prompt templates, an attribute we call structural breadth. Of the three surviving conventional metrics, RFC exhibits a similar breadth-oriented character, while Halstead N and V survive only as a residual effect of size; our top-performing metrics exceed all three. Crucially, the prompt-layer metrics retain significance even when the strongest code-level metric is added as a covariate, establishing prompt complexity as a dimension in its own right. A final validation on 20 components spanning six held-out repositories shows that the two best-performing metrics continue to predict maintenance effort, supporting their generalizability beyond the training set.

AI.tldr: Introduces HECATE, a tool with new metrics that measure complexity in both prompts and code of LLM-integrated applications, showing that prompt-layer structural complexity predicts maintenance effort beyond traditional code metrics.
AI.motivation: LLM-based software systems encode much of their behavior in natural language prompts rather than in program code, but existing software complexity metrics only measure code. This mismatch means we lack proper ways to assess, compare, and manage the true complexity and maintainability of LLM-integrated applications.
AI.method: The authors propose Prompt-as-Specification, a Hoare-logic-inspired view that treats prompts as behavior specifications, then derive 52 candidate complexity metrics across 25 literature-grounded dimensions for both prompt and code layers. They empirically evaluate these metrics on 118 components from 18 open-source LLM application repositories, use version-history-based maintenance activity as a proxy for complexity, statistically control for code size, and discard metrics that lose significance. They then validate the best metrics on 20 components from six held-out repositories.
AI.result: Out of 52 candidate metrics, only ten remain significant predictors of maintenance effort after controlling for code size. Seven of these ten are newly introduced metrics measuring structural breadth in both prompts and code—e.g., counts of distinct LLM call sites, memory attributes, and prompt templates. Among traditional metrics, RFC remains predictive due to similar breadth orientation, while Halstead N and V appear to reflect size more than true complexity. Prompt-layer metrics retain predictive power even when the strongest code-level metric is included as a covariate. In a held-out validation set, the two top-performing metrics still predict maintenance effort, indicating robustness and generalizability.
AI.conclusion: Prompt-layer complexity is a real, measurable dimension in LLM-integrated applications and is not captured by traditional code-only metrics. Structural-breadth metrics, especially those incorporating prompt-level elements, better explain maintenance effort than size-based metrics. HECATE operationalizes these insights, offering practitioners and researchers a more appropriate toolkit for assessing and managing the complexity of systems that integrate LLM prompts with conventional code.

title: On the Role of Directionality in Structural Generalization
summary: Several SLOG test categories explicitly involve directional distinctions (modifier position shifts, argument extraction positions), yet AM-Parser, the previous SOTA, uses an AM algebra whose operations do not encode direction. We redesign the symbolic backend around CCG directed types (deterministic CKY + single linear decoder, 30K learnable parameters). Under the same BERT-base encoder, the system achieves 75.9$\pm$6.4% LF exact match, surpassing AM-Parser (70.8$\pm$4.3%). Per SLOG's own category groupings, gains are highly directional: the CCG system outperforms AM-Parser on all 5 position-shift categories (+29.9pp), while AM-Parser outperforms on all 6 recursive-depth categories. Replacing the encoder with DeBERTa-v3-large yields 90.7$\pm$4.9%, with the largest encoder gains in recursive-depth categories, complementary to directionality's gains. Directional representations shift the bottleneck from the symbolic layer (AM-Parser's 0% category ceiling) to the neural layer, which improves with encoder upgrades.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces a new CCG-based, direction-sensitive symbolic backend for SLOG semantic parsing that significantly improves logical-form exact match over the prior AM-Parser, especially on direction/position-shift categories, while larger encoders mainly help with recursive-depth categories.
AI.motivation: Existing SLOG benchmarks include test categories that crucially rely on directionality (e.g., modifier order and argument extraction positions), but the prior state-of-the-art AM-Parser uses an algebra that is direction-agnostic, limiting its ability to capture these phenomena and leading to category-specific performance ceilings. The authors aim to build a parser whose symbolic layer explicitly encodes direction so that it can better exploit these directional distinctions and shift remaining difficulty to the neural encoder, which can then be improved independently.
AI.method: They redesign the semantic parser’s symbolic backend around Combinatory Categorial Grammar (CCG) with directed types. The parser uses a deterministic CKY parsing algorithm plus a single linear decoder, with about 30K learnable parameters in the symbolic component, while keeping the same BERT-base encoder for a controlled comparison with AM-Parser. They then swap the encoder to a larger DeBERTa-v3-large model to study how encoder capacity interacts with the new directional representations. Performance is reported as logical-form exact match on SLOG overall and per category groupings, especially focusing on position-shift and recursive-depth categories.
AI.result: With a BERT-base encoder, the new CCG-based system achieves 75.9±6.4% logical-form exact match on SLOG, outperforming AM-Parser’s 70.8±4.3%. Per-category analysis shows that the gains are concentrated in direction-sensitive position-shift categories, where the CCG parser beats AM-Parser in all 5 such categories, with an average improvement of +29.9 percentage points. Conversely, AM-Parser still does better on all 6 recursive-depth categories. When upgrading the encoder to DeBERTa-v3-large, performance rises to 90.7±4.9%, with the largest incremental gains appearing in the recursive-depth categories rather than the directional ones.
AI.conclusion: Encoding directionality in the symbolic representation substantially improves performance on direction-sensitive phenomena in SLOG, overcoming AM-Parser’s apparent ceiling on these categories and relocating the main bottleneck from the symbolic to the neural layer. Once directionality is handled by the CCG-based backend, further improvements come mainly from stronger encoders, particularly on recursive-depth phenomena, indicating that symbolic and neural advances address complementary sources of difficulty in semantic parsing.

title: ContextSniper: AntTrail's Token-Efficient Code Memory for Repository-Level Program Repair
summary: Large language model agents can repair real repository issues, but they often spend large context budgets on whole-file reads, broad searches, and long terminal outputs where useful evidence is mixed with irrelevant code and logs. This paper presents ContextSniper, AntTrail's token-efficient code memory layer for repository-level program repair. As the coding specialization of AntTrail's broader agent memory engine, ContextSniper implements the Sniper feature for precision evidence selection: it retrieves candidate code and runtime evidence, ranks it with hybrid retrieval signals, filters long outputs through an intention-aware context gate, and returns compact evidence packets while preserving recoverable source context outside the prompt. We evaluate ContextSniper on SWE-bench Lite with OpenClaw and Claude Code, using 50 task runs per host-agent condition. ContextSniper reduces total token use by 51.5% and logged cost by 36.4% for OpenClaw, and reduces total token use by 38.9% and estimated cost by 27.3% for Claude Code. Submitted-resolution rates decrease slightly, from 26.0% to 24.0% for OpenClaw and from 32.0% to 30.0% for Claude Code. ContextSniper's pilot testing scripts are open-sourced at https://github.com/Calluking/ContextSniper

AI.tldr: ContextSniper is a token-efficient context manager for LLM-based code repair agents that selectively retrieves and compresses repository and runtime evidence to cut context usage and cost with only a small drop in bug-fix success rate.
AI.motivation: LLM agents can fix real-world repository issues but are wasteful with context: they read entire files, run broad searches, and include long, noisy terminal outputs. This leads to high token usage and cost without proportionate gains in repair performance. The paper aims to make repository-level program repair more token-efficient while maintaining effectiveness.
AI.method: The authors design ContextSniper, a code-focused memory layer within the AntTrail agent framework. It implements a precision evidence selection pipeline called Sniper: (1) retrieve candidate code and runtime evidence from the repo and tools, (2) rank evidence using hybrid retrieval signals, (3) apply an intention-aware context gate that filters long outputs to keep only relevant portions, and (4) produce compact evidence packets that fit into the LLM prompt while storing the full source so it remains recoverable outside the prompt. They integrate this into program-repair agents and evaluate on the SWE-bench Lite benchmark with two host agents, OpenClaw and Claude Code, running 50 tasks per configuration.
AI.result: On SWE-bench Lite, ContextSniper substantially reduces token consumption and cost for both agents with modest impact on success rates. For OpenClaw, total tokens decrease by 51.5% and logged cost by 36.4%, while submitted-resolution (successful repair submissions) drops slightly from 26.0% to 24.0%. For Claude Code, total tokens decrease by 38.9% and estimated cost by 27.3%, with success rate decreasing from 32.0% to 30.0%.
AI.conclusion: ContextSniper demonstrates that targeted evidence selection and context gating can roughly halve token usage and significantly lower operating costs for LLM-based code repair agents, with only a small reduction in bug-fix success rates. This suggests that intelligent, intention-aware memory layers are an effective strategy for scaling repository-level program repair. The authors release pilot testing scripts to support further research and adoption.

title: World Wide Models: Literary Tools for Cultural AI
summary: LLMs stage a new form of cultural encounter that is massive, automated, and monolingual. Literary disciplines have always negotiated cultural struggles with comparative reading of literature, narratological and poetic analysis, critical theory, world literature, and translation. These tools have now become indispensable for building culturally literate AI. The essay develops a layered framework toward more nuanced textual models and pluralistic interpretations of AI, emphasizing the natural intersections of literature and AI development, connecting current debates in critical theory with structural monolingualism, and suggesting a new application of world literature approaches to address global AI textuality through macrostructure, circulation, and untranslatability.

AI.tldr: The paper argues that literary theory and world literature methods are essential for designing culturally aware, less monolingual AI language models, proposing a layered framework to analyze and shape AI’s global textual behavior.
AI.motivation: To address the problem that large language models create a new kind of cultural encounter that is large-scale, automated, and dominated by a single (often English-centric) textual standard, which risks reinforcing structural monolingualism and cultural flattening. The authors see a gap between AI development practices and the rich tools that literary studies have long used to understand and negotiate cultural difference.
AI.method: Conceptual and theoretical: the authors draw on literary studies—comparative literature, narratology, poetics, critical theory, world literature, and translation studies—to construct a layered analytical framework. This framework maps how LLMs handle text at multiple levels (e.g., micro textual features, macro textual structures, circulation patterns, and zones of untranslatability) and links current AI debates to established discussions in critical theory and world literature.
AI.result: They formulate a framework that reinterprets LLMs as cultural and literary objects, not just technical ones, and show how world literature concepts like macrostructure, circulation, and untranslatability can be applied to analyze global AI textuality. The framework highlights the structural monolingualism embedded in many LLMs and reveals where existing AI practices fall short in dealing with pluralistic, culturally specific meaning.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that building culturally literate and pluralistic AI requires the systematic integration of literary methods and world literature perspectives into AI design and evaluation. By adopting their layered framework, AI developers and humanities scholars can better identify and counteract structural monolingualism in LLMs, enabling more nuanced interpretations and more equitable global textual interactions with AI.

title: ElephantAgent: Contextual State Continuity in Agentic Systems
summary: Agentic systems enhance their capabilities by invoking external tools and maintaining persistent memory. However, these external dependencies introduce novel attack surfaces. Recent tool and memory poisoning attacks show that maliciously crafted tool descriptors and poisoned memory can covertly bias agent behavior. These threats reflect a deeper issue: the lack of verifiable continuity in the agent's contextual state for planning and execution. We present ElephantAgent, a protocol that enforces Contextual State Continuity to defend against contextual state poisoning. Inspired by prior state-continuity mechanisms (e.g., Nimble), ElephantAgent extends this protection to the evolving contextual state of agentic systems. We define the contextual state as the bounded, security-critical subset of the agent's entire context (e.g., tool state and memory). Before processing each query, ElephantAgent recomputes the digest of the local contextual state and verifies it against the latest authorized digest. Using replicated trusted hardware, ElephantAgent maintains a linearizable ledger of authorized contextual state transitions and detects out-of-band state tampering. To handle in-band semantic abuse, ElephantAgent additionally provides Historical Traceability, enabling conditional post-hoc audit and recovery to a known-good prior state.

AI.tldr: ElephantAgent is a security protocol that ensures the integrity and continuity of an AI agent’s contextual state (tools, memory, etc.) to defend against tool and memory poisoning attacks.
AI.motivation: Agentic systems rely on external tools and persistent memory, which creates new attack surfaces. Malicious tool descriptions and poisoned memory can covertly bias agents, and existing systems lack a way to verify that the context used for planning and execution has not been tampered with over time. The authors aim to provide a mechanism that guarantees the integrity and continuity of this contextual state.
AI.method: The authors introduce ElephantAgent, a protocol that focuses on a bounded, security-critical subset of an agent’s context, called the contextual state (e.g., tool state and memory). Before each query is processed, ElephantAgent recomputes a cryptographic digest of the local contextual state and checks it against the most recent authorized digest stored in a trusted, replicated ledger. This ledger, maintained with trusted hardware, records linearizable state transitions, enabling detection of any out-of-band modifications. To address in-band semantic abuse, the system further adds Historical Traceability, allowing conditional post-hoc auditing and rollback to a previously verified good state.
AI.result: ElephantAgent can detect contextual state poisoning by catching mismatches between the current contextual state digest and the authorized ledger of state transitions, thus identifying both out-of-band tampering and enabling response to in-band semantic abuse. It offers a practical way to enforce continuity and integrity over the evolving contextual state of agentic systems.
AI.conclusion: By enforcing Contextual State Continuity and providing Historical Traceability, ElephantAgent extends state-continuity protections to AI agent contexts, mitigating tool and memory poisoning threats. The protocol provides verifiable guarantees that an agent’s security-critical context has evolved only through authorized transitions and supports auditing and recovery when abuse is detected.

title: HULAT2 at MER-TRANS 2026: Governed Multi-Agent Simplification for Spanish Easy-to-Read Generation
summary: This paper describes the participation of HULAT2-UC3M in the Spanish track of MER-TRANS 2026, a shared task on multilingual Easy-to-Read translation. Three fully automatic Spanish runs were submitted. RUN1 and RUN2 used a LangGraph-based multi-agent workflow combining Gemini 2.5 Flash and RigoChat-7B-v2, parallel generation strategies, internal quality signals, Event-Condition-Action routing, controlled editing and traceable decisions. RUN1 used the base workflow, while RUN2 activated an additional lexical-support layer based on a glossary and lexical resources. RUN3 was a RigoChat-based generate-evaluate-regenerate baseline with prompt engineering and LoRA-based adaptation. The official leaderboard reports BLEU-Orig, BLEU-Gold, SARI and BERTScore. During development, additional internal signals were also inspected, including semantic fidelity, readability, lexical simplicity, syntactic clarity and factual consistency. According to official SARI, RUN1 was the best HULAT2 run, with 44.0543 points, followed by RUN2 with 43.1049 and RUN3 with 38.5136. These results indicate that, in this task setting, signal-guided multi-agent routing outperformed the linear regeneration baseline. They also show that adding lexical support did not automatically improve reference-based scores. Further segment-level and document-level analysis are required to assess readability, factual consistency and user-oriented adequacy.

AI.tldr: The paper reports the HULAT2-UC3M system for the Spanish track of MER-TRANS 2026, comparing a LangGraph-based multi-agent workflow (with and without lexical support) to a simpler generate-evaluate-regenerate baseline, showing that signal-guided multi-agent routing performs best by SARI and that extra lexical support does not necessarily improve reference-based metrics.
AI.motivation: Multilingual Easy-to-Read translation needs automatic systems that not only translate but also simplify text while preserving meaning, readability and factual consistency. The shared task offers a benchmark to test different system architectures and guiding signals for this kind of controlled simplification/translation into Spanish.
AI.method: The authors built three fully automatic Spanish systems. RUN1 and RUN2 used a LangGraph-based multi-agent workflow combining Gemini 2.5 Flash and RigoChat-7B-v2, with parallel generation, internal quality signals, Event-Condition-Action routing, controlled editing and traceable decision-making. RUN1 used the basic workflow; RUN2 added a lexical-support layer grounded in a glossary and lexical resources. RUN3 was a simpler RigoChat-centered baseline using a generate–evaluate–regenerate loop, prompt engineering, and LoRA-based adaptation. The systems were evaluated with MER-TRANS official metrics (BLEU-Orig, BLEU-Gold, SARI, BERTScore), and additional internal signals such as semantic fidelity, readability, lexical simplicity, syntactic clarity and factual consistency were monitored during development.
AI.result: According to the official SARI metric, RUN1 (multi-agent workflow without extra lexical support) scored 44.0543, making it the best among the three. RUN2 (multi-agent with lexical support) scored 43.1049, and RUN3 (baseline generate–evaluate–regenerate) scored 38.5136. Thus, the multi-agent signal-guided systems outperformed the linear baseline, and adding lexical support did not yield better reference-based metric scores.
AI.conclusion: Signal-guided multi-agent routing with internal quality signals is more effective than a linear generate–evaluate–regenerate baseline for Spanish Easy-to-Read translation in this task. However, enriching the system with lexical support does not automatically translate into higher reference-based metrics. More detailed segment- and document-level analyses are necessary to understand effects on readability, factual consistency, and user-centered adequacy beyond automatic scores.

title: A-TMA: Decoupling State-Aware Memory Failures in Long-Term Agent Memory
summary: Long term memory lets LLM agents act as persistent assistants, but user facts change. A useful memory system must know what is true now, what used to be true, and what changed. We study \emph{ghost memory}, a state coordination failure in which old, current, and transition facts coexist in the memory bank, remain mixed during retrieval, and mislead the answer model. We argue that memory systems should be understood and optimized from three levels: bank maintenance, retrieval, and answer time resolution. We propose ATMA, a state aware overlay for existing memory systems. ATMA keeps superseded and transition records in the bank, builds evidence packets for the query's requested state view, and exposes current, historical, and transition labels to QA. We further call for decoupled evaluation of bank, retrieval, and answer level failures, since final QA accuracy can hide where ghost memory occurs. To make this failure measurable, we build LTP (LoCoMo Temporal Plus), a conflict heavy benchmark for ghost memory, and evaluate on LoCoMo for long conversation generalization. On LTP, Graphiti+ATMA improves conflict accuracy by 0.240 absolute over Graphiti. On LoCoMo, Graphiti+ATMA raises temporal F1 from 0.0295 to 0.1705. The gains are host dependent, but they indicate that explicit state roles can reduce memory failures hidden by final QA accuracy.

AI.tldr: The paper identifies and tackles a key failure mode in long-term memory for LLM agents—"ghost memory," where outdated and current facts get mixed and mislead answers. It proposes ATMA, a state-aware overlay that explicitly tracks and uses temporal state (current, historical, transitioning) across memory storage, retrieval, and question answering, and introduces a benchmark (LTP) to evaluate these issues.
AI.motivation: LLM agents with long-term memory must handle changing user facts (e.g., job, address, preferences). Existing memory systems often keep conflicting records without clear temporal state, causing outdated information to pollute answers. This "ghost memory" problem is hard to diagnose because traditional end-to-end QA metrics can hide where in the pipeline (storage, retrieval, or reasoning) the failure occurs. The authors are motivated to (1) define and analyze this failure mode, (2) provide a principled, state-aware way to manage evolving facts, and (3) create benchmarks and metrics that expose these temporal memory failures.
AI.method: The authors conceptualize memory systems as having three distinct levels: (1) bank maintenance (how memories are stored and updated), (2) retrieval (which records are surfaced for a query), and (3) answer-time resolution (how the model reasons over retrieved records). They introduce ATMA, a state-aware overlay that can sit on top of existing memory systems such as Graphiti. ATMA keeps superseded (old) and transition records in the memory bank instead of deleting them, but structures them into "evidence packets" that match the temporal state requested by the query (current, historical, or transition). It labels retrieved records with explicit roles—current, historical, transition—and passes these labels into QA so that the answer model can reason with temporal and state distinctions. They also design LTP (LoCoMo Temporal Plus), a benchmark with many conflicting and changing facts specifically constructed to stress-test ghost memory, and use LoCoMo for long-conversation generalization tests.
AI.result: Using the LTP benchmark, Graphiti enhanced with ATMA shows a 0.240 absolute improvement in conflict accuracy compared to plain Graphiti, demonstrating better handling of conflicting facts. On the LoCoMo dataset, Graphiti+ATMA increases temporal F1 from 0.0295 to 0.1705, indicating much better temporal reasoning over long conversations. The authors note that performance gains depend on the underlying host memory system, but they consistently see benefits when explicit state roles are used.
AI.conclusion: The work concludes that long-term memory for LLM agents must be treated as a temporally structured problem: systems must manage what is currently true, what used to be true, and how facts change over time. The identified "ghost memory" failure mode arises when these states are conflated. ATMA, by providing state-aware overlays and explicit temporal labels at the bank, retrieval, and QA levels, reduces such failures and improves conflict and temporal accuracy. The authors argue for decoupled evaluation of each memory level, showing that relying only on final QA accuracy can mask severe state-handling issues, and they present LTP as a tool to make these failures observable and measurable.

title: Know Your Source: A Public Knowledge Store for Media Background Checks
summary: LLM-based retrieval-augmented generation (RAG) is increasingly used for automated fact-checking (AFC) and related tasks. By grounding LLM outputs in retrieved evidence, RAG-based systems provide transparent justifications while allowing external information to be updated independently of the underlying model. However, existing approaches often assume retrieved evidence is reliable, although real-world information may be conflicting, outdated, and can originate from unreliable or biased sources. Recent work on *source-critical reasoning* addresses this challenge through media background checks (MBCs) (Schlichtkrull, 2024), which assess the credibility of evidence sources to support downstream fact verification. However, generating MBCs relies on costly proprietary search APIs, limiting reproducibility. To mitigate this issue, we introduce MEDIAREF, a publicly available knowledge store of web-sourced documents that enables reproducible, low-cost evaluation of MBC generation across 200 media sources. We describe a reproducible methodology for constructing and updating the collection, assess widely used LLMs on the MBC generation task, and demonstrate that MEDIAREF supports higher-quality MBC generation through both automatic and qualitative evaluation.

AI.tldr: The paper introduces MEDIAREF, a public, reproducible web document collection to support and evaluate media background checks (MBCs) for source-critical reasoning in RAG-based automated fact-checking.
AI.motivation: RAG-based automated fact-checking systems usually assume retrieved evidence is reliable, but in practice web information can be conflicting, outdated, biased, or unreliable. Recent work on source-critical reasoning uses media background checks (MBCs) to assess source credibility, but generating MBCs currently depends on expensive proprietary search APIs that hinder reproducibility and large-scale evaluation. There is a need for an open, low-cost, and updatable resource to support MBC generation and evaluation across many media outlets.
AI.method: The authors construct MEDIAREF, a publicly available knowledge store of web-sourced documents covering 200 media sources, using a reproducible pipeline for collection and updating. They then define an evaluation setup for media background check generation using this store and test several widely used LLMs on the MBC generation task grounded in MEDIAREF. Both automatic metrics and qualitative human assessments are used to compare MBC quality with and without MEDIAREF-based grounding.
AI.result: MEDIAREF enables reproducible and low-cost experimentation on media background check generation. LLMs that use MEDIAREF as a grounding resource generate higher-quality MBCs than baselines that rely on ad-hoc or proprietary retrieval, as shown by automatic evaluation metrics and qualitative analysis of the generated background checks.
AI.conclusion: MEDIAREF is an effective, open resource for source-critical reasoning in RAG-based automated fact-checking, improving the quality of media background checks while enhancing reproducibility and lowering dependence on proprietary search APIs. The methodology for building and updating MEDIAREF also provides a template for future expansions and similar datasets, supporting more robust evaluation of source credibility in fact-checking pipelines.

title: Atomic Task Graph: A Unified Framework for Agentic Planning and Execution
summary: LLM-based agents have shown strong potential for solving complex multi-step tasks, yet existing performance improvements often rely on either scaling to larger backbone models or task-specific fine-tuning. The former incurs substantial computational costs, while the latter typically generalizes poorly across different tasks. Although prompt-based control is training-free and broadly applicable, existing methods still leave input-output dependencies between subtasks implicit in textual trajectories, making verified intermediate results difficult to reuse. To address these limitations, we propose Atomic Task Graph (ATG), a unified control framework for planning and execution. Specifically, ATG maintains an explicit graph to expose dependencies and support reuse. During planning, it recursively decomposes a high-level task into subtasks, forming a sequence of directed acyclic graphs (DAGs) whose evolution can be traced. During execution, the dependencies exposed by ATG allow independent branches to be executed in parallel, thereby improving execution efficiency. When failures are detected, ATG leverages the graph evolution history to localize the error source and repair only the affected region, preserving validated regions unchanged. Experiments show that ATG consistently outperforms strong baselines in success rate and execution efficiency across three interactive benchmarks using only 7B-8B backbones.

AI.tldr: Proposes Atomic Task Graph (ATG), a unified, training-free control framework that explicitly represents task dependencies as evolving DAGs, enabling better planning, parallel execution, and localized repair for LLM-based agents.
AI.motivation: Existing LLM agents handle complex multi-step tasks either by scaling model size or via task-specific fine-tuning, both of which are costly or poorly generalizable. Prompt-based methods avoid extra training but keep subtask dependencies implicit in text, making it hard to reuse verified intermediate results or systematically debug failures. There is a need for a general, training-free control framework that makes dependencies explicit to improve reliability, efficiency, and reusability in multi-step task solving.
AI.method: Introduce Atomic Task Graph (ATG), which maintains an explicit directed acyclic graph over atomic subtasks. In the planning phase, the system recursively decomposes a high-level task into subtasks, constructing and updating a sequence of DAGs that record how the plan evolves. In execution, ATG exploits the exposed dependency structure to run independent branches in parallel. When an error is detected, ATG uses the evolution history of the graphs to localize the fault and repair only the affected subgraph, leaving already-validated parts untouched. The framework operates via prompt-based control over 7B–8B LLM backbones, without additional training.
AI.result: Across three interactive benchmarks, ATG achieves higher task success rates and better execution efficiency compared with strong baseline LLM-agent methods, despite using relatively small 7B–8B backbone models.
AI.conclusion: Explicit, graph-based control over planning and execution—via ATG—can substantially improve the effectiveness and efficiency of LLM-based agents without scaling model size or performing task-specific fine-tuning. Making subtask dependencies explicit enables parallelism, reuse of verified intermediate results, and localized repair, leading to more robust and general multi-step task solving.

title: The Future of NLP may not be at NLP Conferences: Scholarly Migration Patterns in Natural Language Processing
summary: Natural Language Processing (NLP) has traditionally been published in its core disciplinary venues like ACL. However, advances in Large Language Models (LLMs) has led to a blurring of the disciplinary lines between NLP and general Machine Learning (ML), with authors regularly publishing in venues from both fields. Here, we ask whether the disciplinary center of gravity is shifting. Using NLP research published from 2010 to 2026 and studies of both established and new authors, we find that a migration is taking place. First, comparing the pre- and post-LLM eras, established authors lost 19.2pp of share at flagship *ACL main-conference tracks while gaining 14.8pp in the newer Findings tracks, and general ML venues rose 8.6pp, even when adjusting for parallel growth in the fields. Second, among newer authors who debut with at least three first-author NLP-topic papers, the share whose work appears mostly at *ACL venues fell from 84% (2019) to 74% (2024), while the share appearing mostly at general ML venues rose from 5% to 21%. Using causal inference techniques, we estimate that these general ML venues confer a significant citation premium, which influences venue selection. Together, these results point to a significant shift in where NLP research is published.

AI.tldr: The paper quantifies and explains a shift of NLP publications away from traditional *ACL venues toward general machine learning conferences, especially in the LLM era.
AI.motivation: NLP used to be mostly published in specialized venues like ACL, but with the rise of LLMs, the boundary between NLP and broader ML has blurred. The authors want to know whether this has materially changed where NLP research is published, particularly whether general ML venues are attracting more NLP work and why.
AI.method: The authors analyze a corpus of NLP-related publications from 2010–2026, tagging venues as *ACL main, Findings of ACL, and general ML. They compare venue shares for (1) established authors before vs. after the LLM era and (2) new authors with at least three first-author NLP-topic papers, tracking how their publication distribution changes over time. They then apply causal inference methods to estimate the citation advantage (“citation premium”) of publishing in general ML venues while adjusting for confounders like field growth.
AI.result: For established authors, share of publications in *ACL main-conference tracks drops by 19.2 percentage points in the post-LLM era, offset by increases of 14.8pp in Findings and 8.6pp in general ML venues, even after accounting for overall growth. For newer authors (with ≥3 first-author NLP-topic papers), the proportion whose work is mostly at *ACL venues falls from 84% in 2019 to 74% in 2024, while the share focused on general ML venues increases from 5% to 21%. Causal analysis suggests that publishing in general ML venues yields a significant citation premium, which in turn shapes venue choice.
AI.conclusion: NLP’s publication “center of gravity” is shifting: more NLP work, from both senior and junior authors, is moving from traditional *ACL venues to general ML conferences, with Findings absorbing some of the loss from *ACL main. A key driver appears to be a measurable citation advantage for general ML venues. Overall, the field’s institutional landscape is reorganizing in response to the LLM era and incentives around visibility and impact.

title: OntoLearner: A Modular Python Library for Ontology Learning with Large Language Models
comment: 30 pages. Under review at Nature Communications. This version is reformatted with a different section structure; content is unchanged
summary: Ontology learning (OL) aims to automatically construct structured knowledge models from text, yet progress remains fragmented across methods, domains, and evaluation practices. Despite decades of research, OL lacks a shared infrastructure for systematic evaluation and ontology access. This absence has hindered progress and fragmented research, leaving the central challenges of OL largely unaddressed. We introduce OntoLearner, a modular, cross-domain, and first-of-its-kind framework that unifies ontology access, large language model (LLM)-driven learning pipelines, and standardized benchmarking. OntoLearner releases 180 machine-readable ontologies spanning 22 domains and provides pipeline-ready datasets with train/dev/test splits for three core OL tasks: term typing, taxonomy discovery, and non-taxonomic relation extraction. Using this infrastructure, we conduct a large-scale empirical study of OL, evaluating 22 retrieval models and 12 LLMs across domains and tasks. The results converge on a finding that reframes the central challenge of OL: failure modes scale with ontological complexity rather than model size or architectural sophistication. The primary bottleneck is not model capability, but a structural mismatch between how models encode knowledge and how ontologies organize it. These findings establish that effective OL is reachable through the cross-domain, multi-task benchmarking enabled by OntoLearner. OntoLearner is open-source (MIT license) at https://github.com/sciknoworg/OntoLearner/.

AI.tldr: OntoLearner is a unified, open-source framework that provides large, cross-domain resources and benchmarks for ontology learning and shows that current bottlenecks stem from ontology complexity and structural mismatch, not from model size or architecture.
AI.motivation: Ontology learning research has been scattered across different domains, tasks, and ad-hoc evaluations, with no shared infrastructure or standardized benchmarks. This fragmentation prevents consistent comparison, reuse of ontologies, and systematic progress, leaving core challenges of automatic ontology construction unresolved. The authors aim to create a common framework and resource that unifies ontology access, learning pipelines, and evaluation so that the field can be studied and advanced more coherently.
AI.method: The authors build OntoLearner, a modular framework that: (1) releases 180 machine-readable ontologies from 22 domains; (2) prepares pipeline-ready datasets (with train/dev/test splits) for three core ontology learning tasks: term typing, taxonomy discovery, and non-taxonomic relation extraction; and (3) integrates LLM-driven ontology learning pipelines and retrieval models into a standardized benchmarking setting. Using this framework, they systematically evaluate 22 retrieval models and 12 large language models across domains and tasks to analyze performance and failure patterns.
AI.result: Experiments on OntoLearner show that performance problems in ontology learning correlate strongly with ontological complexity rather than with the size or sophistication of the models used. Across the 22 retrieval models and 12 LLMs tested, failure modes scale with how complex and structurally rich the target ontologies are, not with model capacity or architecture. This reveals systematic performance degradation as ontologies become more intricate, even for powerful models.
AI.conclusion: The study concludes that the main obstacle to effective ontology learning is a structural mismatch between how models internally encode knowledge and how ontologies formally organize it, rather than a lack of raw model capability. OntoLearner demonstrates that robust cross-domain, multi-task benchmarking is both feasible and crucial, and positions such benchmarking as the path toward resolving this structural mismatch. By providing open-source resources and a unified framework, the work lays the groundwork for more consistent, scalable progress in ontology learning.

title: Language Models as Measurement Apparatus for Culture
comment: Accepted to the Big Picture workshop co-located with ACL 2026. This version expands the camera-ready (adding Fig. 3 and section 6.3, as well as correcting minor typos) in Proceedings of The Big Picture v2: Crafting a Research Narrative, pp. 131--143, San Diego, CA, USA. Association for Computational Linguistics
summary: Language models are increasingly used to quantify cultural phenomena, but what makes such measurement distinctively cultural? This paper argues that NLP work on culture is a material-discursive practice: the apparatus -- model, data, annotation, evaluation -- participates in constituting the cultural reality it measures, rather than passively recording it. Drawing on Karen Barad's concept of the agential cut -- the contingent boundary between phenomenon and instrument -- I show that the apparatus's substantive design choices draw such boundaries, and that the boundary is entangled from the start because language models have already internalized much of the cultural material they measure. I illustrate this through three case studies on television and film dialogue (measuring structure, interaction, and deviation) and three examinations of the apparatus itself (erasure of cultural markers, attunement to historical material, and agency in an agentic workflow). This big picture analysis proposes a research program that is theory-driven, empirically rigorous, and culturally contingent, treating each agential cut as a conscious commitment, at once methodological and ethical.

AI.tldr: The paper critiques and reframes how NLP uses language models to measure “culture,” arguing that such measurements actively shape, not just observe, cultural reality.
AI.motivation: As language models are increasingly used to study and quantify cultural phenomena (e.g., bias, stereotypes, norms), there is little clarity about what makes these measurements genuinely cultural and how the tools themselves participate in producing the results. The paper aims to clarify the conceptual foundations of “culture measurement” in NLP and reveal its methodological and ethical stakes.
AI.method: The author adopts a theoretical and reflexive methodology grounded in Karen Barad’s concept of the agential cut from agential realism. They analyze how model, data, annotation, and evaluation choices constitute an apparatus that co-produces the cultural phenomena it measures. This is operationalized via three empirical case studies using television and film dialogue (examining structure, interaction, and deviation) and three meta-level analyses of the apparatus (studying erasure of cultural markers, alignment with historical material, and the role of model agency in agentic workflows).
AI.result: The analysis shows that (1) design decisions in NLP pipelines effectively draw agential cuts that define what counts as “culture,” (2) language models already embed cultural material, entangling instrument and object from the outset, and (3) concrete case studies and apparatus examinations illustrate how measurements reconfigure rather than neutrally reflect cultural realities. This exposes how standard NLP practices can erase, reframe, or re-inscribe cultural markers and dynamics.
AI.conclusion: NLP work on culture should be understood as a material-discursive practice where models and pipelines co-constitute the cultural phenomena they “measure.” Researchers should treat each agential cut—choices of models, data, labels, and evaluation—as an explicit, theory-laden, and ethically charged commitment. The paper proposes a research program that is theory-driven, empirically rigorous, and sensitive to cultural contingency, reframing cultural measurement in NLP as both a methodological and ethical endeavor.

title: Multimodal Knowledge Edit-Scoped Generalization for Online Recursive MLLM Editing
summary: Online multimodal knowledge editing requires injecting a continual stream of visual-textual corrections into multimodal large language models (MLLMs) with bounded overhead and minimal disruption to unrelated behaviors. Existing editors mainly emphasize edit reliability and long-horizon stability, but rarely control the semantic boundary of each edit. Our pilot analyses of post-edit behaviors and internal neuronal activities reveal a scope gap behind reliable edits: instance-level success neither guarantees transfer to valid cross-modal variants nor prevents leakage to unrelated inputs, while edit-related cross-modal responses concentrate in deeper semantic layers. Therefore, we formulate Edit-Scoped Generalization, reframing online MLLM editing from merely correcting an instance to controlling the propagation boundary of each edit. To this end, we propose ScopeEdit, a scope-aware online editor that decomposes each update into a modality-local absorption branch and an evidence-gated shared generalization branch. The local branch supports stable edit absorption, whereas the shared branch enables cross-modal propagation only when visual and textual evidence are sufficiently aligned. Both branches perform scope-separated write geometries in orthogonal low-rank spaces and maintain branch-wise preconditioners via Sherman--Morrison recursions, yielding constant per-edit overhead. Extensive experiments across diverse benchmarks, long-horizon edit streams, MLLM backbones, real-world VLKEB scenarios, and complex vision-language architectures show that ScopeEdit consistently improves the trade-off between in-scope cross-modal transfer and out-of-scope locality, while preserving edit reliability, stability and online efficiency. Our code is available at https://github.com/lab-klc/ScopeEdit.

AI.tldr: ScopeEdit is an online, scope-aware editing method for multimodal LLMs that constrains how each visual-textual edit generalizes across modalities, improving in-scope transfer while limiting unwanted behavior changes with constant per-edit cost.
AI.motivation: Existing online multimodal knowledge editing methods focus on making each edit reliable and stable over time, but they poorly control how far an edit semantically spreads. An edit that fixes a specific visual-textual instance may not transfer to legitimate cross-modal variants, or it may leak and alter model behavior on unrelated inputs. Analyses of behavior and neuron activations show that these cross-modal edit effects concentrate in deeper semantic layers and that there is a clear mismatch between desired and actual edit scope. This motivates a new formulation that explicitly manages the semantic boundary of each edit, especially under continual streaming edits.
AI.method: The paper introduces Edit-Scoped Generalization, which redefines the editing objective as not only correcting an instance but also constraining the propagation boundary of each edit. To implement this, the authors propose ScopeEdit, an online editor that splits each parameter update into two coordinated branches: (1) a modality-local absorption branch that updates modality-specific representations to stably absorb the edit; and (2) an evidence-gated shared generalization branch that operates on shared, cross-modal representations but only activates when visual and textual evidence are sufficiently aligned. Both branches apply low-rank, orthogonal update geometries and maintain separate preconditioners using Sherman–Morrison recursions, which ensures constant per-edit computational and memory overhead while enforcing scope separation between local and shared effects.
AI.result: Across multiple benchmarks, long editing sequences, diverse multimodal LLM backbones, real-world vision-language knowledge editing benchmarks, and complex vision-language architectures, ScopeEdit outperforms baselines on the trade-off between in-scope cross-modal generalization and out-of-scope locality. That is, it better transfers edits to semantically appropriate visual-textual variants while more effectively preventing unintended behavior changes on unrelated inputs, all while maintaining comparable edit reliability, long-horizon stability, and online efficiency. Empirical analyses validate that ScopeEdit successfully shapes where and how edits propagate inside the model.
AI.conclusion: The paper concludes that effective online editing of multimodal LLMs must go beyond reliability and stability to explicitly manage the semantic scope of each edit. By decomposing edits into modality-local and evidence-gated shared branches with orthogonal low-rank update spaces and efficient preconditioning, ScopeEdit offers a principled mechanism for scoped generalization with constant per-edit overhead. This framework consistently yields better in-scope cross-modal transfer and stronger locality, without sacrificing reliability or efficiency, suggesting a practical path for safer, more controllable continual knowledge editing in complex multimodal systems.

title: Will Scaling Improve Social Simulation with LLMs?
summary: Large Language Model (LLM) social simulations are a promising research method, but they are not yet faithful enough to be adopted widely. In this work, we investigate whether the current scaling paradigm in language modeling is likely to close these gaps, or whether simulation fidelity is orthogonal to general capabilities and therefore deserving of more research attention. We use scaling laws to study the relationship between LLMs' compute scale, general capability benchmarks, and the fidelity of social simulation in three representative sub-domains: opinion modeling, behavioral simulation, and longitudinal forecasting. Surprisingly, we discover strong compute scaling in all three settings, using a suite of 85 transformer LLMs with the Qwen3 architecture pre-trained on the DCLM web text corpus under fixed-compute budgets from $10^{18}$ to $10^{20}$ FLOPs. Then we evaluate 35 larger and more capable open-weight models up to 70B parameters, allowing us to predict downstream accuracy from loss. This reveals that the majority of behavioral and opinion simulation tasks will rapidly improve with scale, particularly when they involve populations that are well-represented in English web corpora.
